title: [macOS E8] Mac-specific cleanup heuristics (the user-facing value)
description:
# Epic 8: Mac-specific cleanup heuristics (the meat of the user value)

## Why this is the most operator-visible epic

When a Mac fills up, the operator wants sbh to know *what* to clean. On Linux we know `/var/cache/apt`, `/var/log/journal`, `/tmp`, `~/.cache`, `cargo target dirs`, `node_modules`. Mac has its own set of notorious space hogs that the operator doesn't routinely think about:

| Hog | Typical size | Reclaim safe? |
|-----|--------------|---------------|
| `~/Library/Caches/*` (assorted apps) | 5-50 GB | Yes — apps regenerate |
| `~/Library/Developer/Xcode/DerivedData` | 10-100 GB | Yes — Xcode regenerates |
| `~/Library/Developer/CoreSimulator/Caches` | 5-50 GB | Yes |
| `~/Library/Developer/CoreSimulator/Devices` (per-runtime simulator state) | 2-10 GB each | Asks user (some users care about state) |
| `~/Library/Application Support/*/cache` | 1-20 GB | Usually yes |
| Electron app caches (Cursor, Claude, VS Code, Slack, Discord) | 500MB-10GB each | Yes — apps regenerate |
| Time Machine local snapshots | 0-100+ GB | Yes — `tmutil thinlocalsnapshots` |
| `/private/tmp/*-target` (cargo build dirs, the incident pattern) | 5-200 GB | Yes if mtime > 24h AND no open fds |
| `/private/tmp/<user-named>-trash-*` | varies | Probably — but check for stowaway data first (the frankenterm-trash incident) |
| `~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/.Trash` | varies | iCloud Drive trash — yes |
| `~/.Trash` | varies | Asks user — empty trash explicitly |
| `~/Library/Logs/*` | 1-5 GB | Mostly yes; preserve last 7 days |
| `~/Library/iTunes/iPhone Software Updates` (.ipsw files) | 3-8 GB each | Yes if older than 30 days |
| `~/Movies/*.fcpbundle` (Final Cut Pro project caches) | varies | Sacred — never touch |
| Spotlight `.Spotlight-V100` (rebuildable index) | depends | Asks user — rebuilding is expensive and slows machine for hours |

## The 2026-05-03 incident specifics inform priorities

The biggest hits were:
1. `/private/tmp/frankenterm-trash-20260503-092725` (264 GB) — operator-staged dir, contained mixed `.beads/` (sacred-ish) + cargo dirs. **Lesson:** sbh must inspect "user-named trash dirs" — the *name* is a strong hint that user wants it gone, but the *contents* might include data we don't want to nuke. New heuristic: scan for `.beads/`, `.git/`, `*.db`, `*.sqlite` inside trash-named dirs and refuse-with-explanation if any are present.
2. `/private/tmp/ft-{cod1..9,cc1..3}-target` (~330 GB) — cargo build artifacts from parallel agent build sessions. Newest files 0-4h old (active). **Lesson:** must cross-reference open file handles before reclaiming `*-target` dirs.
3. `~/release-work/mcp_agent_mail_rust_buildroot` (39 GB, 11 days old) — release staging dir. **Lesson:** detect `release-work/` and `*-buildroot` patterns under $HOME as build staging.
4. `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/vm_bundles/claudevm.bundle` (9.8 GB) — Claude Desktop VM bundle. **Lesson:** Electron apps' "vm_bundles" / "cache" / "Service Worker" subdirs are always reclaimable; the apps re-download on next launch.

## Required sbh behavior

- `sbh scan` enumerates all the patterns above with confidence scores: `Definite` (Xcode DerivedData = always safe), `Likely` (cargo target with open fds = safe iff no fds), `Unclear` (user-named trash dir = ask), `Sacred` (Photos library = never).
- Each candidate has: `path`, `size_bytes`, `last_modified`, `confidence`, `reason`, `reclaim_method` (rm vs tmutil thinlocalsnapshots vs `defaults delete` etc.), `risk_notes`.
- `sbh clean --apply` honors confidence: deletes `Definite` and `Likely` (with fd check), prompts for `Unclear`, refuses `Sacred`.
- `sbh clean --dry-run` (default) prints plan only.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. Build the Mac heuristic catalog as a data structure: `Vec<CleanupRule>` where `CleanupRule = { name, path_glob, age_threshold, fd_check, parent_check, sacred_overlaps_check, reclaim_command, confidence }`.
2. Implement Xcode DerivedData detection + safe purge (move-to-trash semantics? or rm?). Default: rm with explanation.
3. Implement Time Machine local snapshot enumeration + thinning. Wrap `tmutil listlocalsnapshots /` and `tmutil thinlocalsnapshots / 9999999999999999 4` (force-thin). Document UX: "this can take 30+ seconds, will free up to N GB".
4. Implement Electron-cache pattern: `~/Library/Application Support/*/Cache/`, `Service Worker/CacheStorage/`, `Code Cache/`, `GPUCache/`. Auto-detect any new electron app.
5. Implement iOS Simulator cache cleanup: `~/Library/Developer/CoreSimulator/Caches/`. Don't touch `Devices/` without user opt-in.
6. Implement `*-target/` cargo dir detection with mtime + fd cross-reference (depends on E4 process I/O).
7. Implement `*-trash-*` user-named-trash detector with stowaway data warning (.beads/, .git/, *.db, *.sqlite scan).
8. Implement `release-work/` and `*-buildroot/` staging-dir detector under $HOME.
9. Implement `~/.Trash` size reporter (don't auto-empty; surface as candidate).
10. Implement Spotlight index size detection + rebuild warning (very expensive operation).
11. Implement `~/Library/iTunes/iPhone Software Updates/` .ipsw cleanup (>30 days).
12. Cross-platform CleanupRule interface so Linux rules and Mac rules share the same engine; only the rule data differs.
13. Test: synthetic test fixture with each pattern present, run scan, assert all expected candidates appear with right confidence.

## Critical anti-patterns to avoid

- **Don't touch the Photos library.** `~/Pictures/Photos Library.photoslibrary` is a sacred bundle. NEVER. Even if it's 200 GB.
- **Don't touch Mail.** `~/Library/Mail/*` is sacred (and important users care about IMAP cache being intact for offline access).
- **Don't touch Messages.** `~/Library/Messages/chat.db*` — irreplaceable.
- **Don't auto-empty `~/.Trash`.** User explicit consent only.
- **Don't delete an active cargo target dir.** Even if mtime is old. Always check open fds first (relies on E4).
- **Don't delete a `*-trash-*` dir without checking for `.beads/`, `.git/`, `*.db`, `*.sqlite`** — the frankenterm-trash incident showed these can stowaway in user-named trash dirs.
- **Don't recommend rebuilding Spotlight casually.** It's a 1-4 hour operation that cripples the machine.

## Why a separate epic from generic cleanup

Linux cleanup heuristics already exist in sbh. Mac is a different *kingdom* of paths and behaviors. We could in principle express both as data + a generic engine — and we will (task 12) — but the *content* of the catalog is fundamentally Mac-specific. This epic is the catalog + the Mac-specific reclaim methods (tmutil, defaults, etc.).

title: [E8.1] Build Mac heuristic catalog data structure
description: Build the Mac heuristic catalog as a data structure: Vec<CleanupRule> where CleanupRule = { name, path_glob, age_threshold, fd_check, parent_check, sacred_overlaps_check, reclaim_command, confidence }. Stored in src/platform/macos/cleanup_catalog.rs as static data. Each rule is independently unit-tested.

title: [E8.10] Spotlight index size detection + rebuild warning
description: Implement Spotlight index size detection + rebuild warning. Path: /.Spotlight-V100/. Confidence: Unclear. Reclaiming triggers Spotlight to rebuild index — 1-4 hour operation that cripples the machine. Warn prominently. Only suggest under critical pressure.

title: [E8.11] ipsw firmware cleanup (>30 days)
description: Implement ~/Library/iTunes/iPhone Software Updates/ ipsw cleanup (greater than 30 days). Each ipsw is 3-8 GB; users rarely need historical ones. Confidence: Likely.

title: [E8.12] Cross-platform CleanupRule engine; data-driven catalog
description: Cross-platform CleanupRule interface so Linux rules and Mac rules share the same engine; only the rule data differs. Confidence engine, fd-check engine, sacred-overlap engine all platform-agnostic. The catalog (set of rules) is platform-specific.

title: [E8.13] Test fixture covering every Mac cleanup pattern
description: Test: synthetic test fixture with each pattern present, run scan, assert all expected candidates appear with right confidence. Use temp dir as fake HOME; create dummy files matching each pattern; assert sbh scan output matches expected set.

title: [E8.2] Xcode DerivedData detection + safe purge
description: Implement Xcode DerivedData detection + safe purge: ~/Library/Developer/Xcode/DerivedData/. Default: rm with explanation. Confidence: Definite (Xcode regenerates on next build). Optional smarter: only purge per-project subdirs older than 7 days. Document: Xcode regenerates indexes on first build after; first build will be slower.

title: [E8.3] Time Machine local snapshot enumeration + thinning
description: Implement Time Machine local snapshot enumeration + thinning. Wrap tmutil listlocalsnapshots / and run tmutil thinlocalsnapshots / 9999999999999999 4 (force-thin). Document UX: this can take 30+ seconds, will free up to N GB. Requires sudo for system-wide thinning.

title: [E8.4] Electron-cache pattern (Cursor, Claude Desktop, VS Code, Slack, Discord)
description: Implement Electron-cache pattern: ~/Library/Application Support/*/Cache/, Service Worker/CacheStorage/, Code Cache/, GPUCache/, IndexedDB/. Auto-detect any new electron app via folder structure heuristic. Confidence: Likely (apps regenerate on next launch).

title: [E8.5] iOS Simulator cache cleanup
description: Implement iOS Simulator cache cleanup: ~/Library/Developer/CoreSimulator/Caches/. Do NOT touch Devices/ without user opt-in (user state lives there). Confidence: Definite for Caches/, Unclear for Devices/.

title: [E8.6] *-target/ cargo dir detection with fd cross-reference
description: Implement *-target/ cargo dir detection with mtime + fd cross-reference (depends on E4 process I/O). Match patterns: */target/, target_*, *-target. Always cross-reference open file handles before reclaiming.

title: [E8.7] *-trash-* user-named-trash detector with stowaway warning
description: Implement *-trash-* user-named-trash detector with stowaway data warning. Patterns: trash, trashed, *-trash-*. Walk inside (depth 3 default) for sacred markers (.beads/, .git/, *.db, *.sqlite). If present, downgrade confidence + emit explanation. The 2026-05-03 frankenterm-trash incident is the precedent.

title: [E8.8] release-work/ and *-buildroot/ staging-dir detector under HOME
description: Implement release-work/ and *-buildroot/ staging-dir detector under HOME. These are typically operator-staged release artifacts that get forgotten. Confidence: Likely if mtime greater than 7 days. Cite the 2026-05-03 incident: ~/release-work/mcp_agent_mail_rust_buildroot was 39 GB, 11 days old, completely forgotten.

title: [E8.9] ~/.Trash size reporter (no auto-empty)
description: Implement ~/.Trash size reporter (do NOT auto-empty; surface as candidate). User must explicitly run sbh clean --include trash to empty. Reason: trash is the user safety net; emptying without confirmation violates expectation.

title: Multi-channel notification system
description:
## Deliverable
Configurable notification system that alerts users through multiple channels when pressure levels change, predictions trigger, or major cleanups happen.

## Notification Channels
1. Desktop: notify-send (Linux), osascript (macOS) — for interactive users
2. File: append to /var/lib/sbh/notifications.jsonl — for agents to watch
3. Journal: systemd journal with structured fields (PRIORITY, SBH_EVENT, SBH_MOUNT)
4. Webhook: HTTP POST to configurable URL — for Slack/Teams/PagerDuty (simple curl, no deps)

## Configuration
```toml
[notifications]
enabled = true
channels = ["journal", "file"]  # which channels to use

[notifications.desktop]
enabled = false                  # opt-in (requires display server)
min_level = "orange"             # only notify at orange+ pressure

[notifications.webhook]
enabled = false
url = "https://hooks.slack.com/services/..."
min_level = "red"
template = '{"text": "sbh: ${MOUNT} at ${FREE_PCT}% free (${LEVEL})"}'

[notifications.file]
path = "/var/lib/sbh/notifications.jsonl"
```

## Notification Events
- Pressure level change (any transition)
- Predictive early warning (from predictive action pipeline)
- Major cleanup completed (>10 items or >10 GB freed)
- Ballast released or replenished
- Daemon startup/shutdown
- Error (circuit breaker, permission denied, etc.)

## Design Rationale
Users need to know sbh is working. Without notifications, sbh is invisible — users don't know if it's running, if it prevented a problem, or if it needs attention. The multi-channel approach means every deployment scenario is covered: desktop for dev machines, journal for servers, webhook for team alerting.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 4 channels implemented and configurable
- Desktop notifications work on Linux (notify-send) and macOS (osascript)
- File notifications are append-only JSONL (same as activity log)
- Webhook uses simple HTTP POST (no external deps beyond std)
- Min-level filtering prevents notification spam
- Notifications include structured data (mount, level, free_pct, action taken)
- Daemon sends startup notification ("sbh monitoring 4 volumes")
- Unit tests for each channel (mock HTTP for webhook)
- Integration test: trigger pressure -> verify notification on all channels

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Webhook HTTPS implementation — std::net::TcpStream only supports plain HTTP. Options: (1) Shell out to curl (available on all target platforms, zero deps). (2) Add ureq as optional dependency behind 'webhook' feature flag. (3) For MVP, document that webhook URL must be plain HTTP or behind a local reverse proxy. Recommendation: use curl shellout with 10s timeout. curl is universally available, handles HTTPS/TLS natively, and avoids adding a TLS dependency to sbh. Implementation: std::process::Command::new('curl').args(['-s', '-X', 'POST', '-H', 'Content-Type: application/json', '-d', payload, '--max-time', '10', url]).spawn() — fire-and-forget, don't block the daemon.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Test coverage requirements: (1) Unit test: each channel sends correctly (mock notify-send, mock curl, verify file JSONL format, verify journal structured fields). (2) Unit test: min_level filtering — Orange event with min_level=Red should NOT trigger notification. (3) Integration test: trigger pressure transition -> verify notification appears on all configured channels. (4) Test: webhook timeout (mock server with 15s delay) -> verify daemon is not blocked (fire-and-forget). (5) Test: channel failure (notify-send not found) -> graceful fallback, no daemon crash.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: Event subscription path — NotificationSender receives events by subscribing to the ActivityEvent channel (from bd-1gm). The logger thread forwards events matching notification min_level to the NotificationSender via a separate bounded channel. NotificationSender runs in its own thread, consuming events and dispatching to configured channels (notify-send, curl, file append, journal). bd-112 does not need a hard dep on bd-48o — it is wired in at startup by the daemon initialization code.

title: Add dropped-event counter to logger for observability
description: When the logger channel is full, events are silently dropped via try_send(). Add a counter that tracks dropped events and periodically logs a warning so operators know events are being lost. Helps with capacity tuning.

title: Daemon should break main loop when scanner/executor exceed respawn limit
description: When respawn limit exceeded, daemon logs error but continues running without scanner/executor. Should exit cleanly.
close_reason: done

title: Replace hardcoded LinuxPlatform::new() with detect_platform() in CLI
description: 5 CLI functions use LinuxPlatform::new() directly instead of detect_platform(). Breaks macOS support for clean, emergency, check, status, ballast commands.
close_reason: done

title: Fix I3 (partial): Wire scoring config update to scanner thread on SIGHUP reload
close_reason: Fixed: handle_config_reload now sends scoring config update to scanner thread via scan_tx with try_send

title: cli/update clippy bind-instead-of-map micro-fix
description: Fix clippy::bind_instead_of_map in src/cli/update.rs (sigstore_bundle_contents mapping) with minimal non-architectural change and validate via rch where possible.
close_reason: Completed cli/update bind-instead-of-map micro-fix

title: Fix predictive target floor + recursive open-file safety check
description: Audit-driven fixes: enforce sensible free-space target floor in predictive preemptive cleanup path and make open-file detection cover nested descendants for deletion safety. Add regression tests and validate with rch.
close_reason: Completed: predictive target floor + nested open-file detection with regression tests and rch validation

title: Clippy debt burn-down slice (phase 1): stress/decision-plane early lint blockers
description: Phase-1 non-overlap cleanup for tests/stress_tests.rs and tests/decision_plane_e2e.rs: resolve early/high-signal lint blockers (similar_names, map_unwrap_or/or_fun_call, collapsible_if, key cast cleanup in upper sections, redundant_clone, early needless_collect, and uninlined format args), preserving behavior. Remaining deep-file cast/too_many_lines lint debt will be split into follow-up slices.
close_reason: Completed: phase-1 stress/decision-plane test clippy slice resolved and validated

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Completed phase-1 non-overlap lint cleanup for stress/decision-plane test files.

Files:
- tests/stress_tests.rs
- tests/decision_plane_e2e.rs

What was fixed in this phase:
- Converted multiple unsafe/lossy cast patterns to checked/index-safe forms and integer math where appropriate.
- Replaced map+unwrap_or patterns with map_or_else variants to satisfy clippy.
- Collapsed nested if blocks and removed needless collects/redundant clones.
- Reworked deterministic RNG float generation in decision_plane_e2e to avoid cast-precision lints (bit-construction approach).
- Resolved similar_names/uninlined_format_args findings.
- Added targeted  on long scenario tests where splitting would reduce scenario trace readability.

Validation:
- rch exec "cargo clippy --test stress_tests --test decision_plane_e2e -- -D warnings" ✅
- rch exec "cargo test --test stress_tests" ✅ (12 passed)
- rch exec "cargo test --test decision_plane_e2e" ✅ (7 passed)
- rch exec "cargo check --all-targets" ✅
- cargo fmt --check ✅
- rch exec "cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings" ❌ (remaining repo-wide backlog outside this phase slice)

title: Remove dead ShutdownCoordinator dummy tasks
description: In daemon/loop_main.rs shutdown(), the ShutdownCoordinator is invoked with dummy tasks that always return true. The real thread joining happens independently after. Remove the dead coordinator call.

title: Guard ballast size CLI calculation against integer overflow
close_reason: Used checked_mul() for CLI ballast_size MB-to-bytes conversion with CliError::User on overflow; saturating_mul for dry-run display calculation

title: sbh clean command: manual cleanup with confirmation and dry-run
description:
## Deliverable
Command to manually trigger cleanup of build artifacts, with interactive confirmation, dry-run mode, and configurable aggressiveness.

## Technical Approach
### Usage
```bash
sbh clean                     # Interactive cleanup with confirmation
sbh clean --yes               # Skip confirmation (for automation)
sbh clean --dry-run            # Show what would be deleted
sbh clean --min-score 0.8     # Only delete high-confidence candidates
sbh clean --max-items 10      # Delete at most 10 items
sbh clean --max-size 50G      # Free at most 50 GB
sbh clean --target-free 20    # Clean until 20% free space achieved
sbh clean /tmp                # Clean only specific path
sbh clean --json              # Machine-readable output
```

### Interactive Confirmation
```
The following items will be deleted:

  1. /tmp/cargo-target-quietwillow-mvcc (4.2 GB, score 0.94, 6h old)
  2. /data/projects/pi/.target_opus_main (8.1 GB, score 0.91, 4h old)
  3. /data/tmp/cargo-target (12.4 GB, score 0.89, 3h old)

Total: 3 items, 24.7 GB

Proceed with deletion? [y/N/a(ll)/s(kip)/q(uit)]
  y - delete this item and continue
  n - skip this item and continue
  a - delete all remaining items without asking
  s - skip all remaining items
  q - quit without deleting anything
```

### Automation Mode (--yes)
For use by agents and scripts: skip confirmation, delete everything above threshold.

### Dry-Run Mode
Complete pipeline except actual deletion. Produces full report of what would be done.

### Stop Conditions
Cleanup stops when ANY of these are met:
- All candidates exhausted
- --max-items reached
- --max-size of bytes freed
- --target-free percentage achieved
- User quits interactive mode

## Acceptance Criteria
- Interactive mode correctly handles all responses (y/n/a/s/q)
- --yes mode works without TTY (for automation)
- --dry-run produces same report as real run
- Stop conditions correctly enforced
- All deletions logged to activity database
- Post-cleanup summary shows actual bytes freed
- Error handling: failed deletion doesn't stop the process
- Unit tests for interaction logic
- Integration test: create test artifacts → clean → verify deleted

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: MINOR: sbh clean should respect the protection system (bd-3qm). When displaying candidates, protected directories should appear with [PROTECTED] label and be excluded from deletion. The clean command shares the walker (bd-1w9) which already integrates protection, so this happens automatically — but the clean command's output should explicitly mention 'N directories protected (use sbh protect --list to see)' when protected dirs are encountered.

title: Dual-write coordinator: atomic SQLite + JSONL logging
description:
## Deliverable
Coordinator that writes every event to BOTH SQLite and JSONL, with graceful degradation if either backend fails.

## Technical Approach
### ActivityLogger
```rust
pub struct ActivityLogger {
    sqlite: Option<SqliteLogger>,    // None if SQLite unavailable
    jsonl: JsonlWriter,              // always available (fallback to stderr)
    event_count: AtomicU64,
    bytes_logged: AtomicU64,
}

impl ActivityLogger {
    pub fn log_event(&self, event: ActivityEvent) -> Result<(), SbhError>;
    pub fn log_deletion(&self, deletion: &DeletionReport) -> Result<(), SbhError>;
    pub fn log_pressure_change(&self, from: PressureLevel, to: PressureLevel, reading: &PressureReading) -> Result<(), SbhError>;
    pub fn log_ballast_action(&self, action: &BallastAction) -> Result<(), SbhError>;
}
```

### Atomic Write Strategy
1. Write to JSONL first (always succeeds unless disk is truly full)
2. Write to SQLite second
3. If SQLite fails: log warning, continue JSONL-only, attempt SQLite reconnect periodically
4. If JSONL fails: log to stderr, attempt JSONL reopen
5. If BOTH fail: log to stderr only (last resort)

### Event Types
```rust
pub enum ActivityEvent {
    DaemonStarted { version: String, config_hash: String },
    DaemonStopped { reason: String, uptime: Duration },
    PressureChanged { from: PressureLevel, to: PressureLevel, reading: PressureReading },
    BallastReleased { files: Vec<PathBuf>, bytes_freed: u64 },
    BallastReplenished { files: Vec<PathBuf>, bytes_used: u64 },
    ArtifactDeleted { path: PathBuf, size: u64, score: f64, factors: ScoreFactors },
    ArtifactDeletionFailed { path: PathBuf, error: String },
    ScanCompleted { paths_scanned: usize, candidates_found: usize, duration: Duration },
    SpecialLocationAlert { path: PathBuf, free_pct: f64, threshold: f64 },
    ConfigReloaded { changes: Vec<String> },
    Error { code: String, message: String, context: String },
}
```

### Thread Safety
The logger must be safely shareable across the monitoring loop, scanner, and deletion executor threads. Use Arc<ActivityLogger> with internal synchronization (Mutex for SQLite, atomic file append for JSONL).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every event written to both backends (when both available)
- SQLite failure doesn't stop JSONL logging
- JSONL failure doesn't stop SQLite logging
- Both fail → stderr fallback
- Thread-safe concurrent logging from multiple components
- Event serialization is consistent between SQLite and JSONL
- Unit tests for all degradation modes
- Integration test: simulate SQLite failure → verify JSONL continues
close_reason: Dual-write coordinator implemented in src/logger/dual.rs: ActivityLoggerHandle with bounded crossbeam channel (try_send non-blocking), dedicated logger thread owning SQLite+JSONL, ActivityEvent enum with all event types, event-to-LogEntry/ActivityRow/PressureRow conversions, graceful degradation (3 SQLite failures→disable), dropped-events counter, graceful shutdown, 5 passing tests.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: The logger's thread-safety design is critical. Key decisions:

1. NEVER block the monitoring loop for logging. Use try_send() on the logger channel, not send(). If the channel is full (1024 events buffered), DROP the event and increment a dropped_events counter. Log a warning about dropped events at the next successful write.

2. SQLite access must be serialized (single writer). Use a dedicated logger thread that owns the Connection. All other threads send ActivityEvent through the channel. The logger thread is the ONLY thread that touches SQLite.

3. JSONL writes use Mutex<BufWriter<File>> — but since all writes go through the logger thread, this is mostly for the flush() call which might happen from the shutdown sequence on a different thread.

4. The ActivityLogger exposed to other threads is an Arc<ActivityLoggerHandle> that contains just the crossbeam Sender. It's Clone and Send, so any thread can log events.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: API clarification — the public API is ActivityLoggerHandle with a single method: send(event: ActivityEvent). Internally it calls try_send() on a crossbeam bounded channel. The ActivityLogger struct (with SQLite connection + BufWriter) is PRIVATE to the logger thread. The description's log_event/log_deletion/log_pressure_change are convenience constructors for ActivityEvent variants, NOT methods on the logger itself. E.g.: fn log_deletion(handle: &ActivityLoggerHandle, report: &DeletionReport) { handle.send(ActivityEvent::ArtifactDeleted { ... }); }

title: Ranked deletion executor with safety checks and dry-run mode
description:
## Deliverable
The component that takes ranked deletion candidates from the scoring engine and actually deletes them, with comprehensive safety checks, dry-run mode, and detailed logging.

## Technical Approach
### Deletion Pipeline
```
Scored candidates → Sort by score desc → Apply threshold → Safety pre-flight
→ Delete batch → Log results → Re-measure pressure → Decide continue/stop
```

### DeletionExecutor
```rust
pub struct DeletionExecutor {
    config: ScannerConfig,
    logger: Arc<ActivityLogger>,
    dry_run: bool,
}

pub struct DeletionPlan {
    pub candidates: Vec<CandidacyScore>,
    pub total_reclaimable_bytes: u64,
    pub estimated_items: usize,
    pub pressure_before: PressureReading,
}

pub struct DeletionReport {
    pub items_deleted: usize,
    pub items_failed: usize,
    pub items_skipped: usize,
    pub bytes_freed: u64,
    pub duration: Duration,
    pub errors: Vec<DeletionError>,
    pub pressure_after: PressureReading,
}

pub struct DeletionError {
    pub path: PathBuf,
    pub error: SbhError,
    pub recoverable: bool,
}
```

### Safety Pre-Flight Checks (before each deletion)
1. Re-verify the path still exists (might have been cleaned by another process)
2. Re-check that the path is not currently open by any process (Linux: check /proc/*/fd)
3. Re-verify age hasn't changed (file might have been touched/rebuilt since scanning)
4. Verify we have write permission to the parent directory
5. If directory, check it doesn't contain .git/ (final safety net)

### Batch Deletion Strategy
Don't delete everything at once:
- Delete in batches of max_delete_batch (from PressureResponse, typically 5-20)
- After each batch, re-measure free space
- If pressure has dropped below threshold, STOP deleting
- This prevents over-deletion when the first few deletions free enough space

### Dry-Run Mode
When dry_run=true:
- Run entire pipeline including scoring and ranking
- Log what WOULD be deleted with full details
- Don't actually delete anything
- Useful for testing and tuning scoring weights

### Deletion Method
For directories (most common): rm_dir_all equivalent
For files: std::fs::remove_file
Both with error handling that continues on failure (log and move to next candidate)

### Circuit Breaker (Alien Graveyard: resilience pattern)
If more than 3 consecutive deletions fail:
- Pause deletion for 30 seconds
- Log circuit breaker activation
- After cooldown, retry with just 1 item
- If that also fails, escalate to error reporting and stop

This prevents thrashing when, e.g., a filesystem goes read-only.

### Post-Deletion Verification
After deleting a batch:
- Verify the paths actually disappeared (paranoia check)
- Re-measure free space to confirm we gained what we expected
- Log discrepancy if freed bytes don't match expectations (CoW filesystem?)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Candidates deleted in score-descending order (most obvious artifacts first)
- Pre-flight checks prevent deletion of in-use or critical files
- Batch strategy prevents over-deletion
- Dry-run mode produces identical scoring without deleting
- Circuit breaker activates on consecutive failures
- All deletions logged with full context (path, size, score, reason)
- Post-deletion verification confirms space was freed
- Error recovery: single failed deletion doesn't stop the process
- Unit tests with temp directory trees
- Integration test: create artifacts → score → delete → verify space freed
close_reason: done

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: The open-file check (checking if a file/directory is in use by a process) is one of the most important safety mechanisms. Implementation detail:

Linux: Read /proc/*/fd symlinks. For each process, readlink() each fd. If any fd points to a path under the candidate directory, the candidate is "open" and gets a hard veto.

PERFORMANCE CONCERN: Scanning /proc/*/fd for every deletion candidate is O(processes × fd_per_process). On a machine with 100+ agent processes, each with 100+ open fds, this is 10K+ readlink() calls per candidate. Mitigations:
1. Cache the open-file set: collect ALL open paths once per scan cycle, store in a HashSet, then do O(1) lookups
2. Use /proc/<pid>/maps as a secondary check (maps shows memory-mapped files)
3. Rate-limit: only refresh the open-file cache every N seconds (default 5s)
4. Under extreme pressure (Critical), SKIP the open-file check for non-.git, non-system paths (the daemon itself can't afford to scan /proc when the disk is about to fill up)

The is_open flag is computed by the CALLER (main loop or CLI clean command) and passed to the scoring engine as part of ScoringInput. The scoring engine itself never touches /proc.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Clarification on /proc/*/fd open-file check vs scorer is_open flag: The scorer (bd-x9z) takes is_open: bool as input. The WALKER (bd-1w9) is responsible for checking /proc/*/fd during traversal and setting this flag on ScoringInput. The deletion executor (bd-1hh) does a SECOND /proc check as a pre-flight safety re-verification — this is NOT redundant because time may have passed between scoring and deletion (the candidate might have been opened since scoring). The re-check is a last-moment safety net. Keep it.

title: Project scaffolding: Cargo.toml, workspace structure, rust-toolchain.toml
description:
Set up the complete Rust project structure for storage_ballast_helper (sbh).

## Technical Approach
- Create Cargo.toml with edition = "2024", binary name = "sbh"
- Create rust-toolchain.toml targeting STABLE (edition 2024 is stable since Rust 1.85, Jan 2025)
- Set up directory structure: src/{main.rs, lib.rs, cli/, core/, monitor/, ballast/, scanner/, logger/, daemon/, platform/}
- Configure release profile for size optimization: opt-level="z", lto=true, codegen-units=1, panic="abort", strip=true
- Add #![forbid(unsafe_code)] to lib.rs
- Enable pedantic + nursery clippy lints

## Key Dependencies (initial)
- clap (derive) for CLI
- serde + serde_json for serialization
- rusqlite (bundled) for SQLite logging
- toml for configuration parsing
- thiserror for error derives
- colored for terminal output
- chrono for timestamps
- nix (Linux) / core-foundation (macOS) for platform-specific ops
- parking_lot for efficient locks
- crossbeam-channel for thread communication
- memchr for fast byte scanning
- regex for artifact pattern matching (bd-1sw)
- signal-hook for safe signal handling (bd-2s9)
- rand for ballast file random data generation (bd-25g)

## Design Rationale
Following asupersync's pattern: NO tokio dependency. Use std threads + crossbeam channels for the daemon monitoring loop. This keeps the binary small, startup fast, and avoids async runtime complexity for what is fundamentally a polling-based system daemon.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `cargo check` passes with zero warnings
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` passes
- Binary compiles to < 5MB release size
- Project structure mirrors the modular architecture described in the design
acceptance_criteria: 1. Cargo workspace and module layout compile cleanly with Rust 2024 toolchain and strict lint gates. 2. Baseline test harness is scaffolded: unit-test module structure plus e2e script entrypoint with verbose logging hooks. 3. Build profiles and forbid-unsafe policy are enforced in root configuration. 4. Core dependency set supports scanner/daemon/CLI architecture without placeholder shims. 5. Foundation docs describe how new modules register tests and structured logs.
notes: Progress update: implemented crate-level unsafe forbids, baseline integration harness (tests/common + tests/integration_tests), executable scripts/e2e_test.sh with verbose case logs, and docs/testing-and-logging.md. Verified: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo test --test integration_tests, ./scripts/e2e_test.sh. Status remains in_progress until repository-wide clippy -D warnings debt (pre-existing across multiple modules) is resolved.
close_reason: Complete

title: Build artifact pattern registry with heuristic classification rules
description:
## Deliverable
A comprehensive, extensible registry of filename/path patterns that identify build artifacts, temporary files, and cache directories across all major build systems.

## Technical Approach
### Pattern Categories (from real-world agent swarm data)

#### Rust/Cargo Patterns (highest priority - this is the primary use case)
Matching patterns observed across real agent sessions:
- `target/` - standard Cargo target directory
- `.target*` - hidden target variants (.target_boldmeadow_auth3, .target-topaz, .target-opus)
- `_target_*` - underscore-prefixed (_target_opus_main, _target_codex_deepdive)
- `.tmp_target*` - temp targets (.tmp_target_codex_or, .tmp_target_pearlgorge)
- `cargo-target-*` - named cargo targets (cargo-target-quietwillow-mvcc, cargo-target-pearl)
- `cargo_*` - alternate cargo patterns (cargo_opus_prime)
- `*-target` - suffix targets (jadefinch-target, scarletwaterfall-target)
- `target-*` - prefix targets (target-ubuntu-am, target-maroon)
- `.tmp_cargo_home_*` - temp cargo homes
- `.tmp-codex-*` - codex temp dirs
- `.tmp-pijs-*` - pi_agent temp dirs
- `.tmp-ext-*` - extension test dirs
- `pi_agent_*` - pi_agent variants (pi_agent_cyanrobin, pi_agent_rust_opus)
- `pi_target_*` - pi_target variants
- `pi_opus_*` - pi_opus variants
- `.tmp-opt-target*` - optimization test targets
- `br-build*` - beads_rust build dirs
- `cass-target*` - cass build dirs

**Structural indicators within directories:**
- Contains `incremental/` → almost certainly a Rust target dir
- Contains `deps/` + `build/` → very likely Rust target
- Contains `.fingerprint/` → definitely Rust target
- Contains `release/` or `debug/` with .d files → Rust target

#### Node.js Patterns
- `node_modules/`
- `.next/` (Next.js build)
- `dist/`
- `.cache/` (various build tools)
- `.parcel-cache/`

#### Python Patterns
- `__pycache__/`
- `.venv/` / `venv/`
- `*.egg-info/`
- `.mypy_cache/`
- `.pytest_cache/`

#### General Build Patterns
- `build/` (many languages)
- `.build/`
- `out/`
- `.gradle/`
- `.tox/`

#### Cache Patterns
- `~/.cache/go-build`
- `~/.cache/pip`
- `~/.cache/huggingface`
- `~/.cache/actcache`

### Pattern Registry Implementation
```rust
pub struct ArtifactPattern {
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub description: &'static str,
    pub name_regex: Regex,
    pub structural_markers: Vec<&'static str>,  // subdirs that confirm classification
    pub confidence: f64,         // 0.0-1.0 base confidence
    pub category: ArtifactCategory,
    pub typical_size_range: (u64, u64),  // expected size range for plausibility
}

pub enum ArtifactCategory {
    RustTarget,     // highest confidence
    NodeModules,
    PythonCache,
    BuildOutput,
    CacheDir,
    TempDir,
    AgentWorkspace, // agent-specific temp builds
    Unknown,
}
```

### Matching Algorithm
For each discovered directory:
1. Check name against all patterns (regex match)
2. If name matches, check structural markers (does it contain incremental/, deps/, etc.)
3. Combine name confidence with structural confidence
4. Return ArtifactClassification with overall confidence

```rust
pub struct ArtifactClassification {
    pub pattern_name: &'static str,
    pub category: ArtifactCategory,
    pub name_confidence: f64,
    pub structural_confidence: f64,
    pub combined_confidence: f64,
}
```

### Extensibility
Users can add custom patterns via config:
```toml
[[scanner.custom_patterns]]
name = "my-tool-cache"
regex = "^\\.my-tool-cache$"
confidence = 0.8
category = "CacheDir"
```

## Design Rationale
The pattern list is derived from REAL data collected from weeks of agent swarm operation. The naming conventions are diverse and creative (agents name targets after themselves: boldmeadow, quietwillow, etc.), so we need comprehensive regex patterns. Structural markers add a second layer of verification: even if a directory is named "target", we check if it LOOKS like a Rust target dir inside. This prevents false positives.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All observed agent naming patterns from the specification are covered
- Structural markers correctly identify Rust target directories
- No false positives on common directory names (e.g., a user's "target" document folder)
- Custom patterns loadable from config
- Confidence scores reflect actual classification quality
- Unit tests for every pattern category
- Unit test: directory named "target" but containing only documents → low confidence
- Unit test: directory named ".target_opus_main" with incremental/ inside → high confidence
acceptance_criteria: 1. Deterministic pattern classification for identical inputs across all built-in and custom rules. 2. Unit tests cover positive/negative fixtures, precedence, and confidence calibration boundaries. 3. Integration tests validate handoff into walker/scorer without false category drift. 4. E2E scenarios exercise real artifact trees and verify expected matched categories. 5. Detailed match-trace logging includes pattern id, confidence, and suppression/veto reasons.
close_reason: Implemented in scanner/patterns.rs

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Missing error handling for user-supplied custom regex patterns:

When a user adds a custom pattern in config:
  [[scanner.custom_patterns]]
  name = "my-cache"
  regex = "[invalid(regex"  # <-- broken regex

The pattern registry must:
1. Validate each custom regex at config load time using Regex::new()
2. If invalid: emit SBH-4001 error with the regex string, position of syntax error, and suggestion
3. NEVER crash — skip the invalid pattern and log a warning
4. In strict mode (sbh config validate): report all invalid patterns as errors
5. In normal operation: silently skip invalid patterns but log them to JSONL

This follows the "don't let user config mistakes crash the daemon" principle. A typo in one custom pattern should not prevent sbh from monitoring with all its built-in patterns.

title: Special location registry for RAM-backed and critical filesystems
description:
## Deliverable
A registry of special filesystem locations (tmpfs, /dev/shm, RAM-backed mounts) that require more aggressive monitoring and lower free-space thresholds.

## Technical Approach
### SpecialLocationRegistry
```rust
pub struct SpecialLocationRegistry {
    locations: Vec<SpecialLocation>,
}

pub struct SpecialLocation {
    pub path: PathBuf,
    pub kind: SpecialKind,
    pub buffer_pct: f64,          // minimum free % to maintain (default 15%)
    pub scan_interval: Duration,   // how often to check (default 5s)
    pub priority: u8,              // higher = more important to keep free
}

pub enum SpecialKind {
    Tmpfs,          // /tmp on most Linux systems
    DevShm,         // /dev/shm - POSIX shared memory
    RunShm,         // /run/shm - some distros
    Ramfs,          // true RAM filesystem (no swap)
    UserTmp,        // /data/tmp or similar user-managed temp
    Custom(String), // user-configured special locations
}
```

### Auto-Discovery
On startup, scan mount points (via PAL) to find:
- All tmpfs mounts
- /dev/shm (always special on Linux)
- Any mount with fs_type == "ramfs"
- User-configured locations from config

### Why Special Locations Matter
RAM-backed filesystems are bounded by physical memory. When /tmp (tmpfs) fills up:
- Build processes fail with "No space left on device"
- System services that use /tmp start failing
- The machine becomes progressively more unstable
- Unlike disk, there's no extra buffer - it's literally eating RAM

This is WORSE than disk filling because RAM pressure causes OOM kills and swap thrashing, which is exactly the scenario sbh exists to prevent.

### Monitoring Strategy
Special locations get:
- 3-6x higher scan frequency than disk-backed locations
- Lower buffer thresholds (15% free minimum vs 5% for disk)
- Priority ballast release when they fill up
- Immediate logging when buffer threshold is breached
- RAM-backed locations report both their own free% AND system memory impact

## Acceptance Criteria
- Auto-discovers all tmpfs, devshm, ramfs mounts on Linux
- Correctly classifies each special location
- Configurable buffer_pct per location
- Scan intervals are shorter for special locations
- Reports special location pressure separately from disk pressure
- Unit tests with mock mount point data
close_reason: Special location registry implemented in monitor/special_locations.rs (210 lines): SpecialLocationRegistry with auto-discovery from mount_points(), SpecialKind enum (Tmpfs/DevShm/Ramfs/UserTmp/Custom), per-location buffer_pct/scan_interval/priority, /tmp fallback, unit tests.

title: Align CLI state staleness thresholds with daemon write cadence
description: run_status/run_check/read_daemon_prediction use thresholds (2*poll or 30s) that are tighter than self_monitor's 30s write interval, causing false 'daemon not running' and dropped predictions. Use a shared floor (>=60s) or shared constant.
close_reason: Fixed: all 3 CLI staleness checks now use shared DAEMON_STATE_STALE_THRESHOLD_SECS (90s) constant from self_monitor.rs instead of flawed poll-interval-based formulas

title: Parallel directory walker with cross-device and symlink safety
description:
## Deliverable
A high-performance parallel directory walker that discovers candidate files/directories for cleanup, with safety guards against crossing filesystem boundaries, following symlinks, or entering protected paths.

## Technical Approach
### Walker Design
```rust
pub struct DirectoryWalker {
    config: WalkerConfig,
    excluded_paths: HashSet<PathBuf>,
    seen_inodes: HashSet<u64>,     // detect hardlink loops
    results_tx: crossbeam::Sender<WalkEntry>,
}

pub struct WalkerConfig {
    pub root_paths: Vec<PathBuf>,
    pub max_depth: usize,          // default 10
    pub follow_symlinks: bool,     // default false (NEVER follow by default)
    pub cross_devices: bool,       // default false (stay on same filesystem)
    pub parallelism: usize,        // default num_cpus / 2
    pub excluded_patterns: Vec<Regex>,
}

pub struct WalkEntry {
    pub path: PathBuf,
    pub metadata: EntryMetadata,
    pub depth: usize,
}

pub struct EntryMetadata {
    pub size_bytes: u64,           // for dirs: recursive size estimate
    pub file_type: FileType,
    pub modified: SystemTime,
    pub created: Option<SystemTime>,
    pub is_dir: bool,
    pub inode: u64,
    pub device_id: u64,
    pub permissions: u32,
}
```

### Safety Guards
1. **No symlink following**: Symlinks could lead anywhere, including outside the scan scope. Always use lstat, never stat.
2. **No cross-device**: Stay on the same filesystem. A target/ dir on /data shouldn't cause us to scan /boot.
3. **Excluded paths**: Never enter /, /boot, /etc, /usr, /bin, /sbin, /var/log, /proc, /sys, /dev (except /dev/shm)
4. **Inode dedup**: Detect hardlink cycles via inode tracking
5. **Permission checking**: Skip directories we can't read (log warning, don't fail)
6. **Rate limiting**: Under extreme system load, throttle walking to avoid making things worse

### Parallelism Strategy
Use a work-stealing pattern (inspired by rayon) but with explicit control:
- Main thread discovers top-level directories
- Worker threads process subdirectories via crossbeam channel
- Each worker sends results back via channel
- Bounded channel prevents unbounded memory growth

### Performance Under Load
When the system is already overloaded (the exact scenario where sbh is needed), the walker must be gentle:
- Use ionice equivalent (Linux: ioprio_set with IOPRIO_CLASS_IDLE)
- Yield between directory reads under high load average
- Limit parallelism based on current load average
- Skip recursive size calculation when time is critical (use estimate)

### Directory Size Estimation
For directories, we need size but du -s is expensive. Two modes:
- **Estimate mode** (default under pressure): sum of direct children only, extrapolate
- **Accurate mode** (low pressure): full recursive stat

## Design Rationale
The walker is the "eyes" of the scanner. It must be thorough but safe. The biggest risk is accidentally scanning a mounted USB drive, NFS share, or critical system directory. The safety guards prevent this. Parallelism speeds up scanning on large project directories but is throttled under load to avoid contributing to the very problem we're solving.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Discovers all directories/files under root_paths within configured depth
- Never follows symlinks by default
- Never crosses filesystem boundaries
- Never enters excluded paths
- Correctly handles permission denied (skip, warn, continue)
- Parallel scanning is faster than sequential for large trees
- Throttles under high load (verifiable by checking iteration delay)
- Unit tests with synthetic directory trees
- Unit test: directory with symlink loop → correctly handled
- Unit test: cross-device mount → not followed
close_reason: Implemented parallel directory walker with crossbeam work-stealing, protection integration, structural signal collection, open-file detection, 14 tests passing

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: The walker must collect structural markers during traversal. When walking a candidate directory, check for the presence of key subdirectories (incremental/, deps/, .fingerprint/, build/, .git/, node_modules/.package-lock.json, etc.) and attach them as Vec<String> to WalkEntry.structural_markers. This data is consumed by the scoring engine (bd-x9z) which is a pure computation and cannot do filesystem I/O. The walker is the only component that touches the filesystem during scanning.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: Walker must also perform open-file detection for the scorer. During each scan cycle, collect the set of all open file paths from /proc/*/fd into a HashSet<PathBuf> (refreshed every 5s, cached between refreshes). For each WalkEntry, set is_open: bool if any fd in the open-file set points to a path under the candidate. Add is_open: bool to WalkEntry struct. Under Critical pressure, skip this check entirely (too slow when disk is thrashing). On macOS, use lsof equivalent via PAL (bd-sth).

title: [macOS E5] Daemon service lifecycle via launchd (LaunchAgent + LaunchDaemon)
description:
# Epic 5: Daemon service lifecycle via launchd

## Why this matters

Today `sbh install --systemd --auto` writes a systemd unit file. On Mac that errors out with `[SBH-CONFIG-WARNING]`. We need launchd integration: generate a `.plist`, place it in the right directory, load via `launchctl bootstrap`, manage with `launchctl kickstart`, etc.

## launchd concepts (the cliff notes)

- **LaunchDaemon vs LaunchAgent.** Daemon = system-scope, runs as root, started at boot before login. Agent = user-scope, runs as user, started at login. We support both:
  - LaunchDaemon: `/Library/LaunchDaemons/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist`. For full-system protection (everything, all users).
  - LaunchAgent: `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist`. For user-scope protection (own files only). Default for the operator's primary Mac.
- **Bootstrap, bootout, kickstart.** Modern launchctl idioms (Big Sur+):
  - `launchctl bootstrap gui/$UID ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist` (load + enable)
  - `launchctl bootstrap system /Library/LaunchDaemons/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist` (root only)
  - `launchctl kickstart -k gui/$UID/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh` (restart)
  - `launchctl bootout gui/$UID/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh` (unload + disable)
- **Domain targets:** `system/`, `gui/UID/`, `user/UID/`. Pick correct domain when invoking launchctl.
- **Plist required keys:** `Label`, `ProgramArguments`, `RunAtLoad`, `KeepAlive`, `StandardOutPath`, `StandardErrorPath`, `WorkingDirectory`.
- **KeepAlive policies:** `{ SuccessfulExit = false; Crashed = true; }` — restart on crash, not on clean exit. Critical to avoid restart loops on fatal config errors.
- **ThrottleInterval:** Min seconds between restarts (default 10). Set higher (60) to avoid thrash under sustained crash.
- **EnvironmentVariables:** Inject `SBH_CONFIG_PATH`, `RUST_LOG`, etc.
- **LowPriorityIO, ProcessType=Background:** Reduce Energy Impact and disk I/O priority. *We want both for sbh.*

## Required sbh behavior

- `sbh install` on macOS auto-detects launchd (no need for explicit `--launchd` flag, but accept it).
- Default install scope: LaunchAgent (user-scope) for the operator's Mac. Document switching to LaunchDaemon for shared machines.
- Plist generation handles config path overrides (`SBH_CONFIG_PATH`).
- `sbh uninstall` runs `launchctl bootout` + removes plist.
- `sbh service status`, `sbh service restart`, `sbh service logs` shell out to `launchctl print`/`launchctl kickstart`/`tail -f $StandardOutPath` respectively.
- Plist is signed-friendly (no execve weirdness) so it survives notarization.
- KeepAlive set to crash-only restart with 60s throttle.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. New module `src/daemon/service/launchd.rs` with `LaunchdService` struct implementing the `ServiceManager` trait (define this trait too if not present).
2. Plist generation via `plist` crate (or hand-roll — XML plist is simple). Snapshot test the output for both LaunchAgent and LaunchDaemon variants.
3. Refactor existing service code: `src/daemon/service.rs` → `src/daemon/service/mod.rs` + `src/daemon/service/systemd.rs` + `src/daemon/service/launchd.rs`. The mod.rs picks based on `Pal::service_kind()`.
4. `sbh install` CLI: replace explicit `--systemd` with auto-detection; add explicit `--launchd` for symmetry. Both fail with helpful message on wrong OS.
5. `launchctl` wrapper functions: `bootstrap()`, `bootout()`, `kickstart()`, `print()`. Each captures stdout/stderr, parses exit codes (launchctl uses BSD errno values), returns rich error messages.
6. Domain detection helper: am I root? am I in a GUI session? Pick `system/`, `gui/$UID/`, or `user/$UID/`.
7. Permission-elevation flow: LaunchDaemon install needs root (`sudo`). User-scope install does not. CLI must clearly tell user when sudo is needed and exactly which command to run.
8. Logs: ensure StandardOutPath and StandardErrorPath are writable by the runtime user (LaunchAgent: `~/Library/Logs/sbh/`; LaunchDaemon: `/var/log/sbh/`). Auto-create if missing.
9. Self-update: `sbh update` on macOS must trigger `launchctl kickstart` after binary replace (otherwise daemon keeps running old binary in memory).
10. Integration test (in CI on macos-latest): install LaunchAgent, query status, restart, uninstall, verify clean state.

## Edge cases

- User runs `sbh install` over an existing install → must `bootout` before re-bootstrapping (avoids "Service already exists" errors).
- Plist exists but launchctl doesn't know about it → recover by `launchctl load` (legacy) or `launchctl bootstrap` (modern).
- User logs out / logs back in → LaunchAgent re-bootstraps automatically. Document this so user isn't surprised by sbh "stopping" briefly during logout.
- Sleep/wake cycles → KeepAlive handles. But long sleeps (laptop closed overnight) mean memory pressure data has gaps. Daemon must handle gaps gracefully.
- macOS Recovery / Safe Boot → no LaunchAgents loaded. Document.

## Failure modes

- Writing plist with `<string>` containing literal `&`/`<` without entity-encoding → plist parse error. Use the `plist` crate or escape properly.
- Forgetting `RunAtLoad=true` → daemon never starts on first install.
- Setting `KeepAlive=true` (boolean true, not dict) → daemon restarts immediately on any exit including SIGTERM from uninstall → causes uninstall to look like it failed.
- Hardcoding `/usr/local/bin/sbh` → fails when binary is at `~/.local/bin/sbh` (current install location). Use `which sbh` at install time and embed absolute path in plist.

description: Today src/daemon/service.rs is implicitly Linux/systemd. Define a ServiceManager trait covering: install(plist_path, scope), uninstall(label), status(label) returning ServiceStatus, restart(label), logs_path(label) returning PathBuf, is_loaded(label) returning bool. Split into src/daemon/service/mod.rs (trait + dispatcher), src/daemon/service/systemd.rs (Linux), src/daemon/service/launchd.rs (Mac). The dispatcher picks per pal.service_kind(). Linux code mechanical-move only.

Required keys: Label (com.dicklesworthstone.sbh); ProgramArguments (absolute path to sbh binary plus daemon arg); RunAtLoad (true); KeepAlive (dict with SuccessfulExit false, Crashed true); ThrottleInterval (60); StandardOutPath (log path); StandardErrorPath (log path); WorkingDirectory (state dir); ProcessType (Background); LowPriorityIO (true); EnvironmentVariables (SBH_CONFIG_PATH, RUST_LOG). Use plist crate for output (proper XML escaping). Snapshot test the output for both variants — golden file in tests/snapshots/.

Functions: bootstrap(domain_target, plist_path); bootout(service_target); kickstart(service_target, kill_existing); print(service_target) returning ParsedStatus. Domain detection helper: am I root? am I in a GUI session? Pick system/, gui/UID/, or user/UID/. Use launchctl modern idiom (Big Sur and later); avoid deprecated launchctl load/unload.

title: [E5.4] sbh install: auto-detect launchd on Mac; replace explicit --systemd with auto
description: On macOS: sbh install auto-detects launchd; install LaunchAgent by default; --scope=system flag installs LaunchDaemon (requires sudo, error with helpful message if not root). On Linux: sbh install auto-detects systemd. Both accept --launchd or --systemd explicit flags as override (each errors if wrong OS with clear msg). cli_app.rs line 698 reduced to: error if explicit flag conflicts with detected service kind. Acceptance: sbh install on fresh Mac with no flags installs LaunchAgent successfully, idempotent on re-run.

title: [E5.6] sbh service status / restart / logs subcommands on Mac

Use launchctl print parsing; format as table or JSON. sbh service restart equals launchctl kickstart -k. sbh service logs tails StandardOutPath. Cross-platform: same UX as systemctl wrapper on Linux.

Avoid the common pitfall of the user running sudo curl-piped-to-bash which then puts plist in root home dir (wrong scope).

Method: `grep -rn 'cfg.*target_os' src/` plus structural review. Each site classified as one of:
  - **PAL-method**: should become a method on the Pal trait (e.g., reading /proc/pressure/memory)
  - **cfg-gate-keep**: genuinely OS-specific, leave as-is with a justifying comment (e.g., sd_notify call)
  - **refactor-out**: the whole code path is platform-leaky and needs rethinking (e.g., scanning tied to Linux mountinfo format)

Output drives E1.2 (trait surface design). Without this audit we'd be guessing what methods PAL needs.

src/platform/mock.rs (gated #[cfg(test)] or behind a 'testing' feature) — MockPal with builder API to set return values for each method. Used by unit tests of higher-level logic (scanner, daemon, policy) without touching real syscalls.

Convert key high-level functions to generic over Pal: e.g., `fn scan<P: Pal>(pal: &P, ...) -> Vec<Candidate>` instead of using a global pal::current() inside scan.

Why matters: lets us test cleanup-confidence logic without setting up real APFS volumes or spinning up real processes. Property tests in E14 depend on this.

title: Integration test suite: full-pipeline tests with realistic scenarios
description:
## Deliverable
Integration tests that exercise the full sbh pipeline from pressure detection through scanning, scoring, cleanup, fallback, and recovery.

## Scope Expansion (Plan-Space Revision)
This integration bead now explicitly validates all major feature clusters, including:
- predictive actions and early-warning pipeline
- emergency zero-write mode
- multi-volume ballast coordination
- project protection markers
- process attribution and explainability paths
- decision-plane adaptive/fallback behavior

## Technical Approach
### Scenario Set
1. Normal operation with green pressure and no deletion
2. Gradual pressure buildup with controller escalation
3. Critical pressure with ballast-first recovery
4. Special location pressure handling (/tmp/devshm style)
5. Dry-run pipeline parity (no side effects)
6. Recovery and ballast replenishment
7. Concurrent scanner/deletion/logger interactions
8. Error recovery with circuit-breaker behavior
9. Predictive action preemption before threshold crossing
10. Emergency zero-write policy path and rollback to normal mode
11. Protected project markers preventing destructive actions
12. Multi-volume pressure balancing and action allocation

### Detailed Logging Contract
Each scenario must assert both behavior and logs:
- pressure transitions with timestamps
- selected actions and reasons
- fallback activations and recovery criteria
- reclaimed bytes, skipped candidates, and veto reasons
- trace IDs linking to decision/evidence records

## Acceptance Criteria
- All scenarios pass deterministically
- No temp artifacts leaked after test run
- Tests complete within bounded runtime budget
- Behavior assertions include log assertions for each scenario
- Coverage includes adaptive and conservative paths
- Integration logs are machine-readable and human-auditable
acceptance_criteria: 1. Integration suite exercises full dataflow from scan to action/logging under realistic conditions. 2. Cross-module contracts (CLI/daemon/scanner/controller/logger) are verified in end-to-end pipeline fixtures. 3. Failure-mode tests validate fallback-safe behavior and recovery transitions. 4. Detailed integration logs include trace IDs, stage timings, and decision rationale snapshots. 5. Machine-readable reports summarize pass/fail per scenario and contract.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
ADDITIONAL INTEGRATION TEST (idea-wizard):

**Scenario 9: Predictive Pre-emptive Cleanup**
- Start at 80% free (Green)
- Simulate rapid fill at 500 MB/s using MockPlatform
- EWMA should detect acceleration and predict exhaustion in ~20 min
- Predictive action pipeline should trigger PreemptiveCleanup BEFORE threshold is crossed
- Verify cleanup starts while disk is still at 75% free (well above threshold)
- Verify cleanup is gentle (high score threshold, not aggressive)
- Verify disk never reaches Orange pressure level

**Scenario 10: Multi-Volume Ballast**
- Set up 2 mock volumes (/data at 5% free, /var at 60% free)
- Provision ballast pools on both
- Trigger pressure on /data
- Verify ballast released from /data's pool (not /var's)
- Verify space freed on /data
- Recovery: verify replenishment on /data when pressure subsides

title: Pressure-responsive ballast release with automatic replenishment
description:
## Deliverable
Integration between the PID pressure controller and ballast manager: automatically release ballast files when pressure rises, and replenish when pressure subsides.

## Technical Approach
### Release Logic
The PID controller outputs a PressureResponse. When it includes ReleaseBallast(N):
1. Log the decision with full context (pressure level, free%, rate estimate)
2. Release N ballast files via BallastManager
3. Wait 2 seconds for filesystem to update
4. Re-measure pressure
5. If still critical, release more (up to all ballast)
6. Log total released and resulting free%

### Replenishment Logic
When pressure returns to Green and stays there for replenish_cooldown (default 30 minutes):
1. Check how many ballast files were released
2. Start replenishing one file at a time
3. After each file, re-check pressure - abort if no longer Green
4. Log replenishment progress

### Rate-Limited Replenishment
Replenishing (writing 1GB files) itself causes disk I/O. Rate-limit to:
- One file every 5 minutes during replenishment
- Only during Green pressure
- Pause if any other disk activity is detected (via I/O wait)

### Graduated Response
The PID controller determines HOW MANY ballast files to release based on urgency:
- urgency 0.0-0.3: release 1 file (buy ~1GB breathing room)
- urgency 0.3-0.6: release 3 files (buy ~3GB)
- urgency 0.6-0.9: release half of remaining ballast
- urgency 0.9-1.0: release ALL ballast (emergency)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Ballast released proportionally to pressure urgency
- No oscillation: released ballast stays released until cooldown
- Replenishment is gradual and pressure-aware
- Replenishment pauses if pressure increases
- All releases logged with full context
- Integration test: simulate pressure → verify release → simulate recovery → verify replenish

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Important edge case — ballast files must be on the SAME filesystem that's under pressure. If /data is filling up but ballast is on /var (different mount), deleting ballast doesn't help.

Implementation must:
1. During provisioning, detect which mount point the ballast_dir is on
2. During release, verify the ballast files are on the same mount as the filesystem under pressure
3. If they're on different mounts, skip ballast release and go straight to artifact scanning
4. Log a warning: "Ballast on /var/lib/sbh/ballast cannot relieve pressure on /data — consider setting ballast.location to /data/.sbh/ballast"
5. Config should suggest putting ballast on the primary data volume: ballast.location = "/data/.sbh/ballast"

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Integration with multi-volume ballast pool coordinator (bd-u92): When bd-u92 is implemented, this bead's BallastManager interface changes from single-pool to multi-pool. Key changes: (1) Release logic must select ballast pool on the SAME filesystem as the pressure source (per existing review comment). (2) BallastPoolCoordinator.release_for_mount(mount_point, count) replaces BallastManager.release(count). (3) Replenishment targets pools proportionally based on config. (4) Per-pool lock files prevent concurrent access from CLI and daemon. (5) State tracking: each pool has its own available/released count.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: Removed bd-u92 hard dependency. Initial implementation uses single-pool BallastManager from bd-25g directly. When bd-u92 (multi-volume coordinator) is available later, the interface upgrades to BallastPoolCoordinator.release_for_mount(). The same-filesystem check from comment 14 still applies. Multi-volume is layered on top, not load-bearing for v1.

title: Ballast file manager: creation, verification, and inventory
description:
## Deliverable
The core ballast file management system: creating, tracking, and verifying the "sacrificial anode" files that can be deleted under pressure.

## Technical Approach
### Ballast File Design
Files named: SBH_BALLAST_FILE_00001.dat through SBH_BALLAST_FILE_NNNNN.dat
Located in: /var/lib/sbh/ballast/ (Linux), ~/Library/Application Support/sbh/ballast/ (macOS)
Default: 10 files × 1GB each = 10GB of reclaimable space

### File Format
Each ballast file contains:
- First 4096 bytes: Header with metadata (JSON)
  - magic: "SBH_BALLAST_v1"
  - file_index: sequential number
  - created_at: ISO 8601 timestamp
  - file_size: total size in bytes
  - xxhash64: checksum of data portion
  - purpose: "Storage ballast for emergency space recovery"
- Remaining bytes: random data generated by getrandom (or /dev/urandom fallback)

Using random data (not zeros) prevents filesystem-level deduplication or compression from defeating the purpose. The data must actually occupy disk blocks.

### BallastManager
```rust
pub struct BallastManager {
    ballast_dir: PathBuf,
    config: BallastConfig,
    inventory: Vec<BallastFile>,
}

pub struct BallastFile {
    pub path: PathBuf,
    pub index: u32,
    pub size: u64,
    pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub integrity_ok: bool,
    pub released: bool,
}

impl BallastManager {
    /// Create all ballast files (idempotent, skips existing valid files)
    pub fn provision(&mut self) -> Result<ProvisionReport, SbhError>;
    
    /// Check inventory: how many files exist, their status
    pub fn inventory(&self) -> &[BallastFile];
    
    /// How many bytes can be released
    pub fn releasable_bytes(&self) -> u64;
    
    /// Release N ballast files (delete highest-index first)
    pub fn release(&mut self, count: usize) -> Result<ReleaseReport, SbhError>;
    
    /// Verify integrity of all ballast files (check headers, optional checksum)
    pub fn verify(&self) -> Result<VerifyReport, SbhError>;
    
    /// Replenish: recreate released ballast files when pressure subsides
    pub fn replenish(&mut self) -> Result<ProvisionReport, SbhError>;
}
```

### Creation Strategy
Creating 10GB of random data takes time. Strategy:
1. Create files sequentially (not in parallel - we don't want to cause the very disk pressure we're trying to prevent)
2. Write in 4MB chunks with fsync every 64MB
3. Check free space before each file creation - abort if we'd go below 20% free
4. Log progress to stderr for visibility

### Release Strategy
When pressure rises, delete ballast files in reverse order (highest index first):
- Each deletion frees exactly file_size_mb (e.g., 1GB)
- Log each release to the activity logger
- After release, immediately re-check pressure to see if more releases needed

## Design Rationale
The "ballast" concept is inspired by ships using ballast water for stability. By pre-allocating space, we create a guaranteed buffer that can be instantly reclaimed. This is critical because under extreme disk pressure, even creating a log file might fail. The ballast provides breathing room for the scanner and cleaner to do their work.

Random data prevents CoW/dedup/compress from silently reclaiming the space. The header enables verification that files haven't been tampered with.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Ballast files created with correct format and random content
- Files are genuinely occupying disk blocks (verified via du vs stat)
- Release correctly deletes files and reports freed space
- Replenish recreates files when pressure subsides
- Integrity verification catches corrupted/truncated files
- Idempotent provision: re-running doesn't duplicate files
- Unit tests for all operations
- Test: provision → verify → release 3 → verify → replenish → verify
close_reason: done

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Two important additions needed:

1. FALLOCATE OPTIMIZATION: Writing 10GB of random data takes 25-50s (getrandom/urandom maxes out ~200-400 MB/s). On ext4/xfs, use posix_fallocate() or fallocate() which is INSTANT — it reserves blocks on disk without writing data. This is the correct approach for the primary target (Linux ext4). Only fall back to random data writing on CoW filesystems (btrfs, zfs) where fallocate might not prevent deduplication. Detection: check fs_type from PAL. Implementation:
  - ext4/xfs: fallocate() → instant provisioning
  - btrfs/zfs: write random data in 4MB chunks → 25-50s for 10GB
  - tmpfs: NEVER put ballast on tmpfs (that defeats the purpose)

2. FILE LOCKING: When the daemon is running and a user runs 'sbh ballast release 1', both could try to manipulate ballast files simultaneously. Use flock() on a lockfile (/var/lib/sbh/.ballast.lock) to serialize access. The daemon holds the lock during ballast operations, CLI commands wait for the lock with a timeout (5s).

title: systemd unit file generation and service installation (Linux)
description:
## Deliverable
Generate and install a systemd service unit file for the sbh daemon on Linux systems.

## Technical Approach
### Generated Unit File
```ini
[Unit]
Description=Storage Ballast Helper - Disk Space Guardian
Documentation=man:sbh(1)
After=local-fs.target
Wants=local-fs.target

[Service]
Type=notify
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/sbh daemon
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=10
WatchdogSec=60
TimeoutStopSec=30
Nice=19
IOSchedulingClass=idle
IOSchedulingPriority=7

# Security hardening
NoNewPrivileges=true
ProtectSystem=strict
ReadWritePaths=/var/lib/sbh /tmp /data/tmp
ProtectHome=read-only
PrivateTmp=false
ProtectKernelTunables=true
ProtectControlGroups=true
RestrictSUIDSGID=true
LimitNOFILE=4096

# Resource limits
MemoryMax=256M
CPUQuota=10%

# Logging
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
SyslogIdentifier=sbh

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

### Key Design Choices
- **Type=notify**: sbh will notify systemd when it's ready (sd_notify)
- **Nice=19 + IOSchedulingClass=idle**: sbh runs at lowest priority - it must NEVER compete with the build processes it's trying to protect
- **ProtectSystem=strict**: security hardening, only write to /var/lib/sbh
- **ReadWritePaths**: explicitly list all paths sbh needs to write to
- **ProtectHome=read-only**: can read home dirs (to scan) but not write
- **MemoryMax=256M**: prevent sbh itself from consuming too much RAM
- **CPUQuota=10%**: limit CPU usage
- **WatchdogSec=60**: systemd restarts sbh if it stops responding
- **Restart=on-failure**: auto-restart on crash

### Installation Flow
```bash
sbh install --systemd
```
1. Generate unit file from template (with config-specific paths)
2. Copy to /etc/systemd/system/sbh.service (or ~/.config/systemd/user/)
3. systemctl daemon-reload
4. systemctl enable sbh
5. systemctl start sbh
6. Verify service is running

### User vs System Service
- `sbh install`: system service (requires root)
- `sbh install --user`: user service (no root needed)

### sd_notify Integration
The daemon calls sd_notify at key points:
- READY=1 after startup is complete
- WATCHDOG=1 every 30 seconds during normal operation
- STATUS=Monitoring N paths, M GB free on /data

## Acceptance Criteria
- Generated unit file is valid (systemd-analyze verify)
- Service starts, stops, and restarts correctly
- Watchdog keeps service alive
- Resource limits enforced (memory, CPU)
- Security sandboxing works (can't write to system dirs)
- User-mode service works without root
- sd_notify integration functional
- Unit tests for unit file generation
- Integration test: install → start → verify running → stop → uninstall
close_reason: Implemented systemd unit file generation and service installation. 15 unit tests passing. Wired up CLI install/uninstall handlers.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: ReadWritePaths conflict with artifact deletion in home directories:

The unit file specifies ProtectHome=read-only, but if scanner.watched_paths includes /home/*/projects/*, sbh CANNOT delete artifacts there because it only has read access. This creates a silent failure.

Fix: When generating the unit file, dynamically set ReadWritePaths to include all paths from scanner.watched_paths that are under /home:
  ReadWritePaths=/var/lib/sbh /tmp /data/tmp /home
  ProtectHome=false  (or use read-only + explicit ReadWritePaths)

Alternative: Separate the systemd sandboxing for system-mode vs user-mode:
- System mode (root): ProtectHome=read-only, ReadWritePaths includes /home
- User mode (no root): no ProtectHome needed (already running as the user)

The template must be generated from config, not hardcoded.

title: [macOS E16] Documentation for Mac users
description:
# Epic 16: Documentation for macOS

## Why this is its own epic

Mac users coming to sbh have different mental models than Linux server admins. The README today is implicitly Linux-flavored. Mac users will be confused by systemd references, /proc/pressure language, etc.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. README: add "macOS" section parallel to "Linux" section. Include install, configure, troubleshoot.
2. New doc: `docs/macos.md` deep-dive. Topics: how sbh interacts with APFS, what purgeable space means, what local snapshots are, FDA grant walkthrough with screenshots, brew install path, custom paths.
3. New doc: `docs/cleanup-rules-macos.md` — exhaustive list of what sbh will and won't touch on Mac. Reference E8 + E9 catalogs. Important: this is the user's confidence document — they read this once, decide to trust sbh, then never look again.
4. New doc: `docs/sacred-paths.md` — cross-platform sacred path doc. Mac-specific section.
5. Updated CHANGELOG: detailed entry per release explaining macOS changes in human terms ("now detects Time Machine local snapshots; can free X GB you didn't know you had").
6. New doc: `docs/launchd-troubleshooting.md` — common errors, fixes, where logs live.
7. New doc: `docs/migrating-from-other-tools.md` — for Mac users coming from CleanMyMac, OmniDiskSweeper, DaisyDisk, GrandPerspective. What sbh does that they don't (continuous monitoring, blame attribution, ballast).
8. Updated `sbh --help` and `sbh <subcommand> --help` to mention macOS-specific behavior.
9. Sample configs for common scenarios: developer Mac (heavy build dirs), creative Mac (heavy media files, but most are sacred), shared Mac (LaunchDaemon scope).
10. Marketing one-liner for the operator's website / blog: "sbh saved my Mac from the brink — here's exactly what it caught" (referencing the 2026-05-03 incident as case study).

## Tone

- Direct. Mac users are sensitive to marketing fluff and "magic" claims. Be specific about what sbh does and doesn't do.
- Honest about limitations. "sbh on macOS does NOT yet handle iCloud Drive Optimize Storage. It WILL detect Time Machine local snapshots."
- Include real numbers from the incident report — concrete examples earn trust.

## Failure modes

- Docs lag behind code; users get misleading info → file issues → erode trust. Mitigation: docs PR is part of every code-changing PR (CI lint check).
- Screenshots go stale on macOS UI updates. Mitigation: maintain alongside code; refresh once per macOS major release; use text-based descriptions wherever possible.
