title: SQLite activity database with WAL mode and schema
description:
## Deliverable
SQLite database for persistent activity logging, using WAL mode for concurrent read/write and optimized for append-heavy workloads.

## Technical Approach
### Schema
```sql
-- Core activity log
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS activity_log (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    timestamp TEXT NOT NULL,             -- ISO 8601
    event_type TEXT NOT NULL,            -- 'ballast_release', 'artifact_delete', 'pressure_change', etc.
    severity TEXT NOT NULL,              -- 'info', 'warning', 'critical'
    path TEXT,                           -- affected path (nullable for non-path events)
    size_bytes INTEGER,                  -- size of deleted item
    score REAL,                          -- candidacy score at time of action
    score_factors TEXT,                  -- JSON blob of individual factor values
    pressure_level TEXT,                 -- pressure level at time of action
    free_pct REAL,                       -- free% at time of action
    duration_ms INTEGER,                 -- how long the action took
    success INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,  -- 1=success, 0=failure
    error_code TEXT,                     -- SBH-XXXX error code if failed
    error_message TEXT,                  -- human-readable error
    details TEXT                         -- JSON blob for additional context
);

-- Pressure history (for trend analysis)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS pressure_history (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    timestamp TEXT NOT NULL,
    mount_point TEXT NOT NULL,
    total_bytes INTEGER NOT NULL,
    free_bytes INTEGER NOT NULL,
    free_pct REAL NOT NULL,
    rate_bytes_per_sec REAL,
    pressure_level TEXT NOT NULL,
    ewma_rate REAL,
    pid_output REAL
);

-- Ballast inventory
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ballast_inventory (
    file_index INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    path TEXT NOT NULL,
    size_bytes INTEGER NOT NULL,
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    released_at TEXT,
    replenished_at TEXT,
    integrity_hash TEXT
);

-- Indexes for common queries
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_activity_timestamp ON activity_log(timestamp);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_activity_event_type ON activity_log(event_type);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_pressure_timestamp ON pressure_history(timestamp);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_pressure_mount ON pressure_history(mount_point);
```

### Connection Management
```rust
pub struct SqliteLogger {
    conn: rusqlite::Connection,
    insert_activity: rusqlite::Statement,    // prepared statement
    insert_pressure: rusqlite::Statement,
}
```

### PRAGMAs
```sql
PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;           -- concurrent reads during writes
PRAGMA synchronous = NORMAL;         -- good durability, better perf than FULL
PRAGMA cache_size = -8000;           -- 8MB page cache
PRAGMA mmap_size = 67108864;         -- 64MB mmap for reads
PRAGMA temp_store = MEMORY;          -- temp tables in memory
PRAGMA busy_timeout = 5000;          -- 5s retry on lock contention
```

### Graceful Degradation
If SQLite operations fail (disk full!), sbh must NOT stop working:
- Log error to stderr
- Continue operating using JSONL-only mode
- Attempt to reopen SQLite periodically

## Design Rationale
SQLite with WAL mode provides excellent concurrent read performance (agents can query stats while daemon writes). Prepared statements minimize parse overhead for high-frequency inserts. The schema captures enough detail for comprehensive post-incident analysis while keeping writes fast.

The graceful degradation is critical: if the disk is so full that even SQLite can't write, sbh must still function (using JSONL on a different filesystem, or just stderr).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Database created with correct schema and PRAGMAs
- Prepared statements for all common operations
- WAL mode confirmed active
- Insert latency < 1ms for typical log entries
- Concurrent read/write without lock contention
- Graceful handling of disk-full during write
- Automatic schema migration for version upgrades
- Unit tests for all CRUD operations
- Test: 10,000 rapid inserts → no data loss
- Test: concurrent reader + writer → no errors
close_reason: SQLite logger implemented in src/logger/sqlite.rs: WAL mode, NORMAL sync, 8MB cache, schema with activity_log/pressure_history/ballast_inventory tables, indexes, CRUD operations, aggregate queries (count_events_since/bytes_freed_since), upsert ballast, 7 passing tests.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Database filesystem location consideration:

The SQLite database MUST NOT be on the same filesystem that's under pressure. If /data is filling up and the database is on /data, SQLite writes will fail precisely when logging is most critical.

Default location should be /var/lib/sbh/sbh.db (typically on / root partition, separate from /data). The config default already handles this: sqlite_path = /var/lib/sbh/sbh.db.

However, the bead should explicitly validate during startup that the SQLite database path is NOT on a monitored filesystem (or at least warn if it is). Add a startup check:
1. Resolve the mount point for sqlite_path
2. Compare against scanner.watched_paths mount points
3. If same mount: log WARNING "[SBH-5001] Activity database is on monitored filesystem /data — database writes may fail under extreme pressure. Consider moving to a separate partition."

title: Fix production reclaim failure: scanner channel starvation, /dev/shm handling, and root ballast coverage
description:
Background
First real production run failed: machine reached 100% on / and 100% /dev/shm while sbh reported red pressure but performed zero effective reclaim (state showed scans=0/deletions=0 with recurring 'scan channel full'). Ballast inventory appeared 0/0 for root mount, so no immediate relief on /.

Work
- Eliminate scanner request starvation/backpressure deadlock pattern so pressure-triggered scans always make forward progress under sustained load.
- Ensure special-location pressure (/dev/shm and /tmp) can trigger bounded reclaim actions even when full-tree scans are slow.
- Ensure ballast discovery/provision/release behavior covers pressured root mount (/) and exposes explicit diagnostics when skipped.
- Add regression tests for scanner queue saturation, special-location emergency behavior, and root ballast inventory semantics.

Acceptance criteria
- Under synthetic scanner saturation, daemon still executes scans/deletions over time (no permanent scan=0 stall).
- /dev/shm pressure produces actionable reclaim path independent of giant filesystem walks.
- Root mount ballast is either provisioned/releasable or clearly reported as intentionally disabled with reason.
- Incident scenario no longer requires external cron script to avoid disk-full outages.
close_reason: Completed follow-up: scanner dispatch now preserves post-early candidates and requeues on executor backpressure

comments.text: Reopened: Follow-up fix: scanner can drop candidates after early dispatch; finishing incident hardening

title: sbh status command: real-time system health dashboard
description:
## Deliverable
A status command that shows current system health, pressure levels, ballast inventory, and recent activity in a clear, informative format.

## Technical Approach
### Human Output
```
sbh status

Storage Ballast Helper v0.1.0
  Uptime: 3 days, 14 hours, 22 minutes
  Config: /etc/sbh/config.toml

Pressure Status:
  ┌──────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────┬───────────┐
  │ Mount Point  │ Total     │ Free      │ Free %  │ Level     │
  ├──────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────┼───────────┤
  │ /data        │   1.8 TB  │   342 GB  │  18.5%  │ 🟢 GREEN  │
  │ /            │   100 GB  │    23 GB  │  23.0%  │ 🟢 GREEN  │
  │ /tmp (tmpfs) │    32 GB  │   4.8 GB  │  15.0%  │ 🟡 YELLOW │
  │ /dev/shm     │   256 GB  │   198 GB  │  77.3%  │ 🟢 GREEN  │
  └──────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────┴───────────┘

Rate Estimate:
  /data:     -12.4 MB/s (recovering)  ~∞ until threshold
  /tmp:     +245.6 MB/s (filling)     ~18 seconds to threshold!

Ballast:
  Files: 8/10 available (2 released under pressure)
  Reclaimable: 8 GB

Recent Activity (last hour):
  Deletions: 12 items, 18.7 GB freed
  Most common: cargo-target-* (8), .target_* (3), target/ (1)
  Failures: 0
```

### JSON Output (--json)
```json
{
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "uptime_seconds": 307342,
  "pressure": {
    "mounts": [
      {"path": "/data", "total": 1800000000000, "free": 342000000000, "free_pct": 18.5, "level": "green"},
      ...
    ],
    "overall": "yellow"
  },
  "rate": {
    "/data": {"bytes_per_sec": -12400000, "trend": "recovering", "seconds_to_threshold": null},
    "/tmp": {"bytes_per_sec": 245600000, "trend": "filling", "seconds_to_threshold": 18}
  },
  "ballast": {"available": 8, "total": 10, "released": 2, "reclaimable_bytes": 8000000000},
  "recent_hour": {"deletions": 12, "bytes_freed": 18700000000, "failures": 0}
}
```

### Implementation
Reads from:
- Live filesystem stats (PAL)
- EWMA rate estimator (if daemon running, via shared state or status file)
- SQLite database (recent activity)
- Ballast inventory

If daemon is not running, still shows filesystem stats and database history but notes that live monitoring is inactive.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Shows all monitored mount points with pressure levels
- Color-coded pressure levels (green/yellow/orange/red)
- Rate estimates with time-to-threshold
- Ballast inventory status
- Recent activity summary
- Works even when daemon is not running (degraded mode)
- JSON mode produces complete, parseable output
- Unit tests for formatting functions
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests cover status rendering, JSON schema, and degraded-mode state handling. 2. Integration tests validate daemon state file ingestion plus live monitor metrics merging. 3. E2E scenarios verify behavior when daemon is running, stopped, stale, or partially unavailable. 4. Command performance stays bounded under high activity history. 5. Detailed status logs include source freshness, missing-signal reasons, and fallback mode transitions.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: Removed bd-2np dependency. sbh status works in degraded mode without state.json: shows direct fs stats from statvfs() + database query for recent events. When state.json is available (daemon running), status ADDITIONALLY shows live EWMA predictions, PID state, scan activity. The state.json read is opportunistic, not required.

title: Installer DX Parity Program (dcg-grade automation for curl|bash + update + bootstrap)
description:
## Background
We want `sbh`/`fsfs` install UX to be as user-friendly and automated as `/dp/destructive_command_guard` (`dcg`): one-command setup, robust verification, auto-configuration, guided defaults, and safe update+rollback.

## Why this epic exists
Existing install-related beads cover service setup and baseline CLI work, but they do not yet encode the full parity set that makes dcg's distribution path smooth for both humans and agents.

## dcg parity baseline (concrete features to mirror)
1. Unix installer with rich flags, platform detection, artifact targeting, preflight checks, checksum + optional sigstore verification, source-build fallback, PATH/completions automation, and polished summary UX.
2. Windows PowerShell installer with equivalent integrity and path automation.
3. Uninstall flow with safe cleanup + optional purge behavior.
4. First-class updater with check/cache/force/rollback/version-list/system-user modes.
5. Auto-configuration integrations for AI tooling with backup/merge semantics.
6. Release artifact contract (target archives + `.sha256` + optional `.sigstore.json`) that installers/updaters can rely on deterministically.

## Program goals
- Batteries-included install and update flow with minimal manual steps.
- Default-safe integrity checks and explicit remediation on failures.
- Idempotent bootstrap and reversible rollback.
- Self-contained bead graph (all rationale and intent in issue bodies/comments).

## Success criteria
1. Fresh machine: install -> verify -> run -> update succeeds via documented happy path.
2. Installer automates PATH, completions, integration bootstrap, and first-run setup.
3. Updater has backup+rollback with bounded retention and clear UX.
4. E2E tests cover happy paths and failure injection scenarios.
5. Docs are sufficient for future maintainers without external planning docs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Epic scope covers Unix installer, Windows installer, uninstall parity, update/rollback, model/assets bootstrap, offline bundle support, and migration/self-healing without dropping functionality.
2. Every feature bead in this epic links to both unit-test coverage (bd-2j5.19) and E2E verification (bd-2j5.14) directly or through dependencies.
3. Structured observability (bd-2j5.18) is integrated so install/update failures are diagnosable from logs alone.
4. Release artifact contract (bd-2j5.13) and docs handbook (bd-2j5.15) are complete before epic closure.
5. Dependency graph remains acyclic and supports parallel work where safe.
close_reason: Epic acceptance met: all child beads are closed including unit matrix (bd-2j5.19), E2E matrix (bd-2j5.14), offline bundle mode (bd-2j5.20), update cache/notice policy (bd-2j5.9), release artifact contract (bd-2j5.13), structured observability (bd-2j5.18), and operator handbook (bd-2j5.15). Dependency graph remains acyclic with no blocked issues; key validation gates were re-run via rch during closure pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Implementation note: This epic intentionally mirrors concrete dcg behaviors that drive real-world usability: (1) deterministic artifact resolution, (2) default integrity checks, (3) idempotent auto-configuration, (4) updater rollback semantics, and (5) strong installer summaries with remediation hints. Keep parity decisions explicit: if we diverge, record why in child issue comments.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Revision note (plan-space optimization): Added explicit Windows parity, uninstall parity, observability, unit-matrix, offline bundle, and migration/self-healing tracks. This closes major UX/completeness gaps while preserving all previously scoped functionality. Also promoted explicit acceptance criteria fields and test/logging gates across all children so completion standards are enforceable by tooling, not just narrative descriptions.

title: Parity Matrix and Acceptance Spec for dcg-grade installer/update UX
description:
## Deliverable
A parity matrix that translates dcg installer/update automation into explicit sbh/fsfs behavior requirements and CLI surface definitions.

## Background and rationale
We want implementation to be deterministic, not vibe-driven. This issue codifies exactly what we are mirroring from dcg and what we intentionally diverge from.

## Scope
- Enumerate required installer flags (version pin, dest path, easy mode, verify/no-verify, from-source, offline/no-network, quiet/no-color, no-configure).
- Enumerate updater controls (check, refresh cache, force, rollback, list versions).
- Define required idempotency and rollback guarantees.
- Define output/UX contract (human mode + machine mode).

## Acceptance criteria
1. Matrix maps each dcg automation capability to sbh behavior (implemented/planned/not-applicable + why).
2. Gaps become linked child dependencies in this epic.
3. Future maintainers can make scope decisions from this issue alone.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Parity matrix maps each dcg automation capability to sbh/fsfs behavior: implemented/planned/deferred with rationale.
2. Matrix includes explicit CLI flag contract for install/update/bootstrap/uninstall flows.
3. Matrix includes security policy for verification defaults, bypass behavior, and failure handling.
4. Matrix references required unit/e2e/logging gates for every capability.
5. Output is detailed enough to drive implementation without external planning docs.
close_reason: Completed: parity matrix and acceptance spec documented in docs/installer-dx-parity-matrix.md with capability mapping, CLI contracts, security policy, and gate/bead linkage.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Implemented parity matrix artifact at docs/installer-dx-parity-matrix.md. Includes dcg->sbh capability mapping with implemented/planned/deferred statuses, explicit CLI flag contracts (install/update/bootstrap/uninstall), security defaults/bypass policy, and required unit/e2e/observability gates mapped to beads.

title: Updater backups, rollback flow, and retention pruning
description:
## Deliverable
Updater backup snapshots, rollback command support, and bounded retention pruning.

## Background and rationale
Automatic updates without rollback are operationally unsafe.

## Scope
- Store pre-update binary/config snapshots before replacement.
- Add rollback flow (latest or selected prior version).
- Prune old backups by policy while preserving recent recovery points.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Failed update can be rolled back in one command.
2. Backup inventory is inspectable (list timestamps/versions).
3. Retention policy prevents unbounded disk growth.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Update process always captures restore points before replacement.
2. Rollback supports latest and selected prior versions deterministically.
3. Retention pruning enforces bounds without deleting required recovery points.
4. Unit tests cover backup inventory and rollback selection logic.
5. E2E validates failed-update recovery and rollback logging.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Coordination note from CopperAnchor: rch validation surfaced compile blocker in src/cli/update.rs (E0433 undeclared BackupStore at line 306; downstream E0282). Reported to owning agent on Agent Mail thread bd-2j5.10.

title: Guided first-run install wizard + non-interactive --auto mode
description:
## Deliverable
Guided first-run installer wizard plus non-interactive `--auto` mode.

## Background and rationale
New users should get smart defaults, while automation users need deterministic non-interactive behavior.

## Scope
- Interactive flow for selecting install mode, watched paths, and ballast defaults.
- Clear summary/confirmation step before writing config and provisioning resources.
- `--auto` path for CI/agent usage that applies documented defaults without prompts.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Interactive mode reduces manual post-install editing for first-time users.
2. `--auto` mode is fully non-interactive and script-safe.
3. Wizard choices map cleanly to generated config with reproducible output.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Interactive wizard provides guided defaults and explicit confirmation before mutation.
2. `--auto` mode is fully non-interactive and deterministic for automation.
3. Wizard and auto outputs include config decisions and safety-impacting choices.
4. Unit tests cover wizard decision mapping and non-interactive defaults.
5. E2E validates first-run onboarding and repeat-run idempotency with detailed logs.

title: Automatic model/asset bootstrap: download, verify, cache, prefetch
description:
## Deliverable
Automatic model/asset bootstrap pipeline (download, verify, cache, optional prefetch) for components that require external model/data bundles.

## Background and rationale
User request explicitly calls for automatic model download and batteries-included setup. This must be explicit, policy-driven, and safe.

## Scope
- Define artifact manifest format (name, version, checksum, source URL, size, signature info).
- Implement resumable download + integrity verification + local cache layout.
- Add CLI controls for prefetch, offline mode, and cache inspection/cleanup.
- Integrate bootstrap into install/update where relevant.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Required model/assets can be fetched automatically during install/update.
2. Corrupt or partial downloads are detected and recovered safely.
3. Offline mode fails fast with clear instructions when required assets are missing.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Required model/assets can be auto-fetched, verified, cached, and reused safely.
2. Partial/corrupt downloads are detected and recovered without inconsistent state.
3. Offline behavior is explicit: missing required assets fail fast with remediation.
4. Unit tests cover manifest parsing, checksum validation, and cache policy logic.
5. E2E validates online and offline asset bootstrap paths with detailed logs.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Model/bootstrap note: this issue exists because user explicitly requested automatic model download and batteries-included setup. Treat model/assets as first-class artifacts with manifest-driven integrity, resumable fetch, and offline diagnostics.

title: Release artifact publishing contract for installer/update compatibility
description:
## Deliverable
Release pipeline alignment so installers/updaters can rely on consistent artifact naming, checksums, signatures, and metadata.

## Background and rationale
Installer quality is inseparable from release artifact discipline.

## Scope
- Define required release outputs per platform.
- Ensure CI produces checksums/signature bundles and publishes predictable URLs.
- Validate artifact contract continuously in CI to catch regressions before release.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Every supported target publishes installer-consumable artifacts with metadata.
2. CI fails when artifact naming/metadata contract is broken.
3. Install/update logic no longer depends on ad-hoc release layout assumptions.
acceptance_criteria:
1. CI publishes deterministic installer/updater artifacts for all supported targets.
2. Release outputs include integrity metadata required by verification policy.
3. Contract checks fail CI on naming/metadata drift.
4. Unit/CI tests validate artifact contract manifests.
5. Contract supports both online and bundle-based install/update flows.
close_reason: Release artifact publishing contract fully implemented: ReleaseArtifactContract with deterministic naming for 6 targets, validate_release_assets() for CI enforcement, supply chain verification (SHA256+sigstore), install scripts aligned with contract. 11 unit tests cover all acceptance criteria. CI validates via cargo test --lib.

title: Installer/update/integration E2E matrix with failure injection
description:
## Deliverable
Comprehensive installer/update/integration test matrix, including golden UX snapshots and failure-injection scenarios.

## Background and rationale
These workflows are safety-critical and user-facing; regressions here have outsized impact.

## Scope
- E2E tests for fresh install, reinstall, uninstall, update, rollback, and bootstrap integrations.
- Failure injection for network loss, checksum mismatch, unsupported targets, missing prerequisites.
- Golden output snapshots for key user-visible installer/update screens.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Core workflows are test-covered on Linux and macOS paths, with Windows parity where feasible.
2. Failure paths produce deterministic remediation messages.
3. CI gate prevents shipping installer/update regressions.
acceptance_criteria:
1. E2E suite covers install, reinstall, update, rollback, uninstall, bootstrap, and bundle/offline scenarios.
2. E2E suite includes failure injection: network loss, checksum mismatch, permission failure, unsupported target, partial install.
3. Every E2E run captures detailed logs and structured event artifacts for diagnosis.
4. E2E scripts are CI-runnable and deterministic across repeated runs.
5. E2E gate blocks release when critical workflows regress.
close_reason: Acceptance met: installer/update integration E2E matrix passes with failure injection and lifecycle coverage (rch cargo test --test installer_e2e: 43 passed), offline/update integration scenarios pass (rch cargo test --test integration_tests -- update_check_), and CI compile gate remains green (rch cargo check --all-targets). e2e shell suite script is syntactically valid (bash -n scripts/e2e_test.sh).

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Testing bar: include failure injection for checksum mismatch, truncated download, permission errors, unsupported target, and rollback failure. We want deterministic reproductions rather than ad-hoc manual QA.

Validation: cargo fmt --check -- tests/installer_e2e.rs ✅; rch exec "cargo test --test installer_e2e -- e2e_update_offline_bundle" ✅ (2/2); rch exec "cargo test --test installer_e2e -- e2e_update_" ✅ (4/4); rch exec "cargo check --test installer_e2e" ✅. Also added update_sequence_test_lock guard and applied it to full offline-update (check_only=false) tests to eliminate tempdir millisecond collision flake during parallel runs. title: Installer/Updater operator handbook with troubleshooting and security model
description:
## Deliverable
Operator-facing installer/update handbook that is self-sufficient for humans and agents.

## Background and rationale
The easiest system to use is the one whose expected behavior is clearly documented with examples and troubleshooting.

## Scope
- Document happy path + advanced flags + rollback + recovery procedures.
- Include security model (what is verified, what is optional, what is bypassed by `--no-verify`).
- Provide copy-paste recipes for common deployment scenarios (desktop dev, headless server, CI agent host).

## Acceptance criteria
1. README/docs cover complete installer/update lifecycle.
2. Troubleshooting includes concrete commands and expected outputs.
3. Documentation remains aligned with actual CLI flags and test assertions.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Documentation covers full lifecycle: install, update, rollback, uninstall, bootstrap, offline bundle mode.
2. Security model and bypass implications are explicit and example-driven.
3. Troubleshooting includes concrete command sequences and expected diagnostics.
4. Docs align with implemented flags and validated test behavior.
5. Handbook is sufficient for both human operators and agent workflows.
close_reason: Acceptance met: operator handbook coverage is present across README, docs/installer-dx-parity-matrix.md, and docs/testing-and-logging.md for install, update, rollback, uninstall, bootstrap, offline mode, security model, and troubleshooting workflows. Parity matrix command/flag contract now aligns with CLI semantics including update --version, update --list-backups, and bootstrap via install/setup integration paths.

title: Windows PowerShell installer parity with deterministic verification and rollback support
description:
## Deliverable
Full Windows installer parity (PowerShell) matching Unix installer convenience and safety guarantees.

## Background and rationale
`dcg` has a first-class Windows path. For true batteries-included UX, we cannot leave Windows as second-class. Feature parity reduces support burden and avoids fragmented docs.

## Scope
- Ship `install.ps1` equivalent with version pinning, destination selection, easy-mode PATH update, and verification controls.
- Implement deterministic Windows artifact resolution and integrity checks.
- Ensure non-interactive CI/agent mode and human-friendly interactive mode.
- Produce consistent summary output and machine-readable status.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Windows install supports latest + pinned version flows with deterministic artifact selection.
2. Integrity checks (checksum and optional signature path) behave consistently with Unix policy.
3. PATH/profile updates are idempotent and reversible.
4. Unit tests cover argument parsing, artifact resolution, and PATH mutation logic.
5. E2E test validates install -> verify -> update -> rollback -> uninstall with detailed step logs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Windows installer feature parity is achieved for version pinning, verification, easy-mode PATH updates, and diagnostics.
2. Behavior is deterministic and script-safe in non-interactive mode.
3. Structured logs are emitted with parity to Unix operation phases.
4. Unit tests cover Windows-specific parser/path/integrity logic.
5. E2E validates complete Windows lifecycle with detailed logging.
close_reason: Created install.ps1 PowerShell installer with full parity: version pinning, SHA-256 verification, user/system modes, PATH automation, rollback support, dry-run, JSON output, structured event logging, tar/7z extraction

title: Uninstall parity: safe cleanup modes, dry-run plans, and reversible teardown
description:
## Deliverable
Comprehensive uninstall parity with safe cleanup controls and reversible integration teardown.

## Background and rationale
Great installers require equally robust uninstall paths. Users need confident cleanup without accidental data loss.

## Scope
- Implement uninstall modes: conservative default, keep-data/keep-config/keep-assets, and explicit purge.
- Remove auto-configured integrations/hooks with backup-first semantics.
- Add dry-run/plan output showing exactly what will be removed or kept.
- Support partial-failure recovery so uninstall can be retried safely.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Uninstall behavior is explicit, idempotent, and never silently destructive.
2. Integration teardown restores/retains user config according to selected mode.
3. Dry-run output is complete enough for operator review before execution.
4. Unit tests cover cleanup decision matrix and backup/restore paths.
5. E2E tests cover keep-* variants, purge flow, and partial-failure recovery with detailed logs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Uninstall exposes safe default, keep-* modes, and explicit purge with clear previews.
2. Teardown reverses installer-managed integrations safely with backup awareness.
3. Partial-failure paths are recoverable and idempotent.
4. Unit tests cover deletion policy matrix and teardown sequencing.
5. E2E validates uninstall variants with detailed logs and outcome checks.

title: Structured installer/update observability with trace IDs and phase-level diagnostics
description:
## Deliverable
Structured installer/update observability with trace IDs, phase timings, and dual human+machine output.

## Background and rationale
The user explicitly wants confidence that everything works perfectly. That requires rich logs designed for debugging and automation, not ad-hoc println output.

## Scope
- Define event schema for installer/update/bootstrap operations.
- Emit per-phase start/end/failure events with trace IDs and duration metrics.
- Support human-readable and JSONL outputs without loss of diagnostic detail.
- Capture remediation hints and decision reasons in failure events.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Every major installer/update phase emits structured start/success/failure records.
2. Trace IDs allow correlating multi-step operations end-to-end.
3. Logs are stable enough for automated assertions in CI.
4. Unit tests validate event schema and serialization contracts.
5. E2E scripts archive structured logs as artifacts for failed runs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Installer/update flows emit structured start/success/failure events with trace IDs.
2. Event schema is stable and machine-assertable in tests.
3. Human summaries and JSON logs are consistent in meaning.
4. Unit tests validate schema serialization and reason-code contracts.
5. E2E archives logs and traces for failed scenarios automatically.
close_reason: Implemented structured installer observability in scripts/install.sh with trace IDs, phase-level start/success/failure JSONL events (--event-log), and human/json output parity; added E2E assertions for trace/event schema stability

title: Comprehensive unit-test matrix for installer/update/bootstrap/rollback internals
description:
## Deliverable
Comprehensive unit test matrix for installer, updater, bootstrap, and rollback internals.

## Background and rationale
Reliability in install/update flows depends on deterministic behavior under edge cases. Unit tests must lock those contracts before implementation scales.

## Scope
- Table-driven tests for target resolution, checksums, signature policy, rollback selection, cache TTL, and retention pruning.
- Tests for shell/profile mutation idempotency and path normalization.
- Snapshot/golden tests for key machine-readable outputs.
- Negative tests for malformed manifests, missing assets, and corrupt metadata.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Critical decision logic has deterministic unit coverage with explicit edge-case cases.
2. Golden outputs protect CLI/JSON contract stability.
3. Failure cases produce precise reason codes/messages validated by tests.
4. Coverage includes both Unix and Windows-specific logic branches.
5. Test logs are detailed enough to diagnose failures without rerunning interactively.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Unit matrix covers all core installer/update/bootstrap/rollback decision modules.
2. Edge cases and malformed input paths have explicit tests.
3. Golden outputs protect machine-readable contract stability.
4. Tests include platform-specific branches (Unix and Windows logic).
5. Unit test logs are detailed enough for non-interactive diagnosis.
close_reason: Acceptance met: comprehensive installer/update/bootstrap/rollback unit matrix is now green across modules (full rch cargo test --lib passed 765 tests) with explicit edge-case coverage; machine-readable contracts and failure paths validated; all-target compile gate revalidated via rch cargo check --all-targets.

title: Unix curl|bash Installer with polished UX and idempotent behavior
description:
## Deliverable
A polished Unix `curl|bash` installer entrypoint with explicit flags and sensible defaults for new users.

## Background and rationale
dcg's installer succeeds because it is practical: one command works, advanced users still get control, and output is readable under stress.

## Scope
- Implement shell installer command interface and argument parsing.
- Support user/system destination modes and dry-run mode.
- Provide consistent UX framing (headers, progress sections, summary block, clear failure remediations).
- Keep behavior idempotent on repeated runs.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Fresh install and repeat install both succeed without destructive side effects.
2. `--help` clearly documents all flags and examples.
3. Installer output remains clear in both TTY and non-TTY environments.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Unix curl|bash installer supports idempotent fresh/repeat installs with clear summary output.
2. Installer supports documented flags and deterministic non-interactive behavior for CI/agent usage.
3. Installer emits structured observability events per major phase.
4. Unit tests validate parser and control-flow decisions for installer modes.
5. E2E tests validate install path with failure injection and detailed logs.
close_reason: Implemented scripts/install.sh Unix installer with explicit flags, dry-run, user/system destination handling, idempotent re-run logic, local-fixture test coverage in scripts/e2e_test.sh, and README installer docs

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: UX contract detail: treat installer output as an operator interface. Every failure path must include: cause, what was attempted, and exact copy-paste recovery command. This mirrors dcg's practical diagnostics and is critical for agent-run sessions where logs are often the only artifact.

title: Offline/airgapped bundle mode for installer/update/model assets
description:
## Deliverable
Offline/airgapped bundle mode for installer + updater + model/assets bootstrap.

## Background and rationale
Users in restricted environments still need full batteries-included setup. Bundle mode prevents installer UX collapse when network access is unavailable.

## Scope
- Define bundle artifact format containing binaries, checksums, signatures, and required model/assets manifests.
- Add commands to create and consume bundles deterministically.
- Validate bundle integrity before install/update.
- Ensure update/rollback logic works with bundle-backed sources.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Offline install from bundle supports full bootstrap with no network dependency.
2. Bundle verification enforces integrity exactly as online flow does.
3. Missing/invalid bundle components fail with actionable diagnostics.
4. Unit tests validate bundle manifest parsing and integrity checks.
5. E2E tests simulate airgapped install/update with verbose logs and artifact capture.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Bundle mode supports fully offline install/update/bootstrap for required assets.
2. Bundle integrity checks enforce same trust model as online flow.
3. Bundle build/consume commands are deterministic and documented.
4. Unit tests validate bundle manifests and error handling.
5. E2E validates airgapped workflows with detailed logs.
close_reason: Acceptance met: offline bundle install/update/bootstrap path, deterministic bundle manifest/consume, integrity checks, unit+integration+E2E coverage, and fresh rch validation (cargo test --lib; cargo test --test integration_tests -- update_check_; cargo check --all-targets).

title: Installer bootstrap migration + self-healing for legacy/partial environments
description:
## Deliverable
Bootstrap migration and self-healing for existing configs/integrations across installer and updater runs.

## Background and rationale
Real users have drifted environments. A friendly installer must detect and repair outdated or partially-broken states automatically and safely.

## Scope
- Detect prior installer footprints and stale integration entries.
- Apply deterministic migrations with timestamped backups.
- Repair common broken states (partial installs, stale PATH entries, mismatched integration snippets).
- Expose migration reports in both human summary and JSON output.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Legacy/partial states are migrated or repaired without manual editing in common cases.
2. Every mutation is backup-first and reversible.
3. Migration decisions are logged with clear reason codes.
4. Unit tests cover migration matrix and rollback-on-failure behavior.
5. E2E tests include upgrade-from-older-install scenarios with detailed logs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Legacy and partial installations are detected and migrated/repaired safely.
2. Every migration mutation is backup-first and reversible.
3. Migration reason codes and actions are logged in structured output.
4. Unit tests cover migration matrix and rollback-on-error behavior.
5. E2E validates upgrade-from-older-environment scenarios with detailed logs.

title: Platform/Arch Artifact Resolution Contract for installer/updater
description:
## Deliverable
Deterministic platform/architecture detection and release artifact resolution for installer/update flows.

## Background and rationale
Installer reliability depends on downloading the right artifact every time. dcg resolves target triples and artifact names deterministically.

## Scope
- Detect OS + arch + ABI variants and map to release targets.
- Define canonical artifact naming contract (`tar.xz` on Unix, zip on Windows where applicable).
- Support explicit version pin and channel selection logic.
- Define failure strategy when target is unsupported.

## Acceptance criteria
1. All supported host triples map to exactly one artifact URL.
2. Unsupported hosts fail with actionable guidance.
3. Resolution behavior is shared between installer and updater (no drift).
acceptance_criteria:
1. Supported OS/arch combinations map deterministically to release artifacts with no ambiguity.
2. Unsupported targets fail fast with actionable remediation text.
3. Mapping contract is shared by installer and updater.
4. Unit tests cover all supported mappings and edge/unknown targets.
5. Mapping assumptions are reflected in release contract validation.
close_reason: Implemented deterministic installer/updater target resolver + release artifact contract validation with unit tests

title: Installer Supply-Chain Verification (SHA256 + optional Sigstore)
description:
## Deliverable
Supply-chain integrity pipeline with mandatory checksum verification and optional sigstore/cosign verification.

## Background and rationale
Integrity validation is not optional in curl|bash workflows. It must be default-on and visible.

## Scope
- Download artifact checksum metadata and verify before extraction/install.
- Implement optional signature verification path with graceful degradation messaging if cosign unavailable.
- Add explicit `--no-verify` escape hatch with strong warning text.
- Log/emit structured verification outcomes for diagnostics.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Tampered artifact is always rejected with clear reason.
2. Missing checksum/signature metadata is handled predictably by policy.
3. Verification status is visible in installer summary and machine-readable output.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Checksum verification is default and mandatory unless explicit bypass is requested.
2. Optional signature verification path is implemented with clear degradation messaging.
3. Bypass path is loud, explicit, and logged in structured output.
4. Unit tests cover success/failure/tamper scenarios.
5. E2E tests include integrity failure injection and remediation validation.
close_reason: Implemented SHA256 enforcement + optional/required Sigstore policy contract with structured bypass semantics and tests

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Security decision note: checksum verification is baseline mandatory; signature verification is highly recommended but may be conditionally optional if cosign is absent. Any bypass () must be loud, explicit, and reflected in summary output/log metadata.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Clarification: bypass flag name is --no-verify; using it must emit a high-visibility warning and appear in machine-readable result metadata.

title: From-Source Fallback Install Mode with prerequisite checks
description:
## Deliverable
From-source fallback install mode with prerequisite checks and deterministic build behavior.

## Background and rationale
When release assets are unavailable (airgapped, unsupported target, CI lag), users still need a path to success.

## Scope
- Implement `--from-source` path with toolchain checks and explicit prompts/remediations.
- Support offline/limited-network behavior where feasible.
- Ensure source-build mode respects destination, verification, and summary UX conventions.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Source mode can install from repository tag/commit with reproducible command flow.
2. Missing prerequisites are reported with direct fix commands.
3. Source mode is covered by installer tests.
acceptance_criteria:
1. From-source mode works deterministically with clear prerequisite validation.
2. Source mode preserves destination, verification, and summary behavior parity.
3. Missing prerequisites produce exact fix commands.
4. Unit tests cover source-mode decision logic and prerequisite checks.
5. E2E includes source-install scenario with detailed logs.

title: Post-install automation: PATH, completions, and verification
description:
## Deliverable
Post-install automation for PATH setup, shell completions, and immediate binary verification.

## Background and rationale
The install is not complete until the command actually works in the user's shell with minimal manual edits.

## Scope
- Optional easy-mode PATH mutation for common shells with safe backups/idempotency.
- Install completion scripts (bash/zsh/fish) automatically when requested.
- Run post-install sanity command (`sbh --version`, optional health check) and report status.

## Acceptance criteria
1. New users can run `sbh` in a new shell without manual PATH surgery when easy mode is enabled.
2. Completion install does not clobber unrelated shell config.
3. Verification failures provide exact next-step commands.
acceptance_criteria:
1. PATH/completion automation is idempotent and backup-safe across supported shells.
2. Post-install verification confirms binary usability and reports actionable remediation on failure.
3. Automation outcomes are visible in both human summary and structured logs.
4. Unit tests cover shell profile mutation edge cases.
5. E2E validates end-to-end behavior in clean shell environments.

title: Automatic AI tool integration bootstrap with backup-first safety
description:
## Deliverable
Automatic integration bootstrap for AI coding toolchains (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, and related hook-capable CLIs) with backup-first semantics.

## Background and rationale
dcg succeeds partly because it wires itself into user workflows immediately. We need similar convenience while preserving safety and reversibility.

## Scope
- Detect known tool config files and hook registries.
- Inject/update sbh integration entries idempotently.
- Create timestamped backups before mutation and print restore guidance.
- Support `--no-configure` mode for manual-only users.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Re-running bootstrap does not duplicate hooks or corrupt config files.
2. Backups are always created and discoverable.
3. Installer summary reports per-tool status (configured/skipped/failed + reason).
acceptance_criteria:
1. Integration bootstrap updates known tool configs idempotently with backup-first behavior.
2. Re-runs never duplicate entries or corrupt configuration files.
3. Per-tool status is surfaced in summary and JSON diagnostics.
4. Unit tests cover merge/update and rollback-on-failure logic.
5. E2E validates multi-tool bootstrap with detailed logs and artifacted backups.

title: sbh update command orchestration (check/apply/pin/system-user)
description:
## Deliverable
A first-class `sbh update` command that orchestrates install/update flows with explicit policy controls.

## Background and rationale
Users need an in-product update command, not just docs telling them to re-run installer scripts.

## Scope
- Implement `sbh update` command surface: check-only, install target version, force refresh, system/user destination controls.
- Reuse installer logic for download/verify/install to avoid split behavior.
- Support dry-run and machine-readable output.

## Acceptance criteria
1. `sbh update --check` is fast and safe for cron/agent usage.
2. Updating to latest and pinned versions works across supported platforms.
3. Update output clearly states old/new version and any required follow-up actions.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `sbh update` supports check/apply/pin/system-user controls with deterministic behavior.
2. Update flow reuses installer integrity and artifact resolution logic (no drift).
3. Update outcomes include old/new version and required follow-up actions.
4. Unit tests cover command options, policy decisions, and error mapping.
5. E2E covers update success/failure and rollback handoff with detailed logs.
close_reason: Implemented sbh update command: UpdateArgs with --check/--version/--force/--system/--user/--no-verify/--dry-run, module-based orchestration in cli/update.rs with 11 unit tests (version resolution, report formatting, step tracking), 3 bin tests (CLI parsing, defaults, conflict validation). Shares artifact resolution and verification with installer via resolve_updater_artifact_contract/verify_artifact_supply_chain. Full pipeline: detect platform → resolve contract → check version → download via curl → SHA-256 verify → extract tar.xz → backup+install with rollback. Human and JSON output modes.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Update orchestration note: updater must not fork behavior from installer. Shared code paths are required so install/update verification and artifact resolution semantics cannot drift over time.

title: Update cache + background notice policy + opt-out controls
description:
## Deliverable
Updater version-intelligence cache and background notification policy with clear opt-out controls.

## Background and rationale
Fast startup matters; repeated network calls for every command do not scale. dcg uses cached checks plus optional background refresh.

## Scope
- Add local cache for update metadata with TTL.
- Implement lightweight background refresh policy where appropriate.
- Support config/env opt-out for update checks and notices.

## Acceptance criteria
1. Update check latency stays low with warm cache.
2. Cache expiry/refresh semantics are deterministic and test-covered.
3. Users/agents can disable notices globally in config or env.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Update metadata caching reduces check latency while preserving correctness.
2. TTL and refresh behavior are deterministic and configurable.
3. Opt-out controls work via config/env without side effects.
4. Unit tests cover cache lifecycle and opt-out logic.
5. E2E includes stale-cache and offline-check scenarios with detailed logs.

title: sbh config and sbh ballast subcommands
description:
## Deliverable
Config management and ballast management subcommands.

## Technical Approach
### sbh config
```bash
sbh config show              # Show current effective config (merged layers)
sbh config path              # Show config file path
sbh config validate          # Validate config file
sbh config set <key> <value> # Set a config value
sbh config reset             # Reset to defaults
sbh config diff              # Show diff between file and defaults
```

### sbh ballast
```bash
sbh ballast status           # Show ballast inventory
sbh ballast provision        # Create ballast files (idempotent)
sbh ballast release N        # Manually release N files
sbh ballast replenish        # Manually replenish released files
sbh ballast verify           # Check integrity of all files
```

### Implementation
These commands are relatively straightforward wrappers around the config system and ballast manager. The key value is providing CLI access to internals for debugging and manual intervention.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All config subcommands work correctly
- Config validation produces helpful errors
- Ballast status shows accurate inventory
- Ballast verify checks file integrity
- Manual release/replenish work correctly
- JSON output available for all subcommands
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests cover config key parsing, validation error messaging, and ballast command argument handling. 2. Integration tests validate effective-config layering and ballast manager command effects. 3. E2E scripts cover config show/set/validate/reset/diff and ballast status/provision/release/replenish/verify flows. 4. JSON and human outputs remain consistent and parseable under success and failure paths. 5. Detailed command logs include config source precedence, ballast inventory deltas, and safety guard decisions.

title: Daemon self-monitoring and health reporting
description:
## Deliverable
The daemon monitors its own health: memory usage, thread status, scan latency, and reports this via sd_notify STATUS and the status command.

## Technical Approach
### Self-Monitoring Metrics
```rust
pub struct DaemonHealth {
    pub uptime: Duration,
    pub memory_rss_bytes: u64,      // /proc/self/status on Linux
    pub scan_count: u64,
    pub avg_scan_duration: Duration,
    pub last_scan_at: Instant,
    pub deletions_total: u64,
    pub bytes_freed_total: u64,
    pub errors_total: u64,
    pub thread_status: Vec<ThreadStatus>,
    pub last_pressure_level: PressureLevel,
}

pub enum ThreadStatus {
    Running { name: String, last_heartbeat: Instant },
    Stalled { name: String, stalled_since: Instant },
    Dead { name: String, died_at: Instant, error: String },
}
```

### Thread Heartbeats
Each worker thread periodically writes to an AtomicU64 timestamp. The self-monitor checks these:
- If heartbeat is > 60s stale → thread is stalled
- Log warning and attempt restart

### Health Report for sd_notify
```
STATUS=Monitoring 4 mounts | GREEN 23.4% free on /data | 312 deletions (467 GB freed) | RSS 42 MB
```

### Stale State File
Write a JSON state file (/var/lib/sbh/state.json) periodically:
- Allows `sbh status` to read daemon state even when not connecting directly
- Includes last scan time, current pressure, recent activity summary
- Written every 30 seconds

## Acceptance Criteria
- Memory usage tracked and bounded (< 256MB, alert if exceeded)
- Thread stalls detected within 60 seconds
- sd_notify STATUS updated every 30 seconds
- State file written for status command
- Self-monitoring overhead < 1% CPU
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests cover heartbeat staleness detection, memory parsing, and state serialization correctness. 2. Integration tests validate self-monitor interaction with daemon threads and sd_notify updates. 3. E2E scenarios validate stalled-thread detection, alert emission, and recovery/restart handling. 4. Health state persists accurately across daemon restarts and degraded operation. 5. Detailed health logs include per-thread heartbeat age, memory trends, status payloads, and alert reason codes.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone

title: sbh install / sbh uninstall commands
description:
## Deliverable
Commands to install and uninstall sbh as a system service, including ballast provisioning.

## Technical Approach
### sbh install
```bash
sbh install [OPTIONS]

Options:
  --systemd          Install as systemd service (Linux, default on Linux)
  --launchd          Install as launchd agent (macOS, default on macOS)
  --user             Install as user-level service (no root required)
  --ballast-count N  Number of ballast files to create (default: 10)
  --ballast-size N   Size of each ballast file in MB (default: 1024)
  --ballast-path P   Location for ballast files
  --dry-run          Show what would be done without doing it
```

### Installation Steps
1. Detect platform (Linux → systemd, macOS → launchd)
2. Create data directory (/var/lib/sbh/ or equivalent)
3. Write default config file
4. Provision ballast files (with progress bar)
5. Create SQLite database
6. Generate and install service file
7. Enable and start service
8. Verify service is running
9. Print summary

### sbh uninstall
```bash
sbh uninstall [OPTIONS]

Options:
  --keep-data        Keep data directory and logs
  --keep-ballast     Keep ballast files (don't reclaim space)
  --force            Don't ask for confirmation
```

### Uninstall Steps
1. Stop service
2. Disable service
3. Remove service file
4. Optionally remove data directory (with confirmation)
5. Optionally remove ballast files (with confirmation)
6. Print summary

### Progress Reporting
Ballast provisioning (writing 10GB) takes time. Show progress:
```
Installing sbh v0.1.0...
  Creating data directory... done
  Writing default config... done
  Provisioning ballast files:
    [████████████████░░░░] 8/10 (8.0 GB) - SBH_BALLAST_FILE_00008.dat
  Creating activity database... done
  Installing systemd service... done
  Starting service... done

✓ sbh installed successfully
  Service: sbh.service (running)
  Config:  /etc/sbh/config.toml
  Data:    /var/lib/sbh/
  Ballast: 10 files × 1 GB = 10 GB reclaimable
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Install works on Linux (systemd) and macOS (launchd)
- Idempotent: running install twice doesn't break anything
- Uninstall cleanly removes all components
- Progress bar during ballast provisioning
- --dry-run shows plan without executing
- --user mode works without root
- Verification step confirms service is actually running
- Integration test: install → verify → uninstall → verify clean
acceptance_criteria:
1. `sbh install` and `sbh uninstall` remain idempotent and deterministic across Linux/macOS paths.
2. Install command integrates wizard/auto flow, post-install automation, integration bootstrap, and structured diagnostics.
3. Uninstall command integrates safe cleanup modes and reversible teardown behavior.
4. Unit tests validate install/uninstall option handling and safety decisions.
5. E2E validates install -> verify -> update -> rollback -> uninstall with detailed logs.

  1. Detect all mounted filesystems and show them in a table

This replaces the current 'install with defaults' approach with a guided experience. For headless: sbh install --auto uses all defaults without prompting.

Install command detects platform: Linux -> systemd (bd-26g, required dep), macOS -> launchd (bd-3i3, wired in when available). If bd-3i3 is not yet implemented, 'sbh install' on macOS returns: 'macOS service installation not yet available. Run sbh daemon in foreground instead.'

title: Cross-platform filesystem stats collector
description:
## Deliverable
Efficient filesystem statistics collection that works across all target platforms, with caching and rate limiting.

## Technical Approach
### FsStatsCollector
Wraps the PAL's fs_stats() with intelligent caching and batching:

```rust
pub struct FsStatsCollector {
    platform: Arc<dyn Platform>,
    cache: HashMap<PathBuf, CachedStats>,
    cache_ttl: Duration,  // default: 1 second
}

struct CachedStats {
    stats: FsStats,
    collected_at: Instant,
}
```

### Batch Collection
When monitoring multiple paths, group by mount point to avoid redundant statvfs calls:
- /data/projects/foo and /data/projects/bar → single statvfs for /data mount
- /tmp/build1 and /tmp/build2 → single statvfs for /tmp mount

### Rate Limiting
statvfs is a syscall; avoid calling it too frequently:
- Cache results for configurable TTL (default 1s)
- Under high pressure, reduce TTL to 100ms
- During normal operation, 5s TTL is fine

### Zero-Allocation Hot Path
The stats check in the monitoring loop should not allocate:
- Pre-allocate the stats buffer
- Reuse PathBuf instances
- Use stack-allocated arrays for mount point looking

## Design Rationale
statvfs is fast (~1μs on Linux) but calling it hundreds of times per second across many mount points adds up. Caching by mount point deduplicates the work. The cache TTL adapts to pressure level: more frequent checks when storage is under pressure.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Correctly reports free/total/available for all filesystems
- Caching prevents redundant syscalls (verifiable by counting calls)
- Mount point deduplication works (paths on same mount = single call)
- Thread-safe for concurrent access
- Unit tests with MockPlatform
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests validate cache TTL behavior, mount deduplication, and concurrency safety. 2. Integration tests verify PAL-backed stats correctness across multiple mount configurations. 3. E2E scenarios exercise mixed-path monitoring and confirm no redundant stats collection regressions. 4. Performance bounds are met under high-frequency polling workloads. 5. Detailed collector logs include mount key, cache hit/miss, syscall timing, and normalization decisions.
close_reason: Filesystem stats collector implemented in monitor/fs_stats.rs (274 lines): FsStatsCollector with cache-aware collection, per-mount caching with TTL, mount point deduplication via collect_many(), cache expiry pruning, unit tests for dedup and cache hits.

title: E2E test script with detailed logging
description:
## Deliverable
Shell-based end-to-end test script suite that exercises CLI, daemon, policy modes, and recovery paths with detailed structured logs.

## Scope Expansion (Plan-Space Revision)
E2E scripts must cover:
- core CLI commands and JSON output validation
- scan/clean/ballast lifecycles
- predictive pipeline + fallback-safe transitions
- emergency zero-write mode
- project protection markers
- process attribution output paths
- status/dashboard/stats visibility checks
- decision-plane shadow/canary trace assertions

## Logging Requirements
Each test case logs:
- command executed
- expected vs actual exit status
- expected vs actual output constraints
- timing and environment metadata
- trace IDs and fallback reason codes when present

Output artifacts:
- machine-readable JSON summary
- per-test logs
- compact human summary for CI console

## Acceptance Criteria
- comprehensive command and policy-mode coverage
- deterministic pass/fail behavior
- explicit cleanup on success/failure paths
- integrated into CI e2e stage
- runtime budget and flaky-test guardrails documented
acceptance_criteria: 1. E2E script suite covers normal, degraded, and emergency user workflows without feature gaps. 2. Scenarios validate CLI UX plus daemon behavior and decision-plane transitions where applicable. 3. Every scenario emits verbose structured logs with timestamps, trace IDs, and assertion context. 4. Scripts produce deterministic exit codes and machine-readable summary artifacts for CI. 5. Flake diagnostics are captured with retry metadata and first-failure preservation.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Additional E2E test cases needed:

23. **Signal handling SIGHUP**: Start daemon → send SIGHUP → verify config reload logged → verify daemon still running
24. **Signal handling SIGUSR1**: Start daemon → send SIGUSR1 → verify immediate scan triggered
25. **Concurrent CLI access**: Start daemon → run 'sbh status' while daemon is running → verify no deadlock or corruption
26. **Ballast concurrent access**: Start daemon → run 'sbh ballast release 1' while daemon monitors → verify file locking works (no race condition)
27. **Scoring determinism**: Run 'sbh scan --json' twice on identical directory → verify JSON output is byte-identical
28. **JSONL tailing**: Start daemon → tail -f JSONL file → trigger scan → verify events appear in real-time with complete JSON lines (no partial writes)
29. **Large directory tree**: Create 10,000+ files in nested dirs → 'sbh scan' completes in < 10s
30. **Exit codes**: Verify all documented exit codes (0, 1, 2, 3, 4) match their descriptions

Also: Each test should capture TIMING information (start/end timestamps) and log it, so we can detect performance regressions across runs.

title: Emergency zero-write recovery mode (sbh emergency)
description:
## Deliverable
A zero-write emergency mode that works when the disk is already critically full — the exact moment users need sbh most.

## The Problem
Users install sbh PRECISELY when their disk is full. But the normal startup sequence requires creating directories, writing config, creating SQLite database, provisioning ballast files. ALL of these fail on a full disk. sbh can't help in the exact scenario it exists for.

## The Solution: sbh emergency [paths...]

A completely self-contained mode that:
- Performs ZERO disk writes (no database, no JSONL, no config file)
- Scans specified paths immediately using built-in patterns
- Scores and ranks all candidates using default scoring weights
- Shows interactive deletion list (like sbh clean but stateless)
- All output to stdout/stderr only
- Works with zero configuration — pure binary execution
- Supports automation: sbh emergency --yes --target-free 10 /data

### CLI Interface
  sbh emergency                        # Scan common paths (/data, /tmp, /home)
  sbh emergency /data/projects         # Scan specific path
  sbh emergency --yes --target-free 10 # Auto-delete until 10% free
  sbh emergency --top 20               # Show top 20 candidates only
  sbh emergency --min-score 0.7        # Only high-confidence candidates
  sbh emergency --dry-run              # Just show what would be deleted

### Implementation
Emergency mode creates no objects requiring persistence:
- ScoringEngine with default weights (hardcoded, no config file read)
- DirectoryWalker configured for the specified paths
- PatternRegistry with built-in patterns only (no custom patterns from config)
- No ActivityLogger — all output goes to stderr for logging, stdout for JSON
- No BallastManager, No EWMA, No PID controller — crisis mode, delete by score alone
- No /proc/*/fd open-file checks in emergency (too slow when disk is thrashing)

### Output Format
  sbh emergency — ZERO-WRITE RECOVERY MODE
  Warning: /data is 99.2% used (8.1 GB free / 1.8 TB total)
  Scanning /data/projects... found 47 candidates (182.3 GB reclaimable)
  [interactive deletion list with scores, sizes, ages]
  Delete items? [y/N/a(ll)/s(kip)/q(uit)]

### Post-Recovery Guidance
After freeing space, print: "Emergency cleanup complete: freed 87.3 GB. Next: run sbh install for ongoing protection."

### Safety
- Hard vetoes still enforced: .git directories, system paths, files < 30 min old
- Requires user confirmation unless --yes flag
- Clear EMERGENCY MODE banner

## Design Rationale
This is the single most impactful UX improvement. Every user's first interaction with sbh will likely be "my disk is full, help!" The implementation reuses scoring engine + walker + pattern registry WITHOUT the persistence layer. It is the pure-computation core of sbh exposed directly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Works on a 99%+ full disk (zero disk writes verified via strace)
- Correctly finds and scores build artifacts using built-in patterns
- Interactive confirmation before deletion
- --yes flag enables automated operation
- --target-free stops cleanup when target free% is reached
- --dry-run shows candidates without deleting
- Post-recovery message suggests sbh install
- All hard vetoes still enforced
- Works with zero configuration (no config file needed)
- Exit codes: 0=success, 1=no candidates, 2=user cancelled
- Unit test: scoring without persistence layer
- Integration test: create artifacts -> emergency scan -> delete -> verify
- E2E test: verify zero disk writes

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Emergency mode interaction with protection system (bd-3qm): Emergency mode depends on walker (bd-1w9) which depends on protection (bd-3qm). But emergency mode has NO config file. The protection system handles this via marker-only mode (see bd-3qm comment). Emergency mode WILL honor .sbh-protect marker files (they are on-disk, zero-config). Emergency mode WILL NOT honor config-level glob patterns (scanner.protected_paths). This is documented and acceptable — marker files are the user's explicit intent, while config patterns may be stale or from a different context. Emergency mode output should note: 'Config-level protections are not active in emergency mode. Only .sbh-protect marker files are honored.'

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Emergency mode needs deletion capability. Added bd-1hh (deletion executor) as dependency. Emergency mode creates a DeletionExecutor with dry_run=false (unless --dry-run flag) but skips the circuit breaker (emergency = delete everything above score threshold). The executor's pre-flight safety checks (re-verify exists, check .git, verify permissions) still apply. Emergency mode uses a simplified deletion flow: score -> rank -> present to user -> delete confirmed items. No batch-then-remeasure loop needed since there is no PID target — just delete what the user confirms.

title: CLI framework with clap derive and subcommands
description:
## Deliverable
The main CLI argument parser using clap derive, defining all subcommands and their options.

## Technical Approach
### CLI Structure
```
sbh [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  daemon     Run the monitoring daemon (foreground or background)
  install    Install sbh as a system service
  uninstall  Remove sbh system service
  status     Show current system status and pressure levels
  stats      Show activity statistics and metrics
  scan       Run a manual scan for build artifacts
  clean      Manually clean build artifacts (with confirmation)
  ballast    Manage ballast files
  config     View/edit configuration
  version    Show version and build info
  help       Show help

Options:
  --config <PATH>   Override config file path
  --json            Output as JSON (for agent consumption)
  --no-color        Disable colored output
  --verbose         Increase log verbosity
  --quiet           Quiet mode (errors only)
```

### Clap Derive Setup
```rust
#[derive(Parser)]
#[command(name = "sbh", about = "Storage Ballast Helper - Disk Space Guardian")]
#[command(version, long_about = None)]
pub struct Cli {
    #[command(subcommand)]
    pub command: Command,
    
    #[arg(long, global = true)]
    pub config: Option<PathBuf>,
    
    #[arg(long, global = true)]
    pub json: bool,
    
    #[arg(long, global = true)]
    pub no_color: bool,
    
    #[arg(short, long, global = true)]
    pub verbose: bool,
    
    #[arg(short, long, global = true)]
    pub quiet: bool,
}

#[derive(Subcommand)]
pub enum Command {
    Daemon(DaemonArgs),
    Install(InstallArgs),
    Uninstall(UninstallArgs),
    Status(StatusArgs),
    Stats(StatsArgs),
    Scan(ScanArgs),
    Clean(CleanArgs),
    Ballast(BallastArgs),
    Config(ConfigArgs),
}
```

### Dual Output Mode
Following dcg's pattern:
- Human mode: colored terminal output with tables and progress indicators
- JSON mode: structured JSON to stdout for agent consumption
- Auto-detect based on TTY + --json flag + SBH_OUTPUT_FORMAT env var

### Exit Codes
```
0 → Success
1 → User error (bad arguments, invalid config)
2 → Runtime error (permission denied, disk error)
3 → Internal error (bug)
4 → Partial success (some items cleaned, some failed)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All subcommands parse correctly
- --help produces clear, helpful output
- --version shows version + build metadata
- --json flag works globally
- --no-color respected
- Exit codes match specification
- Shell completions generatable (bash, zsh, fish)
- Unit tests for argument parsing edge cases
close_reason: Completed: clap-derive CLI framework with global flags, expanded subcommands, JSON/human output mode, completions generation, parser tests, and updated integration/e2e scaffolding.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: New subcommands that must be wired into the CLI framework: (1) sbh emergency [paths...] (bd-2qa) — zero-write recovery mode. (2) sbh protect <path> / sbh unprotect <path> / sbh protect --list (bd-3qm) — project protection. (3) sbh tune / sbh tune --apply (bd-7vl) — auto-tuning recommendations. (4) sbh check [path] (bd-g0c) — pre-build disk check. (5) sbh blame (bd-395) — agent/process attribution. (6) sbh dashboard (bd-3s5) — live TUI dashboard. All are gated behind their respective beads as dependencies, but bd-2rq must define the clap subcommand enum variants for all of them upfront so the CLI structure is complete.

title: Signal handling, graceful shutdown, and watchdog heartbeat
description:
## Deliverable
Robust signal handling for the daemon: SIGTERM/SIGINT for graceful shutdown, SIGHUP for config reload, plus watchdog heartbeat for systemd integration.

## Technical Approach
### Signal Handler
```rust
pub struct SignalHandler {
    shutdown_flag: Arc<AtomicBool>,
    reload_flag: Arc<AtomicBool>,
}
```

Using signal-hook crate for safe signal handling:
- SIGTERM/SIGINT → set shutdown_flag, wake monitoring loop
- SIGHUP → set reload_flag (config reload)
- SIGUSR1 → trigger immediate scan and report

### Graceful Shutdown Sequence
1. Set shutdown_flag (all threads check this)
2. Stop accepting new scan requests
3. Wait up to 30 seconds for in-progress operations to complete
4. Flush all logger buffers
5. Close database connections
6. Log shutdown event with stats summary
7. Exit 0

### Config Reload (SIGHUP)
1. Re-read config file
2. Validate new config
3. If valid: apply changes, log what changed
4. If invalid: log error, keep running with old config

### Watchdog Heartbeat (systemd)
Every 30 seconds (half of WatchdogSec=60), notify systemd:
- sd_notify(WATCHDOG=1)
- Include STATUS=... with current state

If the monitoring loop is stuck (deadlock, infinite loop), the watchdog timeout fires and systemd restarts sbh.

### Platform Considerations
- Linux: signal-hook + sd_notify
- macOS: signal-hook only (no sd_notify equivalent)
- Windows: SetConsoleCtrlHandler for Ctrl-C

## Acceptance Criteria
- SIGTERM/SIGINT triggers graceful shutdown within 30s
- SIGHUP reloads config without restart
- SIGUSR1 triggers immediate scan
- Watchdog heartbeat keeps systemd happy
- Invalid config on reload doesn't crash the daemon
- All pending operations complete before shutdown
- Unit tests for signal flag handling
- Integration test: send signals → verify correct behavior
close_reason: done

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: This bead requires the signal-hook crate (already added to bd-1kn's dependency list during review). The nix crate's signal module may also be useful for POSIX signal manipulation (sigprocmask, etc.), which is already a dependency. signal-hook provides safe, higher-level abstractions over raw signal handling that prevent common footguns (e.g., calling non-signal-safe functions in signal handlers).

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: MINOR: SIGHUP reload clarification — on SIGHUP, the daemon should: (1) Re-read config file from disk. (2) Update in-memory config (pressure thresholds, PID gains, scan intervals, scoring weights). (3) Re-discover special locations. (4) Log 'Configuration reloaded' event. It should NOT: restart threads, re-initialize SQLite, re-provision ballast (those require full restart). Implementation: signal_hook::iterator::Signals in the main loop checks for SIGHUP each cycle.

title: Circuit breaker should break instead of cooldown-and-retry
description: In deletion.rs when circuit breaker trips it sleeps 30s then resets and continues cycling indefinitely. Should break out of loop and let daemon next scan cycle retry.

title: Predictive action pipeline with early warning alerts
description:
## Deliverable
Transform sbh from reactive (acts after thresholds are crossed) to predictive (acts before thresholds are crossed based on EWMA forecasts). Includes early warning alerts.

## The Problem
The EWMA estimator (bd-x1k) already computes time_to_exhaustion and acceleration. The PID controller (bd-3po) already determines pressure levels. But the main loop (bd-48o) only acts on CURRENT pressure levels — it waits for thresholds to actually be crossed before doing anything.

This means: if 10 agents simultaneously start cargo builds and disk consumption jumps from 0 MB/s to 500 MB/s, sbh won't start cleaning until the disk actually hits the threshold (say 15% free). By then, with 500 MB/s consumption, the disk may be at 5% within 3 minutes. The reactive approach leaves very little time for cleanup.

## The Solution: Predictive Pre-emptive Action

### Predictive Action Policy
New module that maps EWMA predictions to pre-emptive actions:

```rust
pub struct PredictiveActionPolicy {
    config: PredictiveConfig,
}

pub struct PredictiveConfig {
    pub enabled: bool,                    // default: true
    pub action_horizon_minutes: f64,      // default: 30.0
    pub warning_horizon_minutes: f64,     // default: 60.0  
    pub min_confidence: f64,              // default: 0.7
    pub min_samples: u64,                 // default: 5
}

pub enum PredictiveAction {
    /// No predicted issue within horizon
    Clear,
    /// Disk will be full within warning horizon — log and increase monitoring
    EarlyWarning {
        mount: PathBuf,
        minutes_remaining: f64,
        confidence: f64,
    },
    /// Disk will be full within action horizon — start pre-emptive cleanup
    PreemptiveCleanup {
        mount: PathBuf,
        minutes_remaining: f64,
        confidence: f64,
        recommended_free_target_pct: f64,
    },
    /// Imminent disk full (< 5 min) — emergency measures
    ImminentDanger {
        mount: PathBuf,
        minutes_remaining: f64,
    },
}

impl PredictiveActionPolicy {
    pub fn evaluate(&self, estimate: &RateEstimate, current_free_pct: f64) -> PredictiveAction;
}
```

### Timeline-Based Response
```
time_to_exhaustion  │  Action
> 60 min            │  Clear — no action
30-60 min           │  EarlyWarning — log WARNING, increase scan frequency to 10s
10-30 min           │  PreemptiveCleanup — start scanning and deleting high-score artifacts
5-10 min            │  PreemptiveCleanup (aggressive) — lower score threshold, delete more
< 5 min             │  ImminentDanger — release ballast + aggressive cleanup
< 2 min             │  ImminentDanger (critical) — release ALL ballast + emergency cleanup
```

### Key Innovation: Acting BEFORE Thresholds
The current system: "disk is 92% full -> pressure is Orange -> start cleanup"
The predictive system: "disk is 78% full but filling at 500 MB/s -> predicted 95% in 28 min -> start gentle cleanup NOW while disk is still healthy"

This means:
1. Cleanup has MORE time (starting 30 min early vs 3 min before critical)
2. Cleanup can be GENTLER (lower urgency, higher score threshold)
3. Less chance of user-impacting aggressive deletion
4. Disk never actually enters critical state

### Early Warning Alerts
When predictions cross thresholds, emit structured events:

```json
{"ts":"2026-02-14T16:30:00Z","event":"predictive_warning","mount":"/data","minutes_remaining":42.3,"confidence":0.85,"rate_mbps":487.2,"trend":"accelerating","message":"At current rate, /data will be full in ~42 minutes"}
```

These events go to:
1. Activity log (SQLite + JSONL)
2. systemd journal (sd_notify STATUS update)
3. Optional: notification channel (if notification system bead is implemented)

### Confidence Gating
Pre-emptive action is ONLY taken when:
- EWMA has >= min_samples (default 5) — need enough data
- Prediction confidence >= min_confidence (default 0.7) — high-variance predictions are unreliable
- Trend is Accelerating or Stable (not Recovering or Decelerating)
- Current free% is actually declining (not just noisy measurement)

This prevents false alarms from brief spikes (e.g., one large file creation that immediately finishes).

### Configuration
```toml
[pressure.prediction]
enabled = true
action_horizon_minutes = 30
warning_horizon_minutes = 60
min_confidence = 0.7
min_samples = 5
```

## Design Rationale
This transforms sbh from reactive to proactive. The EWMA data already exists — this connects it to the action pipeline earlier. A 30-minute prediction window means sbh starts gentle cleanup well before an emergency. Users never see a critical alert because the situation was handled proactively. This is THE key differentiator from a dumb cron job running find -delete.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Pre-emptive cleanup starts when predicted exhaustion < action_horizon
- Cleanup intensity proportional to time remaining (not just current pressure)
- Confidence gating prevents false positives
- Early warning events logged with structured data
- Does NOT act on low-confidence predictions
- Does NOT act when trend is Recovering/Decelerating
- Integrates cleanly with PID controller (advisory, not replacement)
- Configuration allows tuning horizons and confidence thresholds
- Unit tests: prediction to action mapping with synthetic scenarios
- Test: 500 MB/s consumption rate at 80% full -> pre-emptive cleanup starts
- Test: brief spike (1 min) -> no pre-emptive action (insufficient samples)
- Test: steady recovery after cleanup -> pre-emptive actions stop
- Integration test: full pipeline with predictive + reactive working together
close_reason: Fully implemented by another agent: PredictiveActionPolicy, PredictiveConfig, PredictiveAction enum, 22+ tests in src/monitor/predictive.rs. Config integrated into PressureConfig.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: MINOR: Notification wiring clarification — when predictive action triggers an EarlyWarning or PreemptiveCleanup, it should emit a structured event that the notification system (bd-112) can pick up IF notifications are configured. The predictive module itself does NOT depend on the notification system. Instead, it emits events to the same ActivityEvent channel that all threads use. The notification system subscribes to that channel and filters by event type and min_level.
