title: JSONL append-only structured logger
description:
## Deliverable
Append-only JSONL (JSON Lines) writer that provides a second, independent log of all sbh activity, optimized for agent consumption and grep-ability.

## Technical Approach
### JSONL Format
Each line is a self-contained JSON object:
```json
{"ts":"2026-02-14T16:30:00Z","event":"artifact_delete","path":"/data/projects/foo/.target_opus","size":3456789012,"score":0.87,"factors":{"location":0.85,"name":0.90,"age":0.95,"size":0.80,"structure":0.85},"pressure":"orange","free_pct":8.3,"duration_ms":145,"ok":true}
{"ts":"2026-02-14T16:30:01Z","event":"ballast_release","path":"/var/lib/sbh/ballast/SBH_BALLAST_FILE_00010.dat","size":1073741824,"pressure":"red","free_pct":4.2,"ok":true}
{"ts":"2026-02-14T16:30:02Z","event":"pressure_change","level":"red->orange","free_pct":12.1,"rate_bps":-50000000}
```

### Writer Implementation
```rust
pub struct JsonlWriter {
    file: BufWriter<File>,
    path: PathBuf,
    bytes_written: u64,
    max_size: u64,           // rotation threshold
    rotation_count: u32,
}
```

### Write Strategy
- Buffer writes in memory (BufWriter with 64KB buffer)
- Flush after every logical event (not every line - batch related events)
- fsync every 10 seconds or on pressure change (not every write - too expensive)
- Each line is atomic: write the full line then newline in one write() call

### Rotation
When file exceeds max_size_mb:
- Rename current file to sbh.jsonl.1 (shift existing rotations)
- Open new sbh.jsonl
- Keep up to 5 rotated files (configurable)

### Agent-Friendly Design
Agents can:
- `tail -f /var/lib/sbh/sbh.jsonl` for live monitoring
- `grep "artifact_delete" sbh.jsonl | jq .` for filtering
- `jq -s '[.[] | select(.event == "artifact_delete")] | length' sbh.jsonl` for counts
- Parse each line independently (no multi-line records)

### Why Both SQLite AND JSONL?
1. SQLite: efficient queries, aggregation, statistics, joins
2. JSONL: portable, grep-able, agent-friendly, survives SQLite corruption
3. JSONL works even when disk is critically full (can be on a different filesystem)
4. JSONL is the "black box recorder" - if everything else fails, this survives

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each line is valid JSON
- Lines are atomic (no partial writes visible to readers)
- Rotation works correctly without data loss
- Agent can tail -f and get real-time events
- All event types have consistent field names
- Timestamps in ISO 8601 UTC
- File rotation handles edge cases (rename failures, etc.)
- Unit tests for writing, rotation, and parsing
close_reason: JSONL logger implemented in src/logger/jsonl.rs: LogEntry/EventType/Severity types, JsonlWriter with BufWriter (64KB), atomic write_all lines, file rotation with configurable max_size/rotated_files, 4-level fallback chain (file→fallback→stderr→discard), fsync batching, try_recover, 6 passing tests.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Strengthened fallback chain specification:

The JSONL writer must implement this explicit degradation ladder:

1. NORMAL: BufWriter → file (64KB buffer, fsync every 10s)
2. DEGRADED-1: If file write fails (disk full), try reopening file on a DIFFERENT filesystem. Config should allow jsonl_fallback_path (e.g., /dev/shm/sbh.jsonl for RAM-backed fallback)
3. DEGRADED-2: If fallback path also fails, write to stderr with [SBH-JSONL] prefix so journald/syslog captures events
4. DEGRADED-3: If stderr write fails (shouldn't happen but defensive), discard events silently — the daemon MUST NOT crash for logging failures

Each degradation level transition must be logged at the NEXT available level.

Also add: Write JSONL lines using write_all() for a complete buffer (line + newline assembled first), NOT separate write() calls for line and newline. This prevents interleaved partial lines when the file is being tailed by another process.

title: Agent/process disk usage attribution (sbh blame)
description:
## Deliverable
Track and display which running processes or agents are consuming the most disk space in build artifacts. sbh status --blame and sbh blame commands.

## Usage
  sbh blame                    # Show disk usage by agent/process
  sbh blame --json             # Machine-readable output

## Output
```
Disk Usage by Agent/Process:

  Agent/Process          │ Build Dirs │ Total Size │ Oldest   │ Newest
  claude-code (PID 1234) │    23      │   45.2 GB  │ 12h ago  │ 15m ago
  codex-cli (PID 5678)   │     8      │   12.1 GB  │  4h ago  │ 30m ago
  pi-agent (PID 9012)    │    14      │   28.7 GB  │  8h ago  │  1h ago
  (orphaned)             │    31      │   67.4 GB  │  3d ago  │  6h ago

  Total: 76 build dirs, 153.4 GB
  Orphaned dirs (no running process) are the safest to clean.
```

## Implementation
1. Scan /proc/*/cwd to find which processes have CWD in project directories
2. Correlate discovered artifacts with the project directory of running processes
3. "Orphaned" artifacts belong to projects where no process currently has CWD
4. Use process binary name (from /proc/*/comm) as agent identifier
5. Cache /proc scan results (refreshed every 30s or on demand)

## Design Rationale
Helps users understand which agents are the worst disk offenders. "Orphaned" artifacts (no running process) are the safest to clean and can be prioritized. This is a diagnostic tool that makes sbh's decisions more transparent.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Correctly attributes artifacts to running processes
- Orphaned artifacts identified and highlighted
- Works when many agents run simultaneously
- Performance: <2s even with 100+ processes and 10K+ artifacts
- JSON output for agent consumption
- Handles /proc access gracefully (permission denied on some PIDs)
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests cover PID-to-path attribution, orphan detection, and aggregation correctness. 2. Integration tests validate /proc scanning behavior with permission denials and stale process state. 3. E2E scenarios validate blame output across concurrent agent workloads and orphan cleanup opportunities. 4. JSON output schema remains stable for agent consumption. 5. Detailed attribution logs include process identity, matched directories, bytes totals, and unresolved-candidate reasons.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: sbh blame is Linux-specific (/proc/*/cwd, /proc/*/comm). Added bd-sth (PAL) as dependency. Implementation: On Linux, use /proc scanning. On macOS, use lsof -c or similar. On other platforms, return an error 'blame command requires Linux or macOS' with exit code 3. The PAL layer provides platform_supports_process_attribution() -> bool and get_process_cwd(pid) -> Option<PathBuf>.

title: Fix pre-existing integration test: pressure_escalation_through_levels expects Yellow but gets Orange
Statistics and metrics query engine with time-window aggregation
description:
## Deliverable
Query engine that provides statistics about sbh's recent behavior across multiple time windows, enabling agents and users to understand what sbh has been doing.

## Technical Approach
### Time Windows
Report statistics for: 10 minutes, 30 minutes, 1 hour, 6 hours, 24 hours, 3 days, 7 days

### Statistics Available
```rust
pub struct WindowStats {
    pub window: Duration,
    pub deletions: DeletionStats,
    pub ballast: BallastStats,
    pub pressure: PressureStats,
}

pub struct DeletionStats {
    pub count: u64,                      // number of items deleted
    pub total_bytes_freed: u64,          // total bytes freed
    pub avg_size: u64,                   // average size of deleted items
    pub median_size: u64,
    pub largest_deletion: Option<PathInfo>,
    pub most_common_category: String,    // e.g., "RustTarget"
    pub most_common_pattern: String,     // e.g., "cargo-target-*"
    pub avg_score: f64,                  // average candidacy score
    pub avg_age_hours: f64,              // average age at time of deletion
    pub failures: u64,                   // failed deletions
}

pub struct BallastStats {
    pub files_released: u64,
    pub files_replenished: u64,
    pub current_inventory: u64,
    pub bytes_available: u64,
}

pub struct PressureStats {
    pub time_in_green_pct: f64,
    pub time_in_yellow_pct: f64,
    pub time_in_orange_pct: f64,
    pub time_in_red_pct: f64,
    pub time_in_critical_pct: f64,
    pub transitions: u64,                // number of pressure level changes
    pub worst_level_reached: PressureLevel,
    pub current_level: PressureLevel,
    pub current_free_pct: f64,
}
```

### Query Implementation
```rust
pub struct StatsEngine {
    sqlite: Arc<SqliteLogger>,
}

impl StatsEngine {
    /// Get stats for all standard windows
    pub fn summary(&self) -> Result<Vec<WindowStats>, SbhError>;
    
    /// Get stats for a specific window
    pub fn window_stats(&self, window: Duration) -> Result<WindowStats, SbhError>;
    
    /// Get top-N most deleted patterns
    pub fn top_patterns(&self, n: usize, window: Duration) -> Result<Vec<PatternStat>, SbhError>;
    
    /// Get top-N largest deletions
    pub fn top_deletions(&self, n: usize, window: Duration) -> Result<Vec<DeletionDetail>, SbhError>;
    
    /// Export stats as JSON (for agent consumption)
    pub fn export_json(&self) -> Result<serde_json::Value, SbhError>;
}
```

### SQL Queries
Efficient aggregation using SQLite's date functions:
```sql
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) as count,
    SUM(size_bytes) as total_bytes,
    AVG(size_bytes) as avg_size,
    AVG(score) as avg_score
FROM activity_log
WHERE event_type = 'artifact_delete'
  AND timestamp > datetime('now', '-1 hour')
  AND success = 1;
```

### Output Format
Both human-readable (colored table for terminal) and JSON (for agents):
```
sbh stats
┌─────────┬────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┬────────────┐
│ Window  │ Deleted│ Space Freed  │ Avg Size     │ Avg Score  │
├─────────┼────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┼────────────┤
│ 10 min  │     3  │       4.2 GB │       1.4 GB │      0.87  │
│ 30 min  │    12  │      18.7 GB │       1.6 GB │      0.82  │
│  1 hour │    28  │      42.1 GB │       1.5 GB │      0.79  │
│  6 hours│    85  │     124.3 GB │       1.5 GB │      0.76  │
│ 24 hours│   312  │     467.8 GB │       1.5 GB │      0.74  │
│  3 days │   891  │    1284.2 GB │       1.4 GB │      0.73  │
│  7 days │  2104  │    3012.7 GB │       1.4 GB │      0.72  │
└─────────┴────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┴────────────┘

Most deleted patterns: cargo-target-* (42%), .target_* (28%), target/ (18%)
Current pressure: GREEN (23.4% free)
Ballast: 8/10 files available (8 GB reclaimable)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All time windows produce correct statistics
- Statistics match actual activity log data (verified by cross-checking)
- JSON output is well-formed and complete
- Human-readable output is clear and informative
- Queries are efficient (< 100ms even with 100K+ log entries)
- Most common patterns correctly computed
- Handles empty database gracefully
- Unit tests with pre-populated test data
close_reason: done

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: MINOR: Stats engine schema coupling with SQLite logger (bd-2f8): The stats engine queries the SQLite database directly. The schema is defined in bd-2f8 and the stats engine reads it. This is intentional tight coupling — they share the same database. Any schema changes in bd-2f8 must be reflected in bd-3cj queries. Document the schema contract in both beads.

title: launchd plist generation and service installation (macOS)
description:
## Deliverable
Generate and install a launchd plist for the sbh daemon on macOS.

## Technical Approach
### Generated Plist
```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
    <key>Label</key>
    <string>com.sbh.daemon</string>
    <key>ProgramArguments</key>
    <array>
        <string>/usr/local/bin/sbh</string>
        <string>daemon</string>
    </array>
    <key>RunAtLoad</key>
    <true/>
    <key>KeepAlive</key>
    <dict>
        <key>SuccessfulExit</key>
        <false/>
    </dict>
    <key>ThrottleInterval</key>
    <integer>10</integer>
    <key>Nice</key>
    <integer>19</integer>
    <key>LowPriorityIO</key>
    <true/>
    <key>StandardOutPath</key>
    <string>/usr/local/var/log/sbh/sbh.log</string>
    <key>StandardErrorPath</key>
    <string>/usr/local/var/log/sbh/sbh.err</string>
</dict>
</plist>
```

### Installation Flow
```bash
sbh install --launchd
```
1. Generate plist from template
2. Copy to ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.sbh.daemon.plist (user) or /Library/LaunchDaemons/ (system)
3. launchctl load plist_path
4. Verify service is running

### macOS-specific Considerations
- No sd_notify equivalent; use different health check
- macOS paths differ (~/Library/Application Support/sbh/)
- Need to handle SIP restrictions (System Integrity Protection)
- macOS doesn't have /dev/shm or tmpfs in the same way

## Acceptance Criteria
- Generated plist is valid XML
- Service loads and starts via launchctl
- Auto-restart on crash (KeepAlive)
- Low priority scheduling (Nice=19, LowPriorityIO)
- Works as user agent (no root required)
- Integration test on macOS: install → start → verify → stop → uninstall

title: PID-based storage pressure controller
description:
## Deliverable
A PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller that determines how aggressively sbh should respond to storage pressure, replacing simplistic threshold-based reactions with smooth, oscillation-free behavior.

## Technical Approach
### Theory (Alien Graveyard: PID control from control theory)
Instead of simple "if free < 5% then panic", use a feedback controller:

- **P (Proportional)**: Response proportional to how far we are from target free%
  - Large deviation → aggressive cleanup
  - Small deviation → gentle cleanup
  
- **I (Integral)**: Accumulated error over time
  - Persistent pressure (free% stayed low for minutes) → escalate response
  - Prevents the system from being "stuck" at a slightly-too-low level
  - Anti-windup clamp prevents integral term from growing unboundedly
  
- **D (Derivative)**: Rate of change (from EWMA estimator)
  - Rapid disk consumption → preemptive aggressive response
  - Rapid recovery → dampen response to prevent over-correction

### Implementation
```rust
pub struct PressureController {
    // PID gains (configurable)
    kp: f64,  // proportional gain (default 1.0)
    ki: f64,  // integral gain (default 0.1)
    kd: f64,  // derivative gain (default 0.5)
    
    // State
    setpoint: f64,           // target free% (default 15.0)
    integral: f64,           // accumulated error
    integral_clamp: f64,     // anti-windup limit
    previous_error: f64,     // for derivative calculation
    last_update: Instant,
    
    // Hysteresis (Alien Graveyard: prevent oscillation)
    hysteresis_band: f64,    // ±2% band around setpoint
    last_action: PressureAction,
}

pub struct PressureReading {
    pub free_pct: f64,
    pub rate_estimate: RateEstimate,
    pub timestamp: Instant,
}

pub enum PressureLevel {
    Green,     // > setpoint + hysteresis → no action needed
    Yellow,    // within hysteresis band → maintain current action
    Orange,    // below setpoint → moderate cleanup
    Red,       // significantly below setpoint → aggressive cleanup
    Critical,  // near 0% → emergency: delete ballast + all safe artifacts
}

pub struct PressureResponse {
    pub level: PressureLevel,
    pub urgency: f64,              // 0.0-1.0 (PID output, clamped)
    pub recommended_actions: Vec<PressureAction>,
    pub scan_interval: Duration,   // adaptive scan interval
    pub max_delete_batch: usize,   // how many items to delete per cycle
}

pub enum PressureAction {
    NoAction,
    IncreaseScanFrequency,
    ReleaseBallast(usize),  // release N ballast files
    DeleteArtifacts { min_score: f64 },
    EmergencyCleanup,
}
```

### Hysteresis (Alien Graveyard: prevent oscillation)
Without hysteresis, the controller would oscillate:
1. Free drops to 14% → start cleaning
2. Free rises to 16% → stop cleaning
3. Free drops to 14% → start cleaning again
4. (repeat forever)

With a 2% hysteresis band around the 15% setpoint:
- Start cleaning when free < 13%
- Stop cleaning when free > 17%
- This creates stable behavior

### Adaptive Scan Intervals
The PID output also controls scan frequency:
- Green: scan every 60s
- Yellow: scan every 30s
- Orange: scan every 10s
- Red: scan every 5s
- Critical: scan every 1s

## Design Rationale
Simple threshold systems lead to oscillation, delayed response, or over-reaction. A PID controller with hysteresis provides:
1. Smooth, proportional responses (not all-or-nothing)
2. Faster reaction to rapid changes (derivative term)
3. Persistent pressure handled by integral buildup
4. No oscillation thanks to hysteresis band
5. Tunable behavior via gains (users can adjust aggressiveness)

## Acceptance Criteria
- PID output is smooth and bounded (no oscillation in test scenarios)
- Hysteresis prevents chattering between states
- Anti-windup prevents integral runaway
- Response proportional to pressure severity
- Scan intervals adapt correctly to pressure level
- Unit tests with simulated pressure scenarios
- Test: sustained 10% pressure → integral buildup → escalated response
- Test: sudden spike → derivative kicks in → fast response
- Test: recovery → smooth return to normal scanning
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests validate PID term calculations, anti-windup, clamps, and hysteresis transitions. 2. Closed-loop integration tests verify stable behavior with noisy EWMA pressure input. 3. E2E pressure scenarios validate action selection under burst, sustained, and recovery phases. 4. Controller output is monotonic with urgency and respects hard safety caps. 5. Detailed control-loop logs include P/I/D components, setpoint error, selected action, and guard/fallback reason codes.
close_reason: PID pressure controller implemented in monitor/pid.rs (359 lines): PidPressureController with PressureLevel enum (Green-Critical), hysteresis, anti-windup integral, predictive boosting (time-to-red signals), response_policy with adaptive scan intervals, unit tests for escalation/hysteresis/prediction.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: Removed bd-2pj dependency, added bd-3uk dependency. PID controller receives PressureReading from the caller (main loop). It does not import or call FsStatsCollector. Data flow: main loop calls FsStatsCollector -> creates PressureReading -> passes to PressureController.update(). PID controller is pure computation on top of the reading. Needs bd-3uk for config validation errors (invalid PID gains).

title: Project protection system with marker files and CLI
description:
## Deliverable
User-controllable project protection that prevents sbh from ever deleting artifacts in protected directories.

## The Problem
The scoring engine has hard vetoes (.git, system paths, age), but there is NO way for users to say "never touch THIS specific project." Users WILL have projects with 6-hour builds they cannot afford to lose. Without explicit protection, they will never trust sbh on production machines.

## The Solution

### Marker Files
Drop a .sbh-protect file in any directory to protect it and all children from sbh cleanup:
  touch /data/projects/critical-app/.sbh-protect

The walker checks for .sbh-protect during traversal. If found, the entire directory subtree is skipped — not scored, not walked, not considered.

### CLI Commands
  sbh protect <path>        # Creates .sbh-protect marker file
  sbh protect --list        # Shows all protected paths (marker files + config)
  sbh unprotect <path>      # Removes .sbh-protect marker file
  sbh scan --show-protected # Includes protected paths in output with [PROTECTED] label

### Config-Level Protection
For system-wide or pattern-based protection:
  [scanner.protected_paths]
  paths = ["/data/projects/production-*", "/home/*/critical-builds"]

Config protection works via glob patterns and does NOT require marker files.

### Implementation
1. ProtectionRegistry: loaded at startup from config + discovered marker files
2. Walker integration: during traversal, check each directory for .sbh-protect before descending
3. Scoring integration: protected paths get hard veto (score = 0.0, veto_reason = "Protected by .sbh-protect")
4. CLI commands: trivial file create/delete operations
5. Protection persists across restarts (marker files are on disk, config is in TOML)

### Data Structure
```rust
pub struct ProtectionRegistry {
    marker_paths: HashSet<PathBuf>,      // discovered .sbh-protect locations
    config_patterns: Vec<GlobPattern>,   // from config file
}

impl ProtectionRegistry {
    pub fn is_protected(&self, path: &Path) -> bool;
    pub fn protection_reason(&self, path: &Path) -> Option<String>;
    pub fn discover_markers(&mut self, root: &Path);
}
```

### .sbh-protect File Format
The file can be empty (presence is sufficient) OR contain optional JSON metadata:
```json
{
  "reason": "Production build - 6 hour compile time",
  "protected_by": "jeff",
  "protected_at": "2026-02-14T10:00:00Z"
}
```
The metadata is displayed in sbh scan --show-protected and sbh protect --list.

## Design Rationale
This is the #1 trust-building feature. Without it, sophisticated users won't deploy sbh on production machines. With it, they feel in control. Implementation cost is near-zero: one additional check in the walker + two CLI commands. The marker file approach is elegant because: (1) it's discoverable (ls shows it), (2) it's portable (survives config changes), (3) it's git-committable (team-wide protection), (4) it requires zero sbh knowledge to create (just touch a file).

## Acceptance Criteria
- .sbh-protect marker file prevents all cleanup in that directory and children
- sbh protect <path> creates marker file correctly
- sbh unprotect <path> removes marker file
- sbh protect --list shows all protections (marker + config) with metadata
- Config-level glob patterns work correctly
- Protected paths appear in sbh scan with [PROTECTED] label
- Walker skips protected directories entirely (no unnecessary traversal)
- Protection survives daemon restart
- Empty .sbh-protect file works (metadata optional)
- Unit tests: protection check logic, glob patterns, nested protection
- Integration test: protect dir -> scan -> verify not scored -> unprotect -> scan -> verify scored
close_reason: Implemented ProtectionRegistry with dual-mode (full/marker-only), marker file discovery, config glob patterns, 26 unit tests all passing

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
IMPORTANT: The protection system must work in TWO modes:
1. FULL MODE (with config): reads scanner.protected_paths from config AND discovers .sbh-protect marker files
2. MARKER-ONLY MODE (without config): only discovers .sbh-protect marker files during walker traversal

This is critical because the emergency mode (bd-2qa) uses the walker, and the walker depends on the protection system. Emergency mode operates WITHOUT any config file. The protection system must gracefully handle the 'no config available' case by falling back to marker-only mode.

Implementation: ProtectionRegistry::new() takes Optional<Config>. When None, skip config-level glob patterns and only use marker file discovery.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: Removed bd-2rq dependency. The ProtectionRegistry is a LIBRARY component (marker file discovery, glob matching, is_protected() API). It does NOT import CLI framework code. CLI commands (sbh protect/unprotect) are wired into bd-2rq when that bead is implemented. bd-3qm deps: bd-3qw (config for glob patterns), bd-3uk (error types), bd-1kn (scaffolding).

title: Configuration system with TOML + env vars + smart defaults
description:
## Deliverable
Comprehensive configuration system supporting layered config: defaults → system config → user config → env vars → CLI flags.

## Technical Approach
### Config File Location (XDG-compliant)
- Linux: ~/.config/sbh/config.toml, /etc/sbh/config.toml
- macOS: ~/Library/Application Support/sbh/config.toml
- Windows: %APPDATA%\sbh\config.toml

### Config Structure
```toml
[general]
log_level = "info"                  # trace/debug/info/warn/error
scan_interval_seconds = 30          # base scan interval
critical_scan_interval_seconds = 5  # scan interval when pressure is high
dry_run = false                     # log what would be deleted without deleting

[ballast]
enabled = true
location = "/var/lib/sbh/ballast"   # or auto-detect
file_count = 10                     # number of 1GB ballast files
file_size_mb = 1024                 # size of each ballast file
min_free_after_release_pct = 15.0   # target free% after releasing ballast

[pressure]
green_threshold_pct = 20.0          # > 20% free = green
yellow_threshold_pct = 15.0         # 10-20% = yellow (warning)
orange_threshold_pct = 10.0         # 5-10% = orange (alert)
red_threshold_pct = 5.0             # < 5% = red (critical)
critical_threshold_pct = 2.0        # < 2% = emergency

# PID controller tuning
pid_kp = 1.0                        # proportional gain
pid_ki = 0.1                        # integral gain (accumulated pressure)
pid_kd = 0.5                        # derivative gain (rate of change)

[scanner]
max_depth = 10                      # max directory recursion depth
min_file_age_minutes = 30           # never delete files newer than this
sweet_spot_min_hours = 2.0          # ideal deletion age range start
sweet_spot_max_hours = 10.0         # ideal deletion age range end
min_size_mb = 10                    # skip files smaller than this

[scanner.watched_paths]
# paths to actively scan for build artifacts
paths = ["/data/projects", "/tmp", "/data/tmp", "/home"]

[scanner.excluded_paths]
# paths to NEVER touch
paths = ["/", "/boot", "/etc", "/usr", "/bin", "/sbin", "/var/log"]

[scanner.special_locations]
# RAM-backed locations requiring hawk-like monitoring
paths = ["/tmp", "/dev/shm", "/run/shm"]
buffer_pct = 15.0                   # maintain this % free minimum

[logger]
sqlite_path = "/var/lib/sbh/sbh.db"
jsonl_path = "/var/lib/sbh/sbh.jsonl"
max_jsonl_size_mb = 100             # rotate JSONL at this size
retention_days = 30                 # keep logs for this many days
```

### Environment Variable Override Pattern
Every config key maps to an env var: `SBH_BALLAST_FILE_COUNT=20` overrides `ballast.file_count`.

### Validation
All config values validated on load with clear error messages. Invalid values → SBH-2xxx errors with suggestions.

## Design Rationale
Layered configuration with env var overrides is essential for containerized/systemd environments where you want to customize behavior without editing files. Smart defaults mean sbh works out of the box with zero configuration on typical Linux systems. The PID controller tuning parameters allow advanced users to tune pressure response behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Default config works out of the box (no config file needed)
- Config loads from correct platform-specific locations
- Env vars override file config
- All values validated with helpful error messages on invalid input
- Config serializes to/from TOML round-trip perfectly
- Unit tests for default values, overrides, validation
close_reason: Configuration system implemented in core/config.rs (481 lines): full TOML parsing, env var overrides (SBH_*), PressureConfig with thresholds, ScannerConfig, ScoringConfig with weights, BallastConfig, TelemetryConfig, PathsConfig, comprehensive validation, unit tests.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Two additions needed:

1. PID CONTROLLER CONFIG: The config bead's TOML example includes pid_kp, pid_ki, pid_kd, hysteresis_band parameters, but the bead description doesn't mention exposing these through the config structure. The Config struct must include a PidConfig section:
  [pressure.pid]
  kp = 1.0
  ki = 0.1
  kd = 0.5
  hysteresis_band_pct = 2.0
  setpoint_free_pct = 15.0
  integral_clamp = 10.0

2. SPECIAL LOCATIONS CONFIG: The config should include a [scanner.special_locations] section for configuring special location monitoring parameters beyond just the paths:
  [scanner.special_locations]
  paths = ["/tmp", "/dev/shm", "/run/shm"]
  buffer_pct = 15.0
  scan_interval_seconds = 5

This ensures the special location registry (bd-1td) can read its configuration from the same config system instead of having hardcoded defaults.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
ENHANCEMENT (idea-wizard): Add per-category retention policies in config. Different artifact types should have different age thresholds:
  [scanner.retention]
  rust_target.min_age_minutes = 60      # Rust builds are expensive
  node_modules.min_age_minutes = 30     # npm install is fast
  python_cache.min_age_minutes = 15     # __pycache__ is trivially regenerated
  cache_dirs.min_age_minutes = 120      # ~/.cache might have important state
  agent_workspace.min_age_minutes = 45  # Agent workspaces need moderate protection

The scoring engine (bd-x9z) would check the category-specific min_age rather than the global min_file_age_minutes. This makes sbh smarter about what to clean first: cheap-to-rebuild artifacts get cleaned before expensive-to-rebuild ones.

title: Live TUI dashboard with real-time pressure visualization
description:
## Deliverable
sbh dashboard — a live terminal UI showing real-time pressure gauges, EWMA trends, scan activity, and deletion history. Uses frankentui reference patterns from /dp/frankentui.

## Technical Approach
### CLI
  sbh dashboard              # Launch interactive TUI dashboard
  sbh dashboard --refresh 2  # Custom refresh interval (default 1s)

### Layout
```
┌─ Storage Ballast Helper v0.1.0 ──────────────────────────── uptime: 3d 14h ─┐
│                                                                               │
│  Pressure Gauges                                                              │
│  /data    [████████████░░░░░░░░] 62% used (684 GB free)  GREEN               │
│  /tmp     [██████████████████░░] 90% used (3.2 GB free)  ORANGE  ⚠ 18m left  │
│  /dev/shm [████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░] 23% used (198 GB free) GREEN               │
│                                                                               │
│  EWMA Trends (last 30 min)                                                    │
│  /data  ▁▁▂▃▄▅▆▅▄▃▂▁▁▁▁  -12 MB/s (recovering)                             │
│  /tmp   ▁▂▃▅▇█████████   +245 MB/s (accelerating) ⚠                         │
│                                                                               │
│  Recent Activity                                          Ballast             │
│  16:30:01 DEL cargo-target-mvcc (4.2 GB, 0.94)          /data: 8/10 avail   │
│  16:29:55 DEL .target_opus_main (8.1 GB, 0.91)          /tmp:  3/3 avail    │
│  16:29:40 SCAN /data/projects (47 candidates)            /: 5/5 avail        │
│  16:29:00 ALERT /tmp approaching threshold                                    │
│                                                                               │
│  PID: P=0.3 I=0.1 D=0.8 | Scans: 1542 | Deleted: 312 (467 GB) | Errs: 2   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation
- Refresh via polling state.json + live fs stats
- Sparkline charts for EWMA trends (unicode block characters)
- Color-coded pressure levels (green/yellow/orange/red with ANSI colors)
- Scrollable activity log
- PID controller state at bottom
- Uses crossterm for terminal manipulation (already used by frankentui)
- Graceful exit on q/Ctrl-C

### Reference
Follow patterns from /dp/frankentui for rendering approach, color schemes, layout primitives.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Live updating display (1s default refresh)
- All monitored volumes shown with pressure gauges
- EWMA trend sparklines for each volume
- Recent activity log with timestamps
- Ballast inventory per volume
- PID controller state summary
- Graceful exit, restores terminal state
- Works when daemon is not running (degraded mode showing static fs stats)
- Handles terminal resize

title: Stress test and load simulator for extreme pressure scenarios
description:
## Deliverable
Stress and load simulator for extreme pressure scenarios, including adaptive-policy guard/fallback paths.

## Scenario Matrix
A. Rapid fill burst under compile-like load
B. Sustained low-free-space pressure
C. Flash fill in RAM-backed locations
D. Recovery under ongoing write pressure
E. High CPU + high I/O overload condition
F. Decision-plane drift event triggering fallback
G. Index integrity failure forcing full-scan mode

## Metrics Collection
- time to detection and first action
- reclaimed vs consumed bytes
- controller mode transitions and guard states
- fallback trigger counts and recovery windows
- sbh CPU/memory/IO overhead

## Detailed Logging Contract
- per-phase trace identifiers
- action selection rationale
- fallback reason codes and guard metrics
- before/after pressure snapshots

## Acceptance Criteria
- all scenarios pass with deterministic checks
- fallback behavior triggered and validated where expected
- no crashes, deadlocks, or unbounded resource growth
- machine-readable stress report emitted
- stress harness integrated with CI/nightly path
acceptance_criteria: 1. Stress scenarios validate behavior under bursty load, sustained pressure, and recovery. 2. Resource ceilings and stability invariants are enforced (no deadlock/leak/unbounded growth). 3. Adaptive-policy guard/fallback transitions are triggered and verified under induced drift/faults. 4. Stress logs include per-phase metrics, action timelines, and failure snapshots for replay. 5. Reports are emitted in machine-readable form and archived by CI/nightly workflows.

title: Core error types with SBH-prefixed error codes
description:
## Deliverable
Define the comprehensive error type system for sbh with machine-parseable error codes.

## Technical Approach
Following dcg's pattern of prefixed error codes (DCG-XXXX), create SBH-XXXX codes:

### Error Categories
- SBH-1xxx: Storage monitoring errors (statvfs failures, mount detection errors)
- SBH-2xxx: Configuration errors (parse failures, invalid values, missing config)
- SBH-3xxx: Runtime/daemon errors (signal handling, service install failures)
- SBH-4xxx: Scanner errors (permission denied, broken symlinks, walk failures)
- SBH-5xxx: Logger errors (SQLite failures, JSONL write errors, disk full during logging)
- SBH-6xxx: Ballast errors (creation failures, integrity check failures)

### Error Traits
- All errors implement std::error::Error via thiserror
- All errors carry structured context (path, filesystem, operation)
- Errors are serializable to JSON for machine consumption
- Recoverability classification: Transient (retry), Permanent (fail), Unknown

### Key Types
```rust
pub enum SbhError {
    Storage(StorageError),
    Config(ConfigError),
    Runtime(RuntimeError),
    Scanner(ScannerError),
    Logger(LoggerError),
    Ballast(BallastError),
}

pub enum Recoverability {
    Transient,  // Safe to retry
    Permanent,  // Unrecoverable
    Unknown,    // Context-dependent
}
```

## Design Rationale
Inspired by asupersync's three-tier error system and dcg's DCG-XXXX codes. Machine-parseable error codes let agents programmatically handle failures. Recoverability classification enables the daemon to auto-retry transient failures (disk temporarily busy) while surfacing permanent failures (permissions wrong) immediately.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All error variants have unique SBH-XXXX codes
- All errors implement Display with human-readable messages
- All errors serialize to JSON with code, category, message, context fields
- Unit tests verify error code uniqueness and serialization round-trip
close_reason: Core error types implemented in core/errors.rs (138 lines): SBH-prefixed error codes (1001-3900), thiserror derives, Recoverability classification (is_retryable), Display/From impls for rusqlite/serde_json/toml errors.

title: Main monitoring loop with tiered scan frequencies
description:
## Deliverable
The central daemon monitoring loop that orchestrates all sbh components: pressure monitoring, ballast management, artifact scanning, and cleanup.

## Technical Approach
### Architecture
The daemon runs as a single process with multiple threads:
1. **Monitor thread**: Polls filesystem stats, updates EWMA, runs PID controller
2. **Scanner thread**: Walks directories, scores candidates (triggered by monitor)
3. **Executor thread**: Deletes candidates from the ranked queue
4. **Logger thread**: Writes to SQLite and JSONL (receives events via channel)

Communication via crossbeam channels (no tokio, following asupersync's no-tokio philosophy):
```
Monitor → (PressureEvent) → Scanner
Scanner → (CandidateList) → Executor
All    → (ActivityEvent)  → Logger
```

### Tiered Scan Frequencies
Different locations get different scan intervals based on criticality:

**Tier 1: Special locations (5-second cycle)**
- /tmp (tmpfs)
- /dev/shm
- Any RAM-backed mount
- Check free% and alert if below buffer threshold

**Tier 2: Primary project locations (30-second cycle)**
- /data/projects/*
- /home/*/projects/*
- Full artifact scanning with scoring

**Tier 3: Cache and misc locations (120-second cycle)**
- ~/.cache/*
- /data/tmp/*
- Less aggressive scanning

### Adaptive Frequency
The PID controller output adjusts these base intervals:
- Green pressure: use base intervals
- Yellow: halve intervals
- Orange: quarter intervals
- Red: minimum intervals (1s for special, 5s for primary, 15s for cache)
- Critical: maximum frequency on all tiers

### Main Loop Pseudocode
```rust
loop {
    // 1. Collect filesystem stats (all monitored paths)
    let stats = fs_collector.collect_all();
    
    // 2. Update EWMA rate estimator
    rate_estimator.update(stats);
    
    // 3. Run PID pressure controller
    let response = pressure_controller.update(stats, rate_estimator.estimate());
    
    // 4. Handle pressure response
    match response.level {
        Green => { /* normal operation, maybe replenish ballast */ },
        Yellow => { /* increase scan frequency */ },
        Orange => { /* start scanning + gentle cleanup */ },
        Red => { /* release ballast + aggressive scan + delete */ },
        Critical => { /* emergency: release all ballast + delete everything safe */ },
    }
    
    // 5. Check special locations independently
    for loc in special_locations.iter() {
        if loc.needs_attention(stats) {
            handle_special_location_pressure(loc, stats);
        }
    }
    
    // 6. Sleep for adaptive interval
    thread::sleep(response.scan_interval);
}
```

### Graceful Degradation Under Load
When the system is already overloaded (high load average, heavy I/O wait):
- Reduce parallelism in directory walker
- Use ionice to lower I/O priority
- Increase sleep between operations
- Prefer ballast release over scanning (instant vs. slow)

The daemon must NEVER make an already-bad situation worse.

### Startup Sequence
1. Load/validate configuration
2. Initialize platform abstraction
3. Open/create SQLite database
4. Open JSONL log file
5. Discover special locations
6. Verify/provision ballast files
7. Initial pressure check (are we already in trouble?)
8. Enter main loop

### Shutdown Sequence (on SIGTERM/SIGINT)
1. Stop accepting new scan requests
2. Complete in-progress deletions (bounded timeout: 30s)
3. Flush logger buffers
4. Close SQLite connection
5. Log shutdown event
6. Exit 0

## Design Rationale
The tiered scanning approach is essential because not all locations are equally critical. RAM-backed locations (tmpfs) need near-real-time monitoring because they can fill up in seconds during a cargo build. Disk-backed locations can be checked less frequently. The adaptive frequency ensures sbh responds quickly to pressure without wasting resources during calm periods.

Using threads with crossbeam channels instead of async/tokio follows the asupersync project's no-tokio philosophy and keeps the daemon simple, predictable, and debuggable. The daemon is fundamentally a polling-based system, not a network server, so async isn't needed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Daemon starts up correctly and enters monitoring loop
- Tiered scanning respects configured intervals
- Adaptive frequency responds to pressure changes
- Special locations monitored independently and more frequently
- All events logged to both SQLite and JSONL
- Graceful shutdown on SIGTERM/SIGINT
- Startup detects and handles "already in trouble" scenarios
- Thread communication via channels is reliable
- Integration test: simulate full lifecycle (start → pressure → cleanup → stop)

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Three critical design details added:

1. BOUNDED CHANNELS & BACKPRESSURE: All crossbeam channels must be bounded to prevent unbounded memory growth under load:
  - Monitor → Scanner channel: bounded(16) — if scanner falls behind, monitor drops old pressure events (latest-wins)
  - Scanner → Executor channel: bounded(64) — if executor is slow, scanner blocks (natural backpressure). This prevents scoring thousands of candidates that will never be executed.
  - All → Logger channel: bounded(1024) — generous buffer since logging is fast. If buffer fills, events are dropped with a stderr warning (never block the monitoring loop for logging).

2. THREAD PANIC RECOVERY: If any worker thread panics, the main monitoring thread must detect it and restart it:
  - Store JoinHandle for each thread
  - Check thread health every 10 seconds (is_finished())
  - If a thread has exited unexpectedly, log the error and respawn it
  - If the SAME thread panics 3 times in 5 minutes, stop respawning and escalate to error reporting
  - The monitoring thread itself is the "last line of defense" — if it panics, systemd's WatchdogSec will restart the process

3. EXECUTOR SLOWER THAN SCANNER: When the executor is slower than the scanner (e.g., deleting large directory trees while new candidates arrive), the bounded channel (64 slots) provides natural backpressure. The scanner blocks on send(), which slows down scanning. This is CORRECT behavior — there's no point discovering more candidates if we can't delete them fast enough. Under extreme pressure, the executor should prioritize the highest-scoring candidates first (the channel should be a priority queue or the executor should sort its batch before processing).

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: After removing bd-112 and bd-2up as hard dependencies, the main loop should accept these as optional integrations wired in at startup: (1) Option<NotificationSender> — if notification system (bd-112) is compiled in and configured, send pressure transition events. If None, notifications are simply skipped. (2) Option<PredictiveActionPolicy> — if predictive pipeline (bd-2up) is available, evaluate EWMA predictions each cycle and pre-empt if confidence is high. If None, rely solely on reactive PID response. This means bd-48o can ship and work correctly WITHOUT either feature. They are layered on top, not load-bearing. The startup sequence checks config to decide whether to construct these Option<T> values.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: DaemonArgs specification: The 'sbh daemon' command starts the monitoring loop. DaemonArgs struct: --foreground (default, systemd manages backgrounding), --pidfile <path> (optional, for non-systemd setups). The daemon subcommand handler calls the startup sequence defined in this bead. Scanner thread data flow: (1) Walker produces Vec<WalkEntry>. (2) Scanner thread constructs ScoringInput for each (adding pressure_urgency, is_open, now). (3) ScoringEngine.score(input) produces CandidacyScore. (4) Candidates above threshold collected into ranked Vec sorted by score desc. (5) Ranked batch sent to Executor thread via bounded channel. All scoring happens inline in the scanner thread — scoring is cheap pure computation.

title: CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions
description:
## Deliverable
GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline for automated linting, comprehensive testing, reproducibility artifact capture, and release.

## Technical Approach
### Workflow: ci.yml
Jobs:
1. check: cargo fmt --check + cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings
2. unit: full unit/property suite (including invariant tests)
3. integration: full pipeline integration suite
4. e2e: scripts/e2e_test.sh plus decision-plane e2e scenarios
5. stress: stress/load scenarios with bounded runtime profile
6. coverage: cargo llvm-cov thresholds with module-specific minimums
7. provenance: emit env/manifest/repro.lock style metadata for benchmark/test runs

### Logging and Artifact Contract
Each CI stage publishes:
- machine-readable test summary
- detailed logs for failed cases
- trace bundles for decision-plane failures
- timing metrics and flaky-test diagnostics

### Workflow: release.yml
Triggered by version bump:
1. Run full quality gate (all ci.yml stages)
2. Build release binaries for Linux/macOS targets
3. Create archives + SHA256 checksums
4. Attach test/provenance summary to release artifacts

## Acceptance Criteria
- CI runs on PR and push to main
- Unit, integration, e2e, and stress gates are enforced
- Decision-plane logs/artifacts are retained on failures
- Coverage and lint thresholds are enforced
- Release jobs require successful quality gates and attach checksums/provenance
acceptance_criteria: 1. CI enforces fmt/clippy/tests/coverage gates on PR and push to main. 2. Unit/integration/e2e/stress suites execute with artifact retention for failures. 3. Decision-plane trace bundles and provenance metadata are attached to failing runs. 4. Release workflow requires passing quality gates and emits checksummed artifacts. 5. CI logging remains structured and queryable for root-cause analysis.

title: [macOS E13] Memory-pressure-driven defenses + interaction matrix
description:
# Epic 13: Memory-pressure-driven defenses on macOS

## What this composes

E3 builds the *measurement* of memory pressure. This epic builds the *response*: when memory is under pressure, what does sbh do differently? Especially: how does memory pressure interact with *disk* pressure?

## The interaction matrix

| Memory | Disk | sbh behavior |
|--------|------|--------------|
| Normal | Normal | Normal poll cadence (15s). |
| Warn | Normal | Light scan; avoid scanning huge dirs (would page in lots of metadata). |
| Critical | Normal | Skip scans entirely; report only. Don't worsen memory. |
| Normal | Warn | Aggressive scan; identify candidates; defer cleanup until disk Critical. |
| Warn | Warn | Cleanup high-confidence candidates; release ballast. |
| Critical | Warn | Cleanup most-promising candidates; preserve memory by avoiding large dir walks. |
| Normal | Critical | Emergency mode; cleanup definite candidates; release ballast. |
| Warn | Critical | Emergency; release ballast first (instant relief), then cleanup definite. |
| Critical | Critical | All-hands emergency: release ballast; cleanup any definite; surface emergency notification. Do minimal work to avoid swap thrash. |

## Mac-specific event subscription

`dispatch_source_create(DISPATCH_SOURCE_TYPE_MEMORYPRESSURE, ...)` fires on Normal→Warn, Warn→Critical, Critical→Warn, Warn→Normal transitions. We subscribe at daemon start. On transition, immediately re-evaluate the matrix above and adjust behavior.

## Required sbh behavior

- Subscribe to memory pressure events at daemon start.
- Maintain current matrix cell as state.
- Behavior dispatch table per cell.
- Logging: every transition logged with new behavior summary.
- `sbh status` reports current matrix cell.
- Notification (E5/E11 integration): if disk Critical AND memory Critical, send macOS user notification (NSUserNotification or `osascript -e 'display notification ...'`) so the operator sees the alert immediately even if Activity Monitor isn't open.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. PAL method `pal.subscribe_memory_pressure(callback) -> SubscriptionHandle`. Mac uses dispatch_source. Linux uses /proc/pressure/memory polling + epoll on the file (PSI supports epoll).
2. Behavior dispatch table data structure.
3. Daemon main loop integration: react to transitions immediately, not just on the next poll tick.
4. macOS user notification integration (osascript fallback first; NSUserNotification later if needed).
5. Cross-platform behavior table — same matrix on Linux + Mac, different transition signals.
6. Test: simulate transitions (mock memory pressure source), assert daemon transitions through correct states, performs correct actions.

## Why event-driven matters on Mac

Mac users keep laptops on battery. Polling at 15s while idle wastes energy. Event subscription means: zero cost during normal, immediate response during pressure. Energy Impact stays green.

## Failure modes

- dispatch_source callback runs on dispatch queue, not main thread. sbh's daemon is single-threaded Rust async runtime. Bridge: callback enqueues a message to daemon's mpsc channel. Don't block in the callback.
- Pressure event flapping (Warn → Critical → Warn → ...). Add hysteresis: minimum 5s between behavior transitions in the same direction.
- Subscription leaks: SubscriptionHandle drop must release the dispatch source. Implement Drop carefully.

title: [E13.1] PAL subscribe_memory_pressure() (event-driven, both OSes)
description: PAL method pal.subscribe_memory_pressure(callback) returning SubscriptionHandle. Mac impl: dispatch_source_create with DISPATCH_SOURCE_TYPE_MEMORYPRESSURE. Linux impl: poll /proc/pressure/memory + epoll on the file (PSI supports epoll-based notification on level transitions). Both produce same MemoryPressureEvent enum.

title: [E13.2] Behavior dispatch table (9-cell mem x disk matrix)
description: Behavior dispatch table data structure encoding the 9-cell matrix (Normal/Warn/Critical x disk x memory). Each cell maps to a BehaviorMode: { scan_aggressiveness, cleanup_action, ballast_action, notification_priority }. Centralized so future changes are config-only.

title: [E13.3] Daemon main loop integrates pressure-event transitions
description: Daemon main loop integration: react to transitions immediately, not just on next poll tick. Subscribe at daemon start; transitions enqueue MemoryPressureEvent on mpsc channel; main loop drains and updates BehaviorMode within 500ms.

title: [E13.4] macOS user notification at Critical+Critical
description: macOS user notification integration: osascript -e display notification fallback first; NSUserNotification later if needed. Throttle notifications: max 1 per 5 minutes per category.

title: [E13.5] Cross-platform behavior parity test
description: Cross-platform behavior table — same matrix on Linux + Mac, different transition signals. Verify via test that synthetic transitions produce identical BehaviorMode on both OSes.

title: [E13.6] Mock-driven matrix transition tests
description: Test: simulate transitions via mock memory pressure source, assert daemon transitions through correct states, performs correct actions (scan/cleanup/ballast). Use MockPal injection.

title: [E13.7] Hysteresis to avoid behavior flapping
description: Hysteresis to avoid flapping: minimum 5s between behavior transitions in same direction. Document and configurable.

title: [macOS E6] Signal handling + IPC for runtime control
description:
# Epic 6: Signal handling, IPC, and runtime control on macOS

## Why this matters

`src/daemon/signals.rs` today is gated `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` for parts that use sd_notify (systemd readiness/watchdog). Mac's launchd doesn't use sd_notify. It does use SIGINFO (BSD-specific signal, not on Linux) for "give me a status dump" interactivity.

## Cross-platform signal mapping

| Behavior | Linux | macOS |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Terminate (graceful) | SIGTERM | SIGTERM |
| Reload config | SIGHUP | SIGHUP |
| Force exit | SIGKILL | SIGKILL |
| Status dump | (custom RPC) | SIGINFO |
| Readiness | sd_notify("READY=1") | (none — launchd just expects process to exit nonzero on failure) |
| Watchdog | sd_notify("WATCHDOG=1") periodically | (KeepAlive handles, no per-tick notify needed) |
| Reload after config change | SIGUSR1 | SIGUSR1 |

## Required sbh behavior

- SIGTERM: graceful shutdown — finish in-flight scan, persist I/O history, exit 0.
- SIGHUP: reload config without restart. Same on both OSes.
- SIGINFO (Mac only): print `sbh status --json` to stderr. Useful when running in foreground for debugging — press Ctrl+T (terminal sends SIGINFO).
- SIGUSR1: same as SIGHUP today; keep semantics.
- Any unhandled signal: exit nonzero so launchd KeepAlive=Crashed=true triggers restart.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. Refactor `src/daemon/signals.rs` to take a `&dyn ServiceManager` reference; signals dispatch to platform-specific notification methods (sd_notify on Linux, no-op on Mac).
2. Add SIGINFO handler on Mac (Linux ignores — SIGINFO is value 30 which on Linux is SIGPWR; we conditionally handle SIGINFO on macos only).
3. Status-dump-on-SIGINFO writes valid JSON to stderr (so it can be captured to file with `2> /tmp/sbh_status.json`).
4. Test: spawn daemon in foreground, send SIGTERM/SIGHUP/SIGINFO, assert behavior.

## Reference

- `man 2 sigaction`, `man 7 signal`, `signal-hook` crate
- launchd: KeepAlive doesn't require any positive notification from the process; absence of crash is the readiness signal
- Apple's expected behavior for launchd-managed processes: just be a long-running process that exits 0 on graceful shutdown, exits nonzero on crash

## Failure modes

- Installing a SIGINFO handler on Linux → compile error or runtime confusion (SIGINFO == SIGPWR on Linux). Gate with `#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]`.
- Forgetting to `SIG_IGN` SIGPIPE → daemon exits when downstream consumer closes a pipe. Set globally on startup.
- Calling `sd_notify` on Mac → link error if linked unconditionally. Already gated; preserve gating.

title: [E6.1] Refactor signals.rs through ServiceManager; sd_notify gated to Linux
description: Refactor src/daemon/signals.rs to take a ServiceManager reference; signals dispatch to platform-specific notification methods (sd_notify on Linux, no-op on Mac since launchd does not require ready/watchdog notifications). Linux signal handling code stays gated; new dispatch surface is platform-agnostic.

title: [E6.2] Conditional SIGINFO handler on Mac
description: Add SIGINFO handler on Mac (Linux ignores SIGINFO since on Linux value 30 is SIGPWR, conflicting). Conditionally compile SIGINFO handler only on macOS via cfg(target_os macos). Handler triggers status dump.

title: [E6.3] SIGINFO emits JSON status dump to stderr
description: Status-dump-on-SIGINFO writes valid JSON to stderr (so it can be captured to a file with shell redirect). Useful when running in foreground for debugging — press Ctrl+T (terminal sends SIGINFO).

title: [E6.4] Signal handler integration test
description: Test: spawn daemon in foreground, send SIGTERM/SIGHUP/SIGINFO via kill -SIGNAL pid, assert behavior. SIGTERM exits 0 within 5s. SIGHUP reloads config (verify via log). SIGINFO emits JSON to stderr with current status snapshot.

title: Comprehensive unit test suite for all modules
description:
## Deliverable
Comprehensive unit and property tests co-located with each module, with deterministic fixtures and detailed failure logging.

## Scope Expansion (Plan-Space Revision)
The unit-test matrix must cover both baseline and advanced tracks:
- core config/errors/platform
- monitor (EWMA, PID, predictive policy)
- scanner (walker, patterns, scoring, deletion)
- ballast and release logic
- logger and stats layers
- decision-plane guards, scheduler, and evidence schema

## Test Families
### Core/Foundation
- error code stability and serialization compatibility
- config parsing/validation/override precedence
- platform trait behavior with mocks

### Monitoring and Control
- EWMA trend/forecast correctness
- PID hysteresis and anti-windup
- predictive policy mode transitions
- conformal/e-process guard behavior

### Scanner and Cleanup
- walker safety invariants (symlink/cross-device/exclusions)
- scoring determinism + veto invariants
- deletion safety/circuit-breaker behavior
- incremental-index equivalence properties

### Logging/Explainability
- SQLite/JSONL dual-write consistency
- evidence-ledger schema compatibility
- explain rendering stability and JSON schema checks

## Detailed Logging Contract
Every failing unit/property test logs seed, minimized fixture, invariant delta, and trace IDs when available.

## Acceptance Criteria
- deterministic and non-flaky unit/property tests
- explicit coverage for advanced decision-plane features
- regression fixtures for discovered edge cases
- CI hard-fail on invariant violations
- machine-readable failure logs suitable for replay
acceptance_criteria: 1. Every core module has deterministic unit coverage including error and boundary paths. 2. Property/invariant tests cover scoring, control logic, and safety veto semantics. 3. Golden fixtures remain stable across runs and are versioned for reproducibility. 4. Test logs include case identifiers, seed info, and failure context sufficient for replay. 5. Coverage report maps each feature bead to at least one unit/property test group.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
REVIEW: Clarifying the role of this bead vs module-level tests:

Each implementation bead (bd-x9z, bd-3po, bd-25g, etc.) MUST include its own unit tests as part of its deliverable — every bead's acceptance criteria already requires tests.

This bead (bd-7ls) serves three distinct purposes that go BEYOND individual module tests:

1. CROSS-CUTTING TEST INFRASTRUCTURE: Create shared test helpers used by many modules:
   - MockPlatform (comprehensive: configurable mount points, memory, fs stats)
   - TestEnvironment builder (creates realistic directory trees with varied ages, sizes, patterns)
   - create_fake_rust_target() — builds a realistic target dir with incremental/, deps/, .fingerprint/
   - SyntheticTimeSeries — generates pressure patterns for EWMA/PID testing
   These helpers live in a tests/common/ module and are imported by all tests.

2. COVERAGE VERIFICATION: Run cargo-llvm-cov and verify coverage thresholds:
   - Overall: > 80%
   - Scoring engine (bd-x9z): > 90%
   - Pattern matching (bd-1sw): > 95%
   - PID controller (bd-3po): > 90%

3. GAP-FILLING TESTS: After all modules are implemented, review coverage reports and add tests for any uncovered edge cases, especially:
   - Cross-module interactions not caught by unit tests
   - Error paths that require multiple components to fail simultaneously
   - Boundary conditions at the intersections of modules

title: Post-event config analysis and auto-tuning recommendations
description:
## Deliverable
After each pressure event, automatically analyze what happened and generate actionable config recommendations. Makes PID controller tuning approachable for non-control-theory users.

## The Problem
sbh exposes PID gains (kp, ki, kd), hysteresis bands, scoring weights, ballast sizing, and threshold percentages. These are powerful but intimidating. Most users will never tune them. Suboptimal defaults for a specific machine's workload pattern make sbh either too aggressive or too passive.

## The Solution

### Automatic Post-Event Analysis
After each pressure event (transition from Green to elevated level and back), analyze:
1. Duration of the event
2. Peak pressure level reached
3. Ballast files released vs available
4. Number of artifacts deleted, their scores, and categories
5. Time from threshold breach to resolution
6. PID controller integral term behavior (did it reach clamp?)
7. EWMA prediction accuracy (did we see it coming?)

### Generated Recommendations
Stored in SQLite activity_log, surfaced via CLI:

**Ballast sizing:**
- "Ballast exhausted 8 min before pressure resolved on /data. Suggest: ballast.file_count = 15 (currently 10)"
- "Ballast was never fully used — 8/10 files always available. Suggest: ballast.file_count = 6 to save 4 GB"

**PID tuning:**
- "PID integral reached clamp 4 times this week without resolving. Suggest: pid_ki = 0.15 (currently 0.1)"
- "Response oscillated between Orange and Yellow 12 times. Suggest: hysteresis_band_pct = 3.0 (currently 2.0)"

**Scoring:**
- "12 artifacts were deleted during active compilation (age < 45 min). Suggest: min_file_age_minutes = 45 (currently 30)"
- "95% of freed space came from RustTarget category. Other patterns rarely match — scan is efficient."

**Threshold:**
- "Pressure stayed Yellow for 4+ hours without escalation. Suggest: yellow_threshold_pct = 18.0 (currently 15.0)"

### CLI Interface
  sbh tune                    # Show current recommendations
  sbh tune --apply            # Apply all recommendations (with confirmation)
  sbh tune --apply --yes      # Apply without confirmation
  sbh tune --history          # Show past recommendations and whether they were applied
  sbh tune --json             # Machine-readable output

### Implementation
```rust
pub struct TuningEngine {
    stats: Arc<StatsEngine>,
    config: Config,
}

pub struct Recommendation {
    pub category: TuningCategory,    // Ballast, PID, Scoring, Threshold
    pub config_key: String,          // e.g., "ballast.file_count"
    pub current_value: String,
    pub suggested_value: String,
    pub rationale: String,           // Plain English explanation
    pub confidence: f64,             // 0.0-1.0
    pub evidence: Vec<String>,       // Supporting data points
    pub risk: TuningRisk,            // Low, Medium, High
}

pub enum TuningRisk {
    Low,    // Safe to apply automatically
    Medium, // Review recommended
    High,   // Manual review required
}
```

### Analysis Triggers
- After each pressure event resolution (Green → Elevated → Green)
- On weekly schedule (for long-term trend analysis)
- On demand via sbh tune --analyze

### Learning from User Feedback
Track which recommendations users accept/reject:
- Accepted: reinforce similar recommendations
- Rejected: reduce confidence for that category
- Applied then reverted: strong negative signal

## Design Rationale
This is the difference between "works OK with defaults" and "gets better over time." The data exists in the SQLite activity database — this is pure analysis. Users who don't understand PID controllers get concrete, actionable suggestions. Self-improving systems feel magical.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Generates recommendations after pressure events
- Recommendations include plain-English rationale
- sbh tune shows current recommendations with confidence levels
- sbh tune --apply modifies config file correctly
- Recommendations based on actual historical data (not hypothetical)
- Handles edge cases: no pressure events yet, insufficient data, conflicting signals
- Risk level assigned to each recommendation
- Unit tests: recommendation generation with synthetic event data
- Integration test: simulate pressure event -> verify recommendation generated
- Test: apply recommendation -> verify config updated -> verify new behavior

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Test coverage requirements: (1) Unit test: generate synthetic PressureEvent sequence -> verify TuningEngine produces correct recommendations for ballast sizing, PID gains, scoring weights. (2) Unit test: conflicting signals (oscillation + integral clamp) -> verify recommendation priority/risk. (3) Integration test: populate SQLite with realistic event data -> run TuningEngine -> verify recommendations match expected output. (4) Test: sbh tune --apply modifies config file atomically (write to .tmp, rename). (5) Test: insufficient data (<3 events) -> TuningEngine returns empty recommendations with 'insufficient data' message.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: MINOR: Config-write capability — sbh tune --apply needs to modify the TOML config file. Implementation: (1) Read existing config file as raw TOML string. (2) Use toml_edit crate (preserves formatting, comments) to modify specific values. (3) Write to .tmp file, then atomic rename. (4) Log each changed value with old and new values. NOTE: toml_edit must be added as a dependency in bd-1kn scaffolding. It is NOT the same as the toml crate (toml parses, toml_edit preserves structure).
