title: [macOS E10] Active build / open file handle awareness
description:
# Epic 10: Active build / open-file-handle awareness on macOS

## The problem this solves

The 2026-05-03 incident showed: `/private/tmp/ft-{cod1..9,cc1..3}-target` (~330 GB) were all actively being written by parallel claude/codex agents running cargo builds. Newest files were 0-4 hours old. Even though they matched the "cargo target dir under /private/tmp" pattern (would normally be reclaimable), nuking them would have:
1. Broken every active cargo build (rustc would error, fail tests, lose work).
2. Not actually freed disk space immediately — open file handles in cargo/rustc keep the inode alive; deleted files appear gone but `df` still counts them until those processes exit.
3. Possibly corrupted build state (cargo's internal lockfile + dep-info graph would think files exist that don't).

## What sbh must do

Before reclaiming any candidate, check:
1. Are any open file handles inside this dir tree? (use libproc proc_pidfdinfo — see E4)
2. Are any process executables (binaries) currently being executed from this dir? (`proc_pidpath` + ancestor check)
3. Are any mmap'd regions backed by files in this dir? (`proc_pidregioninfo`)
4. Is anything inside the dir mtime'd within the last (configurable, default 60 min)?

If any check positive → downgrade reclaim confidence; emit explanation in scan output.

## Cross-platform considerations

The same logic applies on Linux (lsof / `/proc/<pid>/fd/`) but the implementation differs. Build a `FileHandleInspector` trait in PAL with two impls.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. PAL trait method: `pal.open_files_under(path: &Path) -> Result<Vec<OpenFile>>`. Mac impl uses `proc_pidlistfds` + `proc_pidfdinfo`. Linux impl uses `/proc/*/fd/`.
2. PAL trait method: `pal.executables_under(path: &Path) -> Result<Vec<ProcessInfo>>`. Mac uses `proc_pidpath` for each running PID and prefix-match. Linux uses `/proc/*/exe` symlinks.
3. PAL trait method: `pal.mmap_regions_under(path: &Path) -> Result<Vec<MappedRegion>>`. Mac uses `proc_pidregioninfo` iter. Linux uses `/proc/*/maps`.
4. Cleanup confidence engine integrates these checks: any positive → confidence downgrade with structured reason.
5. CLI: `sbh scan --explain` shows full confidence trace including which check fired which downgrade.
6. Test: spawn a process holding an open fd inside a synthetic test dir, run scan, assert dir is NOT in `Definite` reclaim set.

## Performance considerations

`proc_pidlistfds` for every PID can be slow (1000 PIDs × proc_pidlistfds = 1-2 seconds). Mitigations:
- Only run this check on candidates that pass other filters (reduces N).
- Cache the global open-file index for 30 seconds (refresh on demand).
- Limit to candidates above size threshold (default 100 MB) — small dirs aren't worth the check.

## Failure modes

- Process opens fd, sbh checks (no handle), process opens fd, sbh deletes — TOCTOU race. Mitigation: do final fd check immediately before unlink, in the same lookup pass.
- mmap'd binary in `/private/tmp/ft-cod7-target/debug/deps/foo-abc.dylib` keeps the inode alive after rm. Mitigation: refuse to rm dirs with mmap'd executables; require operator opt-in.
- LaunchDaemon visibility: as user-scope LaunchAgent, sbh can't see other users' fds. Document this limitation and adjust confidence accordingly when running user-scope.

title: [E10.1] PAL open_files_under() with Mac impl
description: PAL trait method: pal.open_files_under(path) returning Vec<OpenFile>. Mac impl uses proc_pidlistfds + proc_pidfdinfo (depends on E4.7). Linux impl walks /proc/*/fd/. OpenFile struct: { pid, fd, path, mode, fd_type }.

title: [E10.2] PAL executables_under() with Mac impl
description: PAL trait method: pal.executables_under(path) returning Vec<ProcessInfo>. Mac uses proc_pidpath for each running PID + prefix-match. Linux uses /proc/*/exe symlinks. Returns processes whose executable lives under path. Useful for: dont reclaim a dir that has running binaries inside (those binaries would crash on next syscall).

title: [E10.3] PAL mmap_regions_under() with Mac impl
description: PAL trait method: pal.mmap_regions_under(path) returning Vec<MappedRegion>. Mac uses proc_pidregioninfo iter (proc_pidinfo with PROC_PIDREGIONINFO). Linux uses /proc/*/maps parse. Returns memory-mapped file regions backed by files under path. Critical: mmap holds inode alive after rm; reclaiming such files is futile.

title: [E10.4] Cleanup confidence engine integrates fd/exec/mmap checks
description: Cleanup confidence engine integrates these checks: any positive (open fd, running exec, mmap region) downgrades confidence with structured reason. Reason embedded in scan output: e.g. "Cannot reclaim safely: pid 12345 (rustc) has 3 open file descriptors here, 1 mmap region".

title: [E10.5] sbh scan --explain shows confidence trace
description: CLI: sbh scan --explain shows full confidence trace including which check fired which downgrade. Default scan output is concise; --explain is verbose. Verbose includes: matched cleanup rule, mtime check result, fd check result, exec check result, mmap check result, sacred overlap check result, final confidence.

title: [E10.6] Tests: open fd, running exec, mmap region all downgrade correctly
description: Test: spawn process holding open fd inside synthetic test dir, run scan, assert dir is NOT in Definite reclaim set. Spawn binary running from test dir, run scan, assert dir flagged with running-exec reason. Test mmap-backed region similarly.

title: [E10.7] Performance: cache + size-threshold gating for fd checks
description: Performance: cache global open-file index for 30 seconds; refresh on demand. Limit fd-check to candidates above size threshold (default 100 MB) — small dirs not worth the check.

title: [E10.8] User-scope LaunchAgent visibility limitations + accurate reporting
description: User-scope LaunchAgent visibility: as user-scope, sbh sees only own-user processes fds. Document; emit info-level log when other-user dirs cant be checked. Adjust confidence accordingly: "fd check incomplete: other-user processes not visible".

title: Implement live dashboard command and status watch loop
description: Replace dashboard stub with live-refresh implementation, reuse status rendering path, honor dashboard refresh_ms, and make status --watch functional in human mode with clear output and interval control.
close_reason: Completed

title: Pre-build disk space check command (sbh check)
description:
## Deliverable
A fast (<50ms) command that checks if there is enough disk space for a build, designed for use as a pre-build gate.

## Usage
  sbh check                     # Check default paths, exit 0 if OK
  sbh check /data               # Check specific path
  sbh check --need 20G          # Check if 20GB available
  sbh check --predict 60        # Check if EWMA predicts enough space for 60 min
  sbh check && cargo build      # Pre-build gate
  alias cb='sbh check && cargo build'  # Shell alias

## Exit Codes
  0 = OK (enough space)
  1 = WARNING (space available but predicted to run out within --predict window)
  2 = CRITICAL (insufficient space right now)

## Output (stderr, non-blocking)
  On success: nothing (silent)
  On warning: "sbh: /data has 12.3 GB free but predicted full in 45 min"
  On critical: "sbh: /data has 1.2 GB free (0.8%). Run: sbh emergency /data"

## Implementation
- Read state.json for daemon data (EWMA predictions) if available
- Fallback: direct statvfs call if daemon not running
- Must complete in <50ms (no scanning, no database, just fs stats + prediction check)
- JSON output with --json flag for agent consumption

## Design Rationale
Prevents the problem entirely rather than cleaning up after. Users can integrate into their workflow with a one-line alias. Agents can use it as a pre-build check in their workflows. The <50ms latency requirement means it adds negligible overhead to build commands.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Completes in <50ms (benchmarked)
- Correct exit codes for all scenarios
- Works without daemon running (degraded mode, no prediction)
- Works with daemon running (uses EWMA prediction from state.json)
- --need flag checks absolute space requirement
- --predict flag checks time-to-exhaustion
- --json output for agent consumption
- Silent on success (no stdout, no stderr)
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests cover threshold math, target-free calculations, and exit-code semantics. 2. Integration tests validate coupling with fs stats, notifier, and CLI formatting layers. 3. E2E scenarios simulate low-space and healthy-space runs with --json and human outputs. 4. Command never mutates disk state and returns deterministic recommendations for fixed inputs. 5. Detailed logs include free/used snapshots, threshold comparisons, and recommended next actions.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW: Removed bd-x1k (EWMA) dependency. sbh check reads daemon state from state.json (bd-2np) which already contains EWMA predictions. When daemon is not running, sbh check falls back to direct statvfs() — no EWMA needed. The check command does NOT import or run the EWMA module; it only reads pre-computed predictions from state.json. Dependencies: bd-2rq (CLI framework) + bd-2pj (fs stats for fallback mode).

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW-2: state.json format contract is defined in bd-2np. sbh check reads it opportunistically — if the file exists and is fresh (<30s old), use EWMA predictions for --predict flag; otherwise fall back to direct statvfs(). No hard dep on bd-2np needed since the read is optional with graceful fallback.

title: [macOS E9] Sacred path detection (don't lose user data)
description:
# Epic 9: Sacred path detection on macOS (don't lose user data)

## Why this is its own epic, not just part of E8

Cleanup heuristics (E8) say "what's safe to reclaim". Sacred path detection (this epic) says "what is NEVER touched, no matter what". These are complementary and orthogonal. Sacred paths win. If a cleanup rule's glob would match a sacred path, the cleanup rule is suppressed.

The 2026-05-03 incident exposed a sacred-path gap: the `/private/tmp/frankenterm-trash-20260503-092725` dir contained `.beads/` and `beads.db` files. Per the operator's auto-memory: "coding-agent-search data is SACRED — NEVER delete database, backups, repair-lab, snapshots, or any related files." But sbh wouldn't have known to look inside an "ephemeral" looking dir for sacred data.

## Sacred path categories on macOS

| Category | Paths | Why sacred |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| Photos library | `~/Pictures/*.photoslibrary/`, `~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudPhotos*/` | Irreplaceable; iCloud sync issues if mauled |
| Messages | `~/Library/Messages/`, `~/Library/Containers/com.apple.iChat/` | Conversations history |
| Mail | `~/Library/Mail/`, `~/Library/Containers/com.apple.mail/` | IMAP cache + local-only mail |
| Notes | `~/Library/Group Containers/group.com.apple.notes/` | Local notes |
| Reminders | `~/Library/Calendars/`, `~/Library/Group Containers/group.com.apple.reminders/` | Calendar + reminders |
| iCloud Drive | `~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/` | Live cloud sync — destructive ops propagate |
| Final Cut / iMovie | `~/Movies/*.fcpbundle/`, `~/Movies/*.imovielibrary/` | Video projects, hours of work |
| Logic / GarageBand | `~/Music/Logic/`, `~/Music/GarageBand/` | Audio projects |
| Photos auxiliary | `~/Pictures/Lightroom*/`, `~/Pictures/Capture One*/` | Photo libraries |
| Time Machine local | (handled separately — sbh CAN thin these) | — |
| Beads / agent data | Anywhere `.beads/`, `beads.db`, `beads.db-wal` | Operator-explicit: "agent search data is SACRED" |
| Git data | Anywhere `.git/` | Repo state |
| SQLite databases | Anywhere `*.db`, `*.sqlite`, `*.sqlite3` (with WAL/SHM siblings) | Generally application state |
| SSH keys | `~/.ssh/` | Credentials |
| GPG / age keys | `~/.gnupg/`, `~/.config/age/` | Credentials |
| Browser profiles | `~/Library/Application Support/{Firefox,Google/Chrome,BraveSoftware}/Profiles/` | Bookmarks, history, extensions, saved passwords |
| Tax / Finance | `~/Documents/**/{tax,Tax,TAX,*.tax,*.qbo,*.qbb,*.cpa}*` | Year of tax data |
| Operator-marked | Anywhere with `.sbh-protect` marker file | Explicit user opt-in protection |

## Required sbh behavior

- Sacred paths are loaded from a built-in catalog + user config (`~/.config/sbh/sacred.toml` or `/etc/sbh/sacred.toml`).
- Before any cleanup action, the candidate path is checked against sacred paths. If overlap (path is sacred, contains sacred, or is contained-by sacred), action is suppressed.
- Stowaway detection: when scanning a candidate dir for cleanup, walk inside (depth-limited) looking for sacred markers (`.beads/`, `.git/`, `*.db`, `*.sqlite`). If any present, downgrade confidence to `Unclear` with explanation.
- `sbh protect <path>` writes a marker file (`.sbh-protect`) and adds to user config. `sbh unprotect <path>` reverses.
- `sbh status --sacred` lists active sacred-path patterns + counts overlaps with current scan candidates.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. Define `SacredPath` data type: `{ pattern, kind: ExactMatch | GlobMatch | ContainsAny | StowawayMarker, reason, source: Builtin | UserConfig | Marker }`.
2. Build the macOS sacred catalog (data file in src/platform/macos/sacred.toml or similar, embedded at compile time).
3. Build the cross-platform sacred catalog (`.git`, `.beads`, `*.db`, etc.) shared across OSes.
4. Stowaway scanner: depth-limited (default 3) directory walk looking for sacred markers. Used during cleanup confidence scoring.
5. Sacred-path overlap engine: given a candidate path and a sacred-path catalog, return all overlap matches (path is a parent of sacred, path is a child of sacred, path *contains* sacred, etc.).
6. CLI: `sbh protect <path>`, `sbh unprotect <path>`, `sbh status --sacred`.
7. Marker file `.sbh-protect` syntax: TOML or empty file (presence enough). Empty file is enough; if user wants reasons they can put TOML inside.
8. Test: synthetic dir with `.beads/` inside `*-trash-*` parent, scan, assert downgraded confidence + reason mentions stowaway.

## Why this is a hard problem

Path-based protection is fragile:
- Symlinks (path looks ephemeral but resolves to sacred).
- Bind mounts (Linux) / firmlinks (Mac).
- Container mounts (Docker volumes).
- Renames (operator renames `~/Pictures/Photos Library.photoslibrary` to `~/Pictures/Old Library.photoslibrary` — pattern miss).

Mitigation: in addition to path patterns, use *content* signals (extension, magic bytes, presence of marker subdirs) where the cost is reasonable.

## Failure mode that would be a disaster

Mistakenly classifying `~/Pictures/Photos Library.photoslibrary` as a "stale build dir" and reclaiming. Recovery: only via Time Machine. Operator confidence in sbh: gone forever.

Mitigation: list of bundle extensions (`.photoslibrary`, `.fcpbundle`, `.imovielibrary`, `.app`, `.framework`, ...) is a hard refuse-list. Even with mtime old, even with size huge, sbh refuses to touch.

title: [E9.1] Define SacredPath data type
description: Define SacredPath data type: { pattern, kind: ExactMatch | GlobMatch | ContainsAny | StowawayMarker, reason, source: Builtin | UserConfig | Marker }. Stored in src/platform/types.rs as cross-platform type. Each variant documented with example.

title: [E9.2] Build macOS sacred catalog (built-in patterns)
description: Build the macOS sacred catalog (data file in src/platform/macos/sacred.toml or similar, embedded at compile time via include_str). Categories: Photos library (.photoslibrary), Mail (~/Library/Mail/), Messages (~/Library/Messages/), Notes (~/Library/Group Containers/group.com.apple.notes/), Reminders, iCloud Drive (~/Library/Mobile Documents/), Final Cut/iMovie bundles (.fcpbundle, .imovielibrary), Logic/GarageBand (~/Music/), photo libraries (Lightroom, Capture One), tax/finance Documents patterns.

title: [E9.3] Build cross-platform sacred catalog (.git, .beads, *.db, etc)
description: Build the cross-platform sacred catalog (.git, .beads, *.db, *.sqlite, *.sqlite3, ~/.ssh/, ~/.gnupg/, ~/.config/age/). These apply on all OSes; loaded into PAL alongside per-OS catalog.

title: [E9.4] Stowaway scanner (find sacred markers in candidate dir)
description: Stowaway scanner: depth-limited (default 3) directory walk looking for sacred markers. Used during cleanup confidence scoring. Returns Vec<StowawayMatch> for caller to render in scan output. Performance: short-circuit on first match by default; --explain flag walks fully.

title: [E9.5] Sacred-path overlap engine + confidence downgrade integration
description: Sacred-path overlap engine: given a candidate path and a sacred-path catalog, return all overlap matches (path is parent of sacred, path is child of sacred, path contains sacred via stowaway, etc.). Returns SacredOverlap enum that drives confidence downgrade.

title: [E9.6] sbh protect / unprotect / status --sacred CLI
description: CLI: sbh protect <path>, sbh unprotect <path>, sbh status --sacred. Protect creates marker file (.sbh-protect) in path AND adds to user config (~/.config/sbh/sacred.toml). Unprotect reverses. status --sacred lists active patterns + counts overlaps with current scan candidates.

title: [E9.7] .sbh-protect marker file format + parser
description: Marker file .sbh-protect syntax: empty file = enough; if user wants reasons they can put TOML inside (e.g., reason = "year-end backup", added_by = "jemanuel"). Document in user-facing docs.

title: [E9.8] Tests: stowaway, parent-of-sacred, child-of-sacred, exact-match
description: Test: synthetic dir with .beads/ inside *-trash-* parent, scan, assert downgraded confidence + reason mentions stowaway. Test: candidate exactly matches Photos library bundle pattern, assert refused. Test: candidate is parent of .git, assert downgraded.

title: [E9.9] Bundle extension hard refuse-list
description: Bundle extension hard refuse-list: extensions like .photoslibrary, .fcpbundle, .imovielibrary, .app, .framework, .bundle, .plugin, .kext, .lrcat, .lrlibrary, .aplibrary. Even with mtime old, even with size huge, sbh refuses to touch. Apple uses these extensions as "this is a database / project, not a regular folder" signal — we must respect.

title: [macOS E12] Ballast file management on APFS
description:
# Epic 12: Ballast file management on macOS (APFS)

## What ballast does

sbh's "ballast" feature reserves disk space ahead of time by creating a large file (configurable, default 5 GB). When disk pressure spikes, sbh deletes the ballast to instantly free that space, buying time for orderly remediation. Without ballast, the moment an OOD (out-of-disk) happens, all writers fail simultaneously and the system can wedge.

`src/ballast/manager.rs` has Linux-specific code: uses `fallocate(2)` to reserve space without writing zeros (instant), and `BTRFS_IOC_*` ioctls on btrfs for additional optimizations.

## APFS specifics

1. **Sparse files are default.** `lseek(SEEK_END)` + `write` doesn't reserve space. Ballast must use `F_PREALLOCATE` fcntl with `fst_flags = F_ALLOCATEALL | F_ALLOCATECONTIG` to actually consume bytes.
2. **`F_PREALLOCATE` semantics:** allocates blocks but does not zero them. Bytes consumed; reads return zero or arbitrary depending on fs state. Unlinking truly frees.
3. **APFS clones (`cp -c`).** Two files can share blocks via copy-on-write clone. Ballast must NOT be a clone of any other file (defeats the purpose). Verify by checking shared block count after creation.
4. **APFS snapshots retention.** If a Time Machine local snapshot exists between ballast creation and deletion, the ballast bytes are not actually freed (snapshot retains them). Solution: thin snapshots before relying on ballast deletion; or place ballast on a volume that's snapshot-excluded.
5. **Sparse bundle considerations.** Sparse bundles (used by Time Machine to network drives) have different semantics — but we don't ballast on those.

## Required sbh behavior

- `sbh ballast create` on macOS uses `F_PREALLOCATE` to reserve N GB on the chosen volume.
- Default ballast location: `/private/var/sbh/ballast.bin` (system-scope) or `~/Library/Application Support/sbh/ballast.bin` (user-scope).
- `sbh ballast verify` checks the ballast file consumes the expected bytes (not a sparse hole).
- `sbh ballast release` unlinks + warns if local snapshots exist that retain bytes.
- Daemon auto-recreates ballast when disk pressure clears (configurable).
- Document: "After ballast release, free space won't show in df until: (a) all snapshots referencing ballast bytes are thinned, or (b) the snapshot retention window expires (default 24h)."

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. PAL method: `pal.preallocate_file(path: &Path, size: u64) -> Result<()>`. Mac impl uses `F_PREALLOCATE` fcntl. Linux impl uses `fallocate`.
2. PAL method: `pal.file_block_count(path: &Path) -> Result<u64>` — returns actual blocks consumed (not file size). Mac uses `stat::st_blocks`. Linux same.
3. APFS snapshot interaction: warn user if `tmutil listlocalsnapshots <volume>` returns non-empty when releasing ballast.
4. Refactor `ballast/manager.rs` to use PAL methods instead of direct `fallocate`.
5. Test on actual APFS volume: create 1 GB ballast, verify `du -sh` shows 1 GB, release, verify free space increases.
6. Edge case: APFS volume nearly full, attempt to create ballast. Expect ENOSPC. Daemon must fall back gracefully (don't crash; log; skip ballast; continue with other defenses).
7. Document ballast in user docs with the snapshot caveat prominently.

## Why ballast is critical on macOS specifically

Macs run lots of apps simultaneously — when disk fills, multiple apps get write errors at once and the GUI can become unresponsive. Browsers can't write cookies, mail can't fetch new messages, IDE save fails, system services that write to /var/db get errors. Ballast buys 5-30 seconds of "magically more space" while sbh runs the cleanup; that's enough to keep the GUI responsive.

## Failure modes

- `F_PREALLOCATE` succeeds but file is still sparse → bytes not actually reserved. Verify with `stat::st_blocks * blocksize == requested_size`.
- Ballast on a snapshot-included volume → release doesn't free bytes. Detect snapshots; warn at create time; offer to disable Time Machine snapshots for the ballast path (advanced; opt-in).
- Multiple sbh instances each creating their own ballast → wasteful. Use a lockfile sibling to ballast file.

title: [E12.1] PAL preallocate_file() with Mac F_PREALLOCATE impl
description: PAL method: pal.preallocate_file(path, size). Mac impl uses F_PREALLOCATE fcntl with fst_flags = F_ALLOCATEALL | F_ALLOCATECONTIG to actually reserve disk blocks (not sparse). Then ftruncate to size. Linux impl uses fallocate(2). Both verify final st_blocks * blocksize equals or exceeds requested size.

title: [E12.2] PAL file_block_count() (verify non-sparse ballast)
description: PAL method: pal.file_block_count(path). Returns actual blocks consumed (not file size). Mac uses stat::st_blocks. Linux same. Used to verify ballast is not sparse.

title: [E12.3] APFS snapshot interaction warning at ballast release
description: APFS snapshot interaction: warn user if tmutil listlocalsnapshots <volume> returns non-empty when releasing ballast. Reason: snapshot retains the released bytes until snapshot expires (24h default) or is thinned.

title: [E12.4] Refactor ballast/manager.rs to use PAL methods
description: Refactor src/ballast/manager.rs to use PAL methods (preallocate_file + file_block_count) instead of direct fallocate. Linux behavior identical (refactor only). Mac behavior is now functional.

title: [E12.5] APFS ballast integration test
description: Test on actual APFS volume: create 1 GB ballast, verify st_blocks accounts for 1 GB consumed, release, verify free space increases. Skip on non-APFS test environments. Run on macos-latest CI.

title: [E12.6] ENOSPC edge case: graceful ballast skip
description: Edge case: APFS volume nearly full, attempt to create ballast. Expect ENOSPC. Daemon must fall back gracefully (do not crash; log; skip ballast; continue with other defenses).

title: [E12.7] User docs: ballast + APFS snapshot caveat
description: Document ballast in user docs with the snapshot caveat prominently. Section: "After ballast release, free space wont show in df until: (a) all snapshots referencing ballast bytes are thinned, or (b) the snapshot retention window expires (default 24h)."

title: [E12.8] Default ballast location per scope (user vs system)
description: Default ballast location selection: per scope. user-scope LaunchAgent: ~/Library/Application Support/sbh/ballast.bin. system-scope LaunchDaemon: /private/var/sbh/ballast.bin. Document; configurable via config file.

title: [macOS E3] Memory pressure detection (sysctl + Mach + dispatch_source)
description:
# Epic 3: Memory pressure detection on macOS

## Why this matters for a *disk* helper

sbh's daemon coordinates with memory pressure: when the system is under memory pressure, dropping disk caches won't help and may make things worse (fewer page cache hits, more disk I/O). Conversely, when memory is under pressure *and* swap is filling disk, sbh might escalate disk reclamation. On Linux we read `/proc/pressure/memory`. On Mac there is no such file.

The 2026-05-03 Mac incident showed: `Pages free: 7144 out of 4194304` (essentially exhausted), Swap I/O: `Swapouts: 50914797` (50.9M page units swapped out — hundreds of GB written to swap). The system was thrashing badly. sbh on Mac had no signal of this and couldn't have correlated disk pressure with memory pressure.

## Mac memory pressure surfaces

1. **`memory_pressure(1)` command.** Outputs page-level free/active/inactive/wired stats and Swap I/O totals. Easy to parse but slow (forks). Acceptable for occasional polling, not for hot path.
2. **`vm_stat(1)` command.** Mach VM stats. Same data, also forks.
3. **`sysctl` — `vm.swapusage`, `vm.compressor_bytes_used`, `vm.compressor_compressions`, `vm.page_free_count`, etc.** Available via `sysctl`/`sysctlbyname` syscall — fast, no fork.
4. **`host_statistics64(HOST_VM_INFO64)` Mach call.** Returns `vm_statistics64_data_t` directly. Used by `vm_stat`. Native, fast.
5. **Memory pressure notifications via `dispatch_source_create(DISPATCH_SOURCE_TYPE_MEMORYPRESSURE, ...)`.** Event-driven; system fires when pressure transitions Normal → Warn → Critical. This is what well-behaved Mac apps subscribe to.

## Required sbh behavior

- Daemon polls memory pressure on its existing tick (no new threads).
- Daemon also subscribes to `DISPATCH_SOURCE_TYPE_MEMORYPRESSURE` so transitions fire promptly between polls.
- `sbh status` reports current pressure level (Normal/Warn/Critical) + numerical metrics (free pages, compressor bytes used, swap used).
- `sbh blame` correlates: "Process X opened 4.2 GB of files in last 60s while memory was Critical and swap grew by 1.1 GB."
- Cleanup decisions become memory-aware: under critical memory pressure, prefer cleanups that don't require reading large files (deleting whole dirs by inode, not scanning).

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. `sysctl`/`sysctlbyname` FFI bindings (raw libc).
2. `host_statistics64` Mach FFI bindings.
3. Parse `vm.swapusage` (format: `total = 12288.00M  used = 5230.50M  free = 7057.50M  (encrypted)`).
4. Build `MemoryPressure` struct mirroring Linux's representation: `{ level: Normal|Warn|Critical, free_pages, used_pages, page_size, compressor_used_bytes, swap_total, swap_used }`.
5. `dispatch_source` event subscription for pressure transitions (need `dispatch` crate or write thin Objective-C bridge).
6. Cross-platform `MemoryPressure` enum mapping: Linux's avg10 thresholds → Mac's MemoryPressure level mapping. Document the mapping rationale in code.
7. Daemon integration: existing memory-pressure handler in `src/daemon/loop_main.rs` becomes platform-agnostic.
8. Snapshot test: capture `MemoryPressure` JSON output, freeze it, ensure schema doesn't drift.

## Important subtlety

macOS treats "compressed memory" as not-swap. Mac compresses cold pages in RAM (compressor) before swapping to disk. A Mac with 16 GB RAM can have 30 GB of working set without swapping if compression ratio is 2x. Linux PSI doesn't have a direct equivalent. We must NOT treat compressor-bytes-used as "swap" in our cross-platform data model — it's a separate field. Document this clearly.

## Failure modes

- Treating Mac compressed memory as swap → false swap pressure alarms.
- Polling memory_pressure shell command in a tight loop → fork storm, visible in Activity Monitor as sbh consuming 5% CPU.
- Subscribing to `DISPATCH_SOURCE_TYPE_MEMORYPRESSURE` but not handling rate-limiting → flapping notifications during continuous warn-level pressure.

title: [E3.1] sysctl/sysctlbyname FFI bindings
description: Add FFI for libc::sysctl and sysctlbyname (libc crate has these). Wrap in safe Rust helper sysctl::read::<T>(name: &str) -> Result<T> with size validation. Test: read hw.memsize (u64), hw.physicalcpu (i32), vm.swapusage (string), assert plausible values on macOS. Why both forms: many keys are easier as named (sysctlbyname), some legacy keys are integer-id only (sysctl with mib).

title: [E3.2] host_statistics64 Mach FFI bindings
description: extern "C" bindings for: mach_host_self, host_statistics64, host_page_size. Define vm_statistics64_data_t struct (Mach-specific layout). Wrapper: read_vm_stats() -> VmStats with free_count, active_count, inactive_count, wire_count, compressor_page_count, throttled_count. Reference: <mach/host_info.h>, <mach/vm_statistics.h>. Rust libc may have these; check first. Acceptance: unit test reads stats, asserts free_count + active_count + inactive_count + wire_count approximately equals total pages (within rounding).

title: [E3.3] Parse vm.swapusage sysctl string format
description: vm.swapusage returns: 'total = 8192.00M used = 1234.50M free = 6957.50M (encrypted)'. Parser: extract three byte counts, encrypted flag. Build SwapUsage struct. Edge: format may change in future macOS versions. Keep parser tolerant; if parse fails, return SwapUsage::Unknown rather than crash. Test cases: typical, all-zero, very-large (TB), with/without 'encrypted' suffix.

title: [E3.4] Build cross-platform MemoryPressure struct + Mac impl
description: src/platform/types.rs: MemoryPressure { level, free_bytes, used_bytes, page_size, compressor_used_bytes (mac-only or 0 on linux), swap_total_bytes, swap_used_bytes, source }. PressureLevel enum: Normal, Warn, Critical (mapping to Mac DISPATCH_MEMORYPRESSURE_NORMAL/WARN/CRITICAL). Implement MacOsPal::memory_pressure() composing E3.1 + E3.2 + E3.3 + level inference (heuristic: free pages < 5 percent of total + compressor in heavy use → Warn; <2 percent + swap growing → Critical). Document the level inference heuristic in code comments referencing Apple's own thresholds (Apple does not fully publish; we approximate).

title: [E3.5] dispatch_source memory pressure event subscription
description: Implement MacOsPal::subscribe_memory_pressure(callback). Uses dispatch_source_create with DISPATCH_SOURCE_TYPE_MEMORYPRESSURE. Registers a global dispatch source with mask = NORMAL | WARN | CRITICAL. Bridge to Rust: callback closure stored in static handler; dispatch invokes; handler enqueues to mpsc channel; daemon main loop drains. Use the dispatch crate or write thin Obj-C bridge. Either way, manage lifecycle: subscription handle tied to Drop impl that cancels source. Acceptance: integration test triggers synthetic pressure (allocate big Vec until pressure rises), assert callback fired with new level. Skip on CI runners that don't allow this; run on dev machine.

title: [E3.6] PressureLevel mapping doc: Linux PSI thresholds vs Mac dispatch levels
description: docs/internal/pressure-mapping.md explains how Linux PSI avg10 thresholds (5 percent, 20 percent) map to Mac's NORMAL/WARN/CRITICAL. Decision: keep cross-platform thresholds in BEHAVIOR, not SIGNAL. Linux fires WARN when avg10 >= 5 percent; Mac fires WARN when system declares warn. Both result in same daemon behavior. Document divergence is acceptable.

title: [E3.7] Snapshot test for MemoryPressure JSON output
description: insta snapshot of sbh status --json memory_pressure section. Pin shape; updates require explicit cargo insta accept. Why: prevents unintended schema drift that would break operator dashboards or scripts.

title: [macOS E11] Wizard, install UX, CLI flow
description:
# Epic 11: Wizard, install UX, and CLI flow for macOS

## Why this matters

`src/cli/wizard.rs` (the install wizard) has 6+ `cfg!(target_os = "linux")` branches. The wizard today either (a) assumes Linux/systemd or (b) gives up on non-Linux. Mac users get a confusing experience.

Two goals:
1. **Frictionless install**: `curl -fsSL ... | bash` → working daemon in 30 seconds.
2. **Helpful diagnostics**: every error message tells the user exactly what to do.

## Mac-specific wizard branches

- Detect macOS version (Big Sur+ minimum; we use modern launchctl bootstrap idiom).
- Detect Apple Silicon vs Intel (binary selection).
- Detect FDA (Full Disk Access) status — sbh needs FDA to scan `~/Library/Mail`, `~/Library/Messages`, etc. Without FDA those dirs return 0 bytes silently. The wizard must guide the operator through System Settings → Privacy & Security → Full Disk Access → add sbh.
- Detect homebrew presence (offer brew install path if available).
- Detect existing sbh install (LaunchAgent vs LaunchDaemon vs nothing). Migrate gracefully.
- Detect XDG_DATA_HOME / config locations. macOS convention is `~/Library/Application Support/sbh/` not `~/.config/sbh/`. Support both.

## Required sbh behavior

- `sbh setup` (post-install verification + first-run config) detects and reports: PAL impl, service kind (launchd vs systemd), FDA status, default scope (LaunchAgent), config path, log path.
- `sbh doctor` runs comprehensive checks: binary signed/notarized? launchctl finds the job? plist syntactically valid? logs writable? FDA granted? config valid?
- `sbh install --launchd` is idempotent — running it twice does the right thing.
- `sbh install --launchd --scope=user|system` chooses LaunchAgent vs LaunchDaemon explicitly (default: user).
- `sbh install` warns if running as root with `--scope=user` (accidentally installing for root, not user).
- All error messages reference specific commands to fix.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. Refactor wizard to be PAL-driven instead of cfg-driven (delegates to platform impls for mac vs linux flows).
2. macOS install flow: detect Apple Silicon → recommend arm64 binary → download → verify SHA256 → install → generate plist → bootstrap → kickstart → verify status → done. Print each step's result.
3. FDA status detection: try to read a known FDA-gated path (`~/Library/Mail/V*/MailData/Envelope Index`); if EPERM, FDA missing.
4. FDA grant guide: print step-by-step (open System Settings, navigate to Privacy & Security → Full Disk Access, click +, navigate to /Users/<user>/.local/bin/sbh, toggle on). Include screenshots in docs (linked from CLI output).
5. macOS uninstall flow: bootout → remove plist → optionally clean state dir → done.
6. `sbh doctor` Mac checks: `codesign -dv $(which sbh)` to verify signature; `spctl -a` for notarization; `launchctl print` to verify daemon; FDA check; APFS detection; sufficient disk free for state.
7. Config path detection: prefer `~/Library/Application Support/sbh/` for state, `~/Library/Preferences/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist` (or TOML in App Support) for config.
8. Migration: if old install exists at unusual path, migrate config + state.
9. Brew tap formula skeleton (links to E15 release engineering for actual publishing).

## Diagnostic specificity examples

Bad message: `error: failed to start daemon`
Good message: `error: launchctl bootstrap returned exit code 5 (Input/output error). The plist at ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist exists but launchctl can't load it. Check plist syntax: plutil -lint ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist`

Bad: `error: scan failed`
Good: `error: scan failed for ~/Library/Mail (EPERM). Full Disk Access is required for this path. Grant via: System Settings → Privacy & Security → Full Disk Access → + → /Users/jemanuel/.local/bin/sbh`

## Failure modes

- Wizard runs as root (sudo curl|bash) and installs LaunchDaemon when user wanted LaunchAgent. Mitigation: detect EUID and prompt explicitly.
- FDA check gives false negative on first install before sbh has any state (operator hasn't granted because sbh isn't running yet). Resolve with: install daemon first, then on first scan if FDA missing, surface the prompt.
- Plist generated with non-existent binary path (binary moved). Use absolute path resolved at install time, document the dependency.

title: [E11.1] Refactor wizard.rs to PAL-driven dispatch
description: Refactor wizard.rs to be PAL-driven instead of cfg-driven. Wizard delegates to platform impls (linux::install_wizard, macos::install_wizard) for OS-specific flows. Shared scaffolding (prompts, validation, output formatting) stays cross-platform.

title: [E11.10] Diagnostic specificity in every error message
description: Diagnostic specificity: every error message references a specific command to fix. Bad: error: failed to start daemon. Good: error: launchctl bootstrap returned exit 5 (Input/output error). The plist at PATH exists but launchctl cannot load it. Verify with plutil -lint PATH.

title: [E11.2] macOS install flow (detect arch, download, verify, install, plist, bootstrap)
description: macOS install flow: detect Apple Silicon vs Intel, recommend matching arm64 or x86_64 binary, download from latest GitHub release, verify SHA256, install to ~/.local/bin/sbh, generate plist (E5.2), bootstrap (E5.3), kickstart, verify status, done. Print each step result with ASCII checkmark/X.

title: [E11.3] FDA (Full Disk Access) status detection
description: FDA (Full Disk Access) status detection: try to read a known FDA-gated path (~/Library/Mail/V*/MailData/Envelope Index); if EPERM, FDA missing. Cache result for 60s.

title: [E11.4] FDA grant walkthrough output
description: FDA grant guide: print step-by-step (open System Settings, navigate to Privacy & Security then Full Disk Access, click +, navigate to /Users/USERNAME/.local/bin/sbh, toggle on). Include link to docs page with screenshots. Re-check periodically; emit success log when granted.

title: [E11.5] macOS uninstall flow (idempotent)
description: macOS uninstall flow: bootout via launchctl, remove plist, optionally clean state dir, done. Idempotent. Re-run on already-uninstalled exits 0 with informational message.

title: [E11.6] sbh doctor Mac-specific checks (codesign, spctl, launchctl, FDA)
description: sbh doctor Mac checks: codesign -dv $(which sbh) to verify signature; spctl -a to verify notarization; launchctl print to verify daemon; FDA check; APFS detection; sufficient disk free for state. Report each as PASS/WARN/FAIL with remediation suggestion.

title: [E11.7] Config + state path detection (App Support vs ~/.config)
description: Config path detection: prefer ~/Library/Application Support/sbh/ for state, ~/Library/Preferences/com.dicklesworthstone.sbh.plist (or TOML in App Support) for config. Fall back to ~/.config/sbh/ if user prefers XDG layout. Document precedence.

title: [E11.8] Migration from old install paths
description: Migration: if old install exists at unusual path (e.g., /usr/local/bin/sbh from manual install, or homebrew location, or earlier Linux convention path), migrate config + state to canonical location. Preserve user customizations.

title: [E11.9] Brew tap formula skeleton
description: Brew tap formula skeleton (links to E15 release engineering for actual publishing). Formula installs latest binary, runs sbh setup, prints FDA reminder. Tap repo: Dicklesworthstone/homebrew-sbh.

title: TEST_DELETE_ME
Fix I30: JSON injection via rfind in integrations
close_reason: Fixed: replaced rfind with depth-tracking parser that handles strings and JSONC comments

title: Alien Artifact Decision Plane v1 (shadow-first, explainable, calibrated)
description:
## Background
sbh has strong foundational beads, but advanced decision-plane capabilities (shadow rollout, calibrated uncertainty, explainability contracts, and reproducible proof artifacts) were not represented as a cohesive executable plan.

## Goal
Ship a mathematically-rigorous decision plane that stays conservative by default, exposes full evidence for every action, and provides deterministic fallbacks under uncertainty and drift.

## Scope
This epic coordinates six implementation tracks plus dedicated verification tracks:
1. Shadow mode + progressive rollout controller
2. Evidence ledger schema + explain surfaces
3. Incremental Merkle scan index
4. VOI-based scan budget scheduler
5. Conformal/e-process guardrails for adaptive actions
6. Proof-grade benchmark/replay/fault-injection harness
7. Decision-plane unit/property tests
8. Decision-plane e2e scenarios with trace logging

## Constraints
- Safe under partial failure and low-confidence prediction
- Deterministic conservative fallback always available
- Comprehensive unit and e2e validation with detailed logs
- Reproducibility artifacts attached to performance and safety claims

## Adoption Wedge
observe-only (shadow) -> canary -> controlled ramp -> default-on with kill-switch.

## Budgeted Mode Requirement
Every adaptive component must define explicit resource caps (time/memory/work budget) and exact on-exhaustion behavior.

## Success Criteria
- No dependency cycles introduced
- Every track has explicit fallback semantics and recovery criteria
- Decision traces are explainable and queryable via CLI/API
- Unit and e2e tracks cover all decision-plane features
- Performance and safety claims have replayable evidence packs
acceptance_criteria: 1. All decision-plane tracks define deterministic fallback-safe behavior and recovery criteria. 2. Every adaptive decision is explainable via evidence ledger and trace surfaces. 3. Unit/property/e2e/stress/proof beads collectively cover all scoped decision-plane features. 4. Detailed structured logging and trace bundles are mandatory for failures and regressions. 5. No dependency cycles or unverifiable performance/safety claims remain.

title: Shadow-mode policy engine with progressive delivery gates
description:
## Deliverable
A decision-policy engine that runs in shadow mode first (observe-only), computes the same cleanup actions as enforce mode, and logs decision deltas without deleting anything.

## Background and Rationale
The project already has predictive and scoring beads, but production-safe rollout needs an explicit policy lifecycle. Shadow-first rollout is the highest-confidence way to prevent destructive false positives while still learning from real workload behavior.

## Adoption Wedge
observe -> canary -> enforce, with immediate fallback_safe at any time.

## Budgeted Mode
- Max evaluations per loop: 100 candidates
- Max hypothetical delete set per loop: 25
- Max canary deletes per hour: configurable cap
- On budget exhaustion: stop action planning and remain in observe mode

## Expected-Loss Model
States: useful, abandoned
Actions: keep, delete, review
Loss defaults: L(delete,useful)=100, L(keep,abandoned)=30, L(review,*)=5
Policy applies delete only when expected_loss_delete + guard_penalty is strictly below expected_loss_keep.

## Calibration and Fallback Trigger
Fallback_safe trigger when any condition holds:
- calibration_score below configured floor for N windows
- guardrail breach (delete budget, drift alarm, policy error)
- evidence serialization failure
Recovery requires M consecutive clean windows.

## Implementation Plan
1. Add policy modes and mode transitions to daemon loop.
2. Execute scoring/planning in observe mode with side effects disabled.
3. Emit decision_trace events for hypothetical and actual paths.
4. Enforce rollout guardrails and kill-switches.
5. Add automatic fallback transitions and recovery gates.

## Proof Artifacts
- Shadow-vs-enforce replay diff report
- Drift and guardrail activation report
- Deterministic trace corpus with fixed seeds

## Rollback
Single config/env switch forces fallback_safe and disables enforce actions.

## Baseline Comparator
Current threshold-plus-scoring reactive execution without progressive delivery gates.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Observe mode produces no filesystem mutation
- Guardrails cap canary impact deterministically
- Fallback_safe engages automatically on trigger conditions
- Recovery requires explicit clean-window criterion
- Unit tests for transition graph and guardrails
- E2E scenario validates shadow, canary, fallback with detailed logs

title: Evidence ledger schema + explain command (galaxy-brain levels)
description:
## Deliverable
A unified evidence-ledger schema recording why each cleanup decision was made, with explain surfaces for operators and agents.

## Background and Rationale
Advanced policy behavior is unusable without transparent decision provenance. This bead provides machine-readable and human-readable explainability that can be audited and replayed.

## Schema Contract
Each decision record must include:
- decision_id, trace_id, policy_mode, timestamp
- candidate features and factor contributions
- posterior estimates and expected-loss values
- selected action and guard outcomes
- calibration_score, fallback_active, fallback_reason
- comparator_action (for shadow/canary diffing)

## Explain Surfaces
- sbh explain --id <decision_id>
- sbh scan --explain <path>
- dashboard integration with level-0 to level-3 detail

## Galaxy-Brain Levels
Level 0: concise recommendation
Level 1: weighted factor table
Level 2: posterior, loss, calibration values
Level 3: full serialized trace payload for replay/debug

## Budgeted Mode
If evidence rendering is expensive, cap expanded explain payload generation per request; return compact mode with pointer to stored trace.

## Fallback Trigger
Any ledger write/serialization failure triggers safe policy fallback and emits explicit error events.

## Proof Artifacts
- Snapshot corpus for explain outputs
- Compatibility tests for schema evolution
- Replay verifier reading ledger-only traces

## Baseline Comparator
Current ad-hoc scan explanation and log records without unified decision provenance.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every decision has a persistent evidence record
- Explain output deterministic for identical inputs
- Stats engine can aggregate on evidence fields
- Unit tests for schema and rendering stability
- E2E test covers scan/clean/explain path with verbose logs

title: Incremental Merkle scan index with full-scan fallback
description:
## Deliverable
An incremental scan index keyed by path and metadata hashes so repeated daemon cycles avoid full recursive scans when nothing changed.

## Background and Rationale
Full recursive scanning is expected to dominate runtime cost as monitored trees grow. Incremental Merkle indexing provides an asymptotic and practical latency reduction while preserving deterministic behavior via reference fallback.

## Adoption Wedge
- Phase A: index build + shadow comparison only
- Phase B: canary incremental reads on subset of paths
- Phase C: full incremental default with periodic full-scan verification

## Budgeted Mode
- Max subtree hash updates per loop
- Max checkpoint write bytes per interval
- On budget exhaustion: partial updates deferred and full scan used for affected roots

## Isomorphism Proof Plan
For the same filesystem snapshot, incremental and full scan must yield identical candidate set/order.
- Candidate-set equality checks
- Stable ordering checks
- Mismatch auto-fallback and trace emission

## Safety and Fallback
Any index checksum mismatch, corruption, or replay inconsistency forces immediate full-scan mode and marks index unhealthy until rebuilt.

## Proof Artifacts
- Replay fixtures for churn/no-churn workloads
- p50/p95/p99 scan latency report before/after
- mismatch incident traces with root-cause fields

## Baseline Comparator
Current full recursive walk on each scan cycle.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Incremental and full paths are behaviorally isomorphic
- Automatic fallback works for all detected integrity failures
- Demonstrable low-churn speedup without safety regression
- Unit tests for Merkle updates/checkpoint recovery
- E2E tests for healthy/degraded/index-rebuild scenarios
close_reason: Implemented incremental Merkle scan index with full-scan fallback: 20 unit tests, SHA-256 metadata hashing, checkpoint persistence with integrity verification, budget-aware diff, automatic corruption detection

title: Value-of-Information scheduler for scan budget allocation
description:
## Deliverable
A Value-of-Information scheduler that allocates scan budget to paths with highest expected reclaimed-bytes-per-IO while maintaining safety and exploration guarantees.

## Background and Rationale
Fixed-frequency scanning wastes cycles under pressure and large tree counts. VOI scheduling prioritizes high-yield scan targets while preserving deterministic safety behavior.

## Utility Model
utility(path) = expected_reclaim_bytes - io_cost_penalty - false_positive_risk_penalty
with explicit uncertainty discount and exploration bonus for under-sampled paths.

## Budgeted Mode
- Fixed scan budget per interval
- Per-path minimum exploration quota
- On budget exhaustion: deterministic round-robin fallback for remaining paths

## Expected-Loss Framing
States: high-yield, low-yield, risky
Actions: scan_now, defer, fallback_scan
Loss discourages starvation and risky over-scanning.

## Calibration and Fallback
If forecast-error exceeds threshold across N windows, disable VOI prioritization and revert to baseline tiered scheduler until recalibrated.

## Proof Artifacts
- Forecast vs realized utility report
- reclaim-per-IO comparison against fixed-tier baseline
- starvation guard audit for low-activity paths

## Baseline Comparator
Current tiered fixed-interval scan scheduling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Higher reclaim-per-IO than baseline in benchmark scenarios
- No path starvation under exploration constraints
- Deterministic fallback path tested and documented
- Unit tests for ranking, quotas, and forecast error handling
- E2E mixed-workload scenario with full scheduler telemetry
close_reason: Implemented VOI scheduler: utility model (reclaim/IO/FP-risk/exploration), budgeted mode with exploration quotas, calibration with automatic round-robin fallback, 14 unit tests

title: Conformal and e-process guardrails for adaptive controller actions
description:
## Deliverable
A statistical guard layer for predictive and PID-driven adaptive decisions using conformal-style calibration checks plus anytime-valid e-process alarms.

## Background and Rationale
Adaptive controllers can become overconfident under distribution shift. This bead provides explicit finite-sample guardrails so high-impact actions are blocked when confidence is not justified.

## Guard Contract
- Compute rolling calibration diagnostics for forecast quality
- Maintain e-process statistic for drift/overconfidence detection
- Gate aggressive cleanup and emergency escalation on guard pass
- Trigger fallback-safe policy on guard fail

## Budgeted Mode
- Bounded historical window for guard computation
- Bounded per-loop statistic updates
- On compute budget exhaustion: mark guard unknown and force conservative fallback

## Expected-Loss and Safety
Expected-loss controller output is considered valid only when guard status is PASS.
States: calibrated, uncalibrated
Actions: adaptive_allowed, adaptive_blocked
Loss strongly penalizes adaptive actions under uncalibrated state.

## Recovery Policy
Require M consecutive clean windows plus low e-process evidence before re-enabling adaptive mode.

## Proof Artifacts
- Synthetic drift injections with trigger timestamps
- False-alarm and miss-rate tradeoff report
- Replay traces showing guard-driven action suppression

## Baseline Comparator
Current controller behavior without formal calibration gate.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Guard catches induced drift and overconfidence cases
- High-impact actions blocked when guard != PASS
- Recovery flow requires explicit clean-window criterion
- Unit tests for sequential guard math and thresholds
- E2E drift scenario validates fallback activation with detailed logs

title: Decision-plane proof harness: replay, fault-injection, and reproducibility pack
description:
## Deliverable
A proof harness validating decision-plane behavior with deterministic replay, fault-injection, and reproducibility artifacts suitable for CI and local triage.

## Background and Rationale
Advanced policy work must ship with evidence, not anecdotes. This harness is the contract that turns optimization/safety claims into reproducible proof artifacts.

## Scope
1. Deterministic replay engine for decision traces and mode transitions
2. Fault-injection suite (IO errors, stale stats, lock contention, delayed telemetry, serializer failures)
3. Reproducibility pack emission: env.json, manifest.json, repro.lock, trace bundle
4. Statistical benchmark report: p50/p95/p99 with baseline comparator and variance context

## Budgeted Mode
Test harness supports fast and full modes:
- Fast mode: reduced scenario set for pre-commit
- Full mode: complete matrix for CI/nightly

## Fallback Validation
Every fault scenario must assert expected fallback behavior and explicit reason codes.

## Proof Artifacts
- Replay report with pass/fail by invariant
- Fault matrix with expected vs observed fallback action
- Benchmark delta report against baseline

## Baseline Comparator
Current ad-hoc tests without formal replay/fault/provenance packaging.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Replay outcomes deterministic across repeated runs
- Fault matrix validates all fallback invariants
- Repro packs generated and consumable by tooling
- Unit tests validate parser/invariant checks
- E2E workflow integrates into CI with verbose logging outputs
acceptance_criteria: 1. Proof harness reproduces decision outcomes from evidence traces across runs. 2. Fault-injection suite validates conservative fallback on serialization/index/guard failures. 3. Benchmark and replay artifacts include manifest + seed + environment provenance. 4. Harness emits machine-readable verdicts and differential reports for regression detection. 5. Logging captures full proof chain from input trace through policy decision assertions.

title: Decision-plane e2e scenario pack with verbose trace logging
description:

title: Decision-plane unit-test matrix with invariant/property checks

title: [macOS E4] Process inventory + I/O attribution (libproc + Mach)
description:
# Epic 4: Process inventory & I/O attribution (macOS)

## Why sbh needs this

`sbh blame` is the diagnostic command: "Disk grew by 47 GB in the last 6 hours; here are the processes responsible, ranked." On Linux it reads `/proc/<pid>/io`, `/proc/<pid>/cmdline`, `/proc/<pid>/status`. On Mac there is no `/proc`. We use libproc + Mach FFI.

## Mac process inventory APIs

1. **`proc_listpids(PROC_ALL_PIDS, 0, ...)`** — returns array of all PIDs. Cheap.
2. **`proc_pidinfo(pid, PROC_PIDTASKINFO, ...)`** → `proc_taskinfo` struct: CPU, virtual size, resident size, faults.
3. **`proc_pidinfo(pid, PROC_PIDTHREADINFO, ...)`** → per-thread CPU.
4. **`proc_pidinfo(pid, PROC_PIDPATHINFO, ...)`** → executable path.
5. **`proc_pidpath(pid, ...)`** → path of binary.
6. **`proc_pid_rusage(pid, RUSAGE_INFO_V4, &out)`** → `rusage_info_v4` struct: ri_diskio_bytesread, ri_diskio_byteswritten — *this is the gold*. Per-process disk I/O.
7. **`proc_pidinfo(pid, PROC_PIDLISTFDS, ...)` then `proc_pidfdinfo(pid, fd, PROC_PIDFDVNODEPATHINFO, ...)`** → list open file descriptors and their paths. Equivalent to `lsof -p <pid>`.

## Required sbh behavior

- `sbh blame --top N` lists top N processes by recent disk write activity, with: PID, parent PID, command, executable path, recent bytes_written delta, recent bytes_read delta, current open file paths matching cleanup-candidate dirs.
- `sbh scan` uses open-fd info to know which candidate dirs are *currently* in use (don't reclaim those — like the `ft-*-target` dirs in the incident).
- The daemon samples `ri_diskio_byteswritten` periodically (15s default) per PID, computes delta, and accumulates into a per-process disk-write history (sliding 1h window).
- `sbh blame --tree` shows process tree (parent → children) so "claude → cargo → rustc" attribution is visible.

## Tasks (see child beads)

1. libproc FFI bindings (link against libproc, declare extern "C" signatures for `proc_listpids`, `proc_pidinfo`, `proc_pidpath`, `proc_pid_rusage`, `proc_pidfdinfo`).
2. `ProcessInfo` struct cross-platform: `pid`, `ppid`, `name`, `command_line`, `executable`, `start_time`, `vsize`, `rss`, `cpu_user`, `cpu_system`.
3. `ProcessIo` struct cross-platform: `bytes_read_total`, `bytes_written_total`, `bytes_read_recent_15m`, `bytes_written_recent_15m`.
4. Open-FD enumeration: `proc_pidfdinfo` → `OpenFile { path, fd_type, mode }`. Build path-prefix index: "is anything open under /private/tmp/ft-*?".
5. Sliding window history of per-PID I/O for last 1 hour. Store in-memory in daemon, periodically snapshot to `~/Library/Application Support/sbh/io_history.bin` (or platform equivalent).
6. `sbh blame` command implementation, with `--json`, `--tree`, `--top N`, `--since DURATION` flags.
7. Process command-line collection on macOS (different from Linux: use `KERN_PROCARGS2` sysctl).
8. Test: spawn a known process that writes 100 MB to `/private/tmp/sbh_blame_test/`, run `sbh blame`, verify it appears in top 5.

## Permissions consideration

macOS restricts `proc_pidinfo` for processes owned by other users when SIP is active. sbh as user-scope sees only own-user processes; sbh as root via LaunchDaemon sees all. Document this clearly in `sbh status` output ("Visibility: own-user processes only" vs "all processes"). This affects what `blame` can attribute.

## Failure modes

- Calling `proc_listpids` allocating a too-small buffer → silent truncation. Always two-call: query size first, allocate, then fetch.
- Sampling `ri_diskio_byteswritten` too frequently → measurable CPU. Default 15s tick is fine for daemon polling.
- Treating zombie PIDs (pid that died between listpids and pidinfo) as errors → spurious noise. Filter ESRCH gracefully.
- Running as user-scope launchd Agent and complaining about other users' processes not visible — that's expected; emit info-level log, not warning.

title: [E4.1] libproc FFI bindings (proc_listpids, proc_pidinfo, proc_pidpath, proc_pid_rusage, proc_pidfdinfo)
description: Link against libproc (system library on macOS). FFI for: proc_listpids(typ, typeinfo, buffer, buffersize) -> i32; proc_pidinfo(pid, flavor, arg, buffer, buffersize) -> i32; proc_pidpath(pid, buffer, buffersize) -> i32; proc_pid_rusage(pid, flavor, buffer) -> i32; proc_pidfdinfo(pid, fd, flavor, buffer, buffersize) -> i32. Define corresponding structs: proc_taskinfo, proc_taskallinfo, rusage_info_v4, proc_fdinfo, proc_fileinfo, vnode_fdinfowithpath. Use libc crate where possible; manually declare for libproc-specific types. Two-call pattern for arrays: first call with NULL buffer to get size, second with sized buffer. Helper: proc_listpids_safe() handles this.

title: [E4.2] ProcessInfo struct + MacOsPal::process_list()
description: Cross-platform ProcessInfo: { pid, ppid, name, command_line, executable, start_time, vsize_bytes, rss_bytes, cpu_user_us, cpu_system_us, uid }. MacOsPal::process_list() iterates proc_listpids → proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDTASKALLINFO) → ProcessInfo. Filter zombies (PID 0) and sbh's own PID by default. Performance: 1000 PIDs × proc_pidinfo approximately 50-100 ms on M-series. Acceptable for daemon's 15s tick.

title: [E4.3] ProcessIo struct + MacOsPal::process_io() via rusage_info_v4
description: ProcessIo: { bytes_read_total, bytes_written_total }. Mac uses rusage_info_v4.ri_diskio_bytesread and ri_diskio_byteswritten. CRITICAL caveat: rusage I/O counts are per-process lifetime totals, NOT deltas. The sliding-window bookkeeping (E4.5) computes deltas. Permission caveat: rusage on other-user processes returns EPERM unless sbh is running as root. Document; user-scope LaunchAgent has limited visibility.

title: [E4.4] Process command-line collection via KERN_PROCARGS2 sysctl
description: Mac doesn't store cmdline at /proc/<pid>/cmdline. Use sysctl with mib [CTL_KERN, KERN_PROCARGS2, pid]. Returned format: argc (i32) + argv strings (NUL-separated) + env strings + path. Parser: read argc, walk strings. Edge: very long argvs truncated by kernel; check buffer fill. Test: spawn known process with known argv, read back, assert match.

title: [E4.5] Sliding-window per-PID I/O history (1h window, 15s buckets)
description: Daemon maintains in-memory ring buffer per PID: last 240 samples × 15s = 1h window. Each sample: timestamp + bytes_read_total + bytes_written_total. Compute deltas: recent_15m_bytes_written = sum of (sample[i] - sample[i-1]) for last 60 samples. Persistence: snapshot to ~/Library/Application Support/sbh/io_history.bin every 5 min so restart doesn't lose history. Use bincode or postcard for compact format. Memory budget: 1000 PIDs × 240 samples × 24 bytes = approximately 5.7 MB. Acceptable.

title: [E4.6] sbh blame command implementation
description: New CLI: sbh blame [--top N] [--since DURATION] [--tree] [--json]. Output ranks PIDs by recent_bytes_written. Each row: PID, parent PID, command, exe path, recent_writes, recent_reads, open file paths matching cleanup-candidate dirs. --tree: render parent→child tree using ppid links. --since: configurable window (1m, 5m, 1h). --json: machine-readable for scripts. Acceptance: spawn synthetic process writing 100 MB to /tmp/sbh_blame_test_XXXX; sbh blame --top 5 includes it.

title: [E4.7] Open file enumeration via proc_pidfdinfo (used by E10)
description: MacOsPal::open_files_under(path) -> Vec<OpenFile>. Algo: for each PID, proc_pidlistfds → for each fd of type PROC_FDTYPE_VNODE → proc_pidfdinfo(PROC_PIDFDVNODEPATHINFO) → vnode_fdinfowithpath.pvip.vip_path. Filter to those starting with given path. Performance: O(num_pids × avg_fd_per_pid). Cache result with 30s TTL; refresh on demand. Used by E10 active-build-awareness.

title: [E4.8] Test: synthetic 100 MB writer surfaces in sbh blame top 5
description: Integration test using mkfile (Mac native) for safety: 'mkfile -n 100m /private/tmp/sbh_blame_test_$$' creates a sparse 100MB file in 1 syscall, then read from it to force disk I/O. Query 'sbh blame --since=1m --json --top 10'. Assert child PID present with bytes_written greater than or equal to expected. Cleanup: rm -f /private/tmp/sbh_blame_test_* in tearDown. Why mkfile vs other write tools: native Mac, no exit-code surprises, allowed by destructive-command-guard rules out of the box.

title: Remove unwrap panic risk in dashboard refresh anchor
description: Replace checked_sub(...).unwrap() in cli/dashboard run loop with safe fallback and add regression test for oversized refresh durations.

title: sbh scan command: manual artifact scan with scoring report
description:
## Deliverable
Command to manually trigger a build artifact scan, showing all candidates with their scores and classification, without deleting anything.

## Technical Approach
### Usage
```bash
sbh scan                     # Scan all configured paths
sbh scan /data/projects      # Scan specific path
sbh scan --min-score 0.5     # Only show candidates above threshold
sbh scan --top 20            # Show top 20 candidates
sbh scan --json              # Machine-readable output
sbh scan --explain <path>    # Detailed scoring breakdown for specific path
```

### Output
```
Build Artifact Scan Results
  Scanned: 15,234 directories in 3.2 seconds
  Candidates found: 47 (above threshold 0.3)

  ┌─────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────┬──────────┬────────┬────────┐
  │  #  │ Path                                     │ Size     │ Age      │ Score  │ Type   │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼──────────┼────────┼────────┤
  │  1  │ /tmp/cargo-target-quietwillow-mvcc       │   4.2 GB │ 6h 15m   │  0.94  │ Rust   │
  │  2  │ /data/projects/pi/.target_opus_main      │   8.1 GB │ 4h 02m   │  0.91  │ Rust   │
  │  3  │ /data/tmp/cargo-target                   │  12.4 GB │ 3h 45m   │  0.89  │ Rust   │
  │  4  │ /tmp/target-ubuntu-am                    │   2.8 GB │ 8h 30m   │  0.87  │ Rust   │
  │  ...│                                          │          │          │        │        │
  └─────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────┴──────────┴────────┴────────┘

  Total reclaimable: 142.7 GB
  Use 'sbh clean' to delete these candidates.
```

### Explain Mode
For debugging scoring decisions:
```
sbh scan --explain /data/projects/pi/.target_opus_main

Scoring Breakdown for: /data/projects/pi/.target_opus_main
  Type: Directory (8.1 GB, 14,523 files)
  Modified: 4 hours 2 minutes ago
  Classification: RustTarget (confidence: 0.95)

  Factor        │ Weight │ Raw Value │ Weighted
  ──────────────┼────────┼───────────┼─────────
  Location      │  0.25  │    0.80   │   0.200
  Name/Pattern  │  0.25  │    0.95   │   0.238
  Age           │  0.20  │    0.90   │   0.180
  Size          │  0.15  │    0.95   │   0.143
  Structure     │  0.15  │    0.90   │   0.135
  ──────────────┼────────┼──────────┼─────────
  Base Score    │        │           │   0.895
  Pressure ×    │        │     1.0   │
  Final Score   │        │           │   0.895

  Structural markers found: incremental/, deps/, .fingerprint/
  No vetoes triggered.
  Verdict: HIGH confidence deletion candidate
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Scans configured paths and reports all candidates
- Scoring matches the scoring engine output exactly
- --explain provides full factor breakdown
- --json output includes all scoring data
- Performance: scans /data/projects in < 10 seconds
- Results sorted by score descending
- No modifications to filesystem (read-only scan)
- Unit tests for output formatting
acceptance_criteria: 1. Unit tests cover filter parsing, ranking limits, and deterministic ordering output. 2. Integration tests validate scanner + scorer + protection interactions and veto visibility. 3. E2E scripts cover dry-run style scans, min-score, top-N, explain output, and JSON contracts. 4. Output schemas remain stable and machine-parseable under empty and high-volume scan sets. 5. Detailed scan traces include candidate factors, veto reasons, and summary counters.

title: macOS Support: full cross-platform parity with Linux (master epic)
description:
# Add full macOS support to sbh (single binary, OS-adaptive)

## Overarching goal

Make `sbh` a true cross-platform disk-pressure guardian. Today (2026-05-03) `sbh` ships a macOS binary but every operational subcommand returns `[SBH-1101] unsupported platform: only Linux is currently implemented`. The Mac was *completely* unprotected while the entire Linux fleet had it. We learned this the hard way: jemanuel's local Mac filled to **147 MB free out of 1.95 TB** — a 264 GB `frankenterm-trash` dir plus ~330 GB of actively-compiling `ft-*-target` build dirs in `/private/tmp` were the culprits. Manual rescue freed ~340 GB. None of that should have been needed.

## Non-negotiable architectural constraint

**Single binary, single project, single source tree.** We do NOT fork sbh or create a separate "sbh-mac". The same `sbh` binary detects the OS at runtime and dispatches to the correct platform implementation behind the existing `src/platform/pal.rs` Platform Abstraction Layer (PAL). When new OS support is added later (FreeBSD, illumos, Windows), it slots into the same PAL pattern. Build-time `#[cfg(target_os = "...")]` gates may still exist for platform-specific syscalls/FFI, but the public surface (CLI, daemon behavior, config schema, output JSON) is OS-agnostic.

## Why this matters

1. **Operator surprise is failure.** A tool that silently does nothing on a major platform creates the *worst* class of incident: the operator believes they're protected, then disk fills with no warning. We just lived this.
2. **The fleet is heterogeneous.** Most fleet hosts are Linux (Threadripper, EPYC, KVM VPS), but the operator's primary workstation is macOS arm64. The workstation is where they review work, run agents locally, and where build artifacts pile up fastest.
3. **Mac has *worse* disk pressure dynamics in some ways.** APFS purgeable storage masks "free" space. Local Time Machine snapshots quietly hold deleted files. Spotlight indexing balloons disk use. The Electron-app cache problem (Cursor, Claude Desktop, VS Code) is acute. Without sbh, none of these are managed.
4. **Same operator, same expectations.** The operator runs the same agent workloads on Mac and Linux. They expect the same `sbh` behavior, same CLI, same JSON output, same dashboard, same blame attribution. Diverging would be a coordination disaster.

## Scope (what's in)

- All operational sbh subcommands work on macOS: `daemon`, `install`, `uninstall`, `status`, `stats`, `scan`, `clean`, `ballast`, `config`, `emergency`, `protect`, `unprotect`, `tune`, `check`, `blame`, `dashboard`, `log`, `setup`.
- launchd integration replacing systemd where Mac requires it.
- APFS-aware capacity, snapshot, and purgeable handling.
- Mac-specific cleanup heuristics (Xcode DerivedData, ~/Library/Caches/*, Electron caches, iOS Simulator, /private/tmp build dirs, user-named "trash" dirs).
- Mac-specific sacred path protection (Photos library, Mail, Messages, iCloud Drive, etc.).
- Memory pressure detection via Mach + sysctl.
- Notarized, signed binaries; Homebrew tap formula.
- Full test coverage on macOS in CI.

## Scope (what's out for v1)

- Windows support (separate epic later).
- DMG/PKG GUI installer (we'll ship a curl|bash installer + brew tap; native installer can come later).
- iCloud-aware reclamation of "Files in iCloud" via the Optimize Storage API (needs entitlements and is its own product).
- Universal binary fat file (we'll ship arm64 and x86_64 separately to keep size down — arm64 is the only target for new Macs).

## Success criteria (acceptance)

1. On a fresh macOS arm64 machine: `curl -fsSL <installer> | bash` results in a running launchd job within 30 seconds and `sbh status` returns valid JSON within 100 ms.
2. `sbh status` on macOS reports the same JSON shape as on Linux (with platform-specific keys clearly nested under `"platform"`).
3. `sbh scan` on macOS finds and ranks reclaim candidates. Running the scan on jemanuel's actual Mac (the one we just rescued) would have found at least the 264 GB frankenterm-trash dir and surfaced it with appropriate confidence.
4. `sbh clean --apply` (with confirmation) successfully reclaims the candidates, respecting active build file handles (does NOT delete `ft-*-target` dirs while cargo is writing them).
5. `sbh blame` on macOS attributes recent disk growth to specific PIDs and parent processes (e.g., "claude pid=12345 wrote 47 GB to /private/tmp/ft-cod7-target in last 6 hours").
6. CI green on `macos-latest` and `macos-13` runners with all unit + integration + snapshot tests.
7. Documentation: a Mac user landing on the README can install, configure, and verify in under 10 minutes.

## Architectural approach (high-level)

- **PAL (Platform Abstraction Layer) is the seam.** Every place that today does `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` either becomes a PAL method call (preferred) or stays gated but with an explicit Mac branch alongside.
- **Trait-based PAL with two impls:** `LinuxPal` and `MacOsPal`, both implementing the same `Pal` trait. Compile-time selection via `cfg`. A `MockPal` for tests.
- **No runtime polymorphism overhead.** The platform is fixed at compile time per target; we use generics or compile-time selection, not `dyn`.
- **Daemon process model is the same.** Single long-running daemon. On Linux it's launched by systemd; on Mac by launchd. The daemon code itself is identical.
- **Filesystem operations isolated to `src/platform/fs/`.** Linux uses statvfs + /proc/self/mountinfo. Mac uses statfs + getmntinfo. Both produce the same `MountInfo` struct.
- **Memory pressure isolated to `src/platform/mem/`.** Linux reads /proc/pressure/memory. Mac uses sysctl + Mach calls. Both produce the same `MemoryPressure` struct.
- **Service install isolated to `src/daemon/service/`.** Today `service.rs` is implicitly Linux/systemd. Split into `service/systemd.rs` + `service/launchd.rs` + a `service/mod.rs` dispatcher.

## Why not just shell out to `df`, `vm_stat`, `launchctl`?

We will, *occasionally*, when the FFI cost isn't justified — but the daemon's hot path must not fork subprocesses. Forking is slow (5-50 ms on macOS), pollutes Energy Impact stats (visible in Activity Monitor), and creates a scheduling tax under load. For polled metrics, we use direct syscalls (`statfs`, `sysctl`, `host_statistics64`, `proc_listpids`, `proc_pidinfo`). For one-shot install/diagnostic flows we can shell out to `launchctl`, `tmutil`, `mdfind` because operator latency tolerance is much higher there.

## What this epic does NOT do (deferred)

- Doesn't add new sbh features for any platform — this is *only* the Mac port + the refactor it forces.
- Doesn't change the daemon's policy engine — same risk thresholds, same scoring, same blame attribution algorithm.
- Doesn't change the JSON output schema (we'll add fields, not remove).

## Project bookkeeping

- One epic per major work area (16 sub-epics below).
- Tasks within each epic are sized 1-8 hours each.
- Dependencies modelled with `br dep add` — most epics block on E1 (PAL foundation).
- Cross-cutting deps (e.g., E14 testing depends on most code epics) modelled explicitly.
- Each task bead carries enough body that a future contributor (human or agent) can pick it up cold.

## Background incident report (the spark)

Date: 2026-05-03. Operator's primary Mac (Apple Silicon, 1.95 TB internal). State at start of investigation:

```
df -h /System/Volumes/Data
/dev/disk3s5    1.95Ti   1.95Ti   147Mi    100%
```

Top consumer: `/private/tmp/frankenterm-trash-20260503-092725` (264 GB), staged by operator earlier that day. Contained `.beads/`, `beads.db`, `beads.db-wal` — beads database files mixed with cargo target dirs (`.cargo-rch-goldenplateau-*`). Newest file 10h old.

Plus `/private/tmp/ft-{cod1..9,cc1..3}-target` — ~330 GB total, all actively being written by parallel claude/codex agents running cargo builds (newest files 0-4h old).

Plus `~/release-work/mcp_agent_mail_rust_buildroot` (39 GB, 11 days old), various Rust target dirs, `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/vm_bundles/claudevm.bundle` (9.8 GB).

Manual cleanup freed ~340 GB. **The entire time**, sbh was installed (v0.4.5) but every command returned `SBH-1101 unsupported platform`. There was no daemon, no monitoring, no warning, no defense. The Mac filled silently from "lots of room" to "147 MB free" over the course of one agent-heavy workday.

This epic ensures that incident class is impossible going forward.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
ENHANCEMENT (idea-wizard): Centralize filesystem type detection and strategy selection. Currently ad-hoc across beads (bd-25g uses fs_type for fallocate, bd-1w9 has cross-device checks). Add to Platform trait:
  fn fs_strategy(&self, path: &Path) -> FsStrategy
Where FsStrategy encodes:
  - ballast_method: Fallocate | RandomData | Skip (for tmpfs/NFS)
  - deletion_method: Standard | CowAware (btrfs/zfs may not free space immediately)
  - scan_strategy: Full | ReadOnly | Skip (NFS mounts: read-only scanning, skip deletion, warn user)
  - size_estimation: StatBased | DuBased (CoW filesystems lie about size via stat)
This prevents each bead from independently querying fs_type.
